The TUI header shows `budget $X.XX/$10.00` where the $10.00 ceiling is hardcoded at `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:376` (`cost_budget: (0.0, 10.0)`) and serves no functional purpose — it's purely cosmetic. Nothing enforces the cap: sessions keep running past it, no models freeze, no warnings fire beyond a color shift. Source comment at `main.rs:262` self-describes as 'still partially mocked v1'.

Real enforcement is parked under mu-fvy0 (multi-axis budget ledger, umbrella). That's a sizeable architectural piece. The mid-term fix is to make the display honest — don't show a ratio against a fictional cap.

## Short-term fix (this bead)

Drop the `/$10.00` denominator. Replace the header span with:

```
spent $X.XX
```

or similar — show accumulated cost, no implied ceiling. The aggregation logic from mu-fqvc stays; only the rendering changes. Color thresholds either go away or attach to absolute dollar values (e.g., yellow >$5, red >$10) rather than a ratio.

## Acceptance

- TUI header no longer shows `/$N.NN` ceiling
- No hardcoded $10.00 anywhere in mu-tui main.rs
- Sessions render the same; only the header span differs
- cargo fmt + clippy + test clean

## Out of scope (deferred)

The longer-term shape tcovert sketched 2026-05-18:

- **Session tree with rollups**: per-session totals visible in the session pane; aggregate rollups by provider and per-model
- **Per-provider caps, not overall cap**: `anthropic_api` and `openrouter_api` (pay-per-token) want soft/hard ceilings; `anthropic_oauth` and `openai_oauth` (subscription) don't
- **Optional overall ceiling** as a fallback, but per-provider is the more natural axis

That's mu-fvy0's surface area + a TUI consumer. File as a separate bead linked under mu-fvy0 once mu-fvy0's ledger primitives exist.

title: Future: secret-aware prompt/content scanning and redaction
description:
Add pre-send/pre-log secret scanning and optional content decoupling. Baseline should be default-on, configurable mechanical local scanners (rg/regex/entropy/prefix) for API keys, PEM blocks, JWTs, provider tokens, .env-style assignments, high-entropy strings, etc.

Events may store content_sha256/content_ref/redaction_status/redacted_preview rather than full text when appropriate; plaintext can live in a local/encrypted/optional content store.

Optional later layer: fast local-only LLM/classifier for semantic sensitivity (HIPAA, classified markings, corporate IP), advisory/risk-scoring only — not the sole guard. Remote models must never be used to detect secrets before sending, because that is already exfiltration.

Related memories: bc1d46ec, ac66f02f.

title: mu-phl v0 Phase C.1 — SubprocessRecallProvider (shells out to agent memory context)
description:
Step 3 of mu-phl v0. Implements the subprocess-based recall provider.

## What

`crates/mu-core/src/context/recall/subprocess.rs` — `SubprocessRecallProvider`. Shells out to `~/.local/bin/agent memory context --cwd <cwd>` with `std::process::Command`, 500ms timeout, captures stdout, wraps the full markdown as a single `RecalledItem` with `source: Memory`, content built via `SpanText::from(stdout_str.as_str())`.

Tolerates absent binary (empty vec + `tracing::warn!` once per session, no panic).

## Why

Reuses the existing `agent` CLI (~10ms latency, well-tested ranking) rather than reimplementing scoring + sqlite access. v1's `EventLogRecallProvider` replaces this; the trait shape is identical.

## Acceptance

- Unit tests: stub binary path; assert stdout is wrapped correctly; missing-binary test returns empty vec without panic.
- 500ms timeout enforced (test with a hanging stub binary).
- One commit with `## Files` block and `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase B (step 2) — uses the `RecallProvider` trait and `RecalledItem` / `RecallSource::Memory` from the recall module.
close_reason: Landed (Phase C.1) on feat/mu-phl-v0. SubprocessRecallProvider shells out to ~/.local/bin/agent memory context, wraps stdout as one RecalledItem{source=Memory}. AtomicBool guards single-warn-per-session for missing binary. blake3 short-hash for stable_id. 5 unit tests cover missing-binary/once-only-warn/non-zero-exit/happy-path/empty-stdout. cargo clippy -D warnings clean; 762 tests pass.

title: mu: modular pane architecture + protocol-as-foundation for multi-renderer views (TUI / web / native GUI)
description:
## Vision

mu's protocol layer (JSON-RPC over stdio today; unix-socket / tcp in the future) is the foundation. The TUI is **one renderer** that consumes it. Anything that speaks the protocol can be a view:

- Multiple TUI compositions (single-pane vs. command-center; per-pane standalone)
- Web frontend
- Native GUI (Tauri / SwiftUI / etc.)
- Mobile
- Remote / cross-device

Operator stays on TUI personally, but the architecture should make alternative renderers a first-class extension point rather than a rewrite.

## Modular TUI panes

Each pane in the current TUI (Live Sessions list, Session Detail transcript, Firehose events strip, F8 events explorer, F5 usage dashboard, F2 session tree) is conceptually independent. Today they live as render functions inside one mu-tui process. Make them **runnable standalone** so the operator can compose their own layout per use case:

- `mu-tui --pane=transcript --session=session-3` — single-session frameless view (the current Inline mode but as a dedicated invocation)
- `mu-tui --pane=command-center` — the framed multi-pane layout (current F1)
- `mu-tui --pane=events --filter=...` — just the firehose / F8 events explorer
- `mu-tui --pane=tree` — F2 session tree only
- `mu-tui` (no args) — current default: command-center with mode switching

### Composability

Operator's actual workflow (2026-05-19): claude-personal in half a tab, claude-work in another tab, occasionally pi_ts or mu adjacent. With modular panes:

```
zellij layout:
┌─ tab 1 ─────────────────────────────────┐
│ pane: claude-personal │ pane: mu-tui    │
│                       │  --pane=transcript │
│                       │  --session=X    │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

Each pane is a single-purpose tool; mux composes them. Replaces the current single-binary-with-modes shape.

### When farms of workers come into play

Per-session panes don't scale to 20+ active sessions. The command-center composition is the right view for fleet management — overview metrics, batch operations, dashboards. **Single-session panes for focused work, command-center for fleet oversight.** Same binary, different invocation flag, different layout.

## Multi-renderer via protocol

The protocol is already JSON-RPC over stdio (mu-001, mu-002). Adding unix-socket / tcp transports is straightforward (mu-037 partially covers cross-daemon comms). Once that's there:

- Web frontend (browser) connects via WebSocket → JSON-RPC bridge
- Native GUI (Tauri-rust, SwiftUI, etc.) connects via unix socket on same host
- Remote view (different device) connects over TCP/TLS
- All consume the same event-log + session.list / session.events + capability gates

**The TUI stays primary**, but alternative views become contributable rather than requiring a full reimplementation.

## Why this is filed as a single umbrella

Each piece (modular panes; remote protocol transport; alternate renderers) is its own work, but the **architectural shape** is one decision: "the views are clients of the protocol; not all clients are TUIs." Once that's accepted, downstream work decomposes naturally:

- **Sub-bead candidates**: standalone transcript pane; standalone events pane; standalone tree pane; protocol-over-unix-socket transport; protocol-over-tcp/tls transport; web frontend skeleton; etc.
- **Companion beads**: mu-6nuq (per-session pane spawn — partial mux delegation; this bead generalizes the principle to all panes), mu-dere (own-buffer transcript with editor extract — a TUI design choice that becomes one of multiple renderer choices), mu-037 (cross-daemon mailbox / peer comms — the transport layer this bead needs), mu-71q (MCP adapter — same pattern in MCP-shaped form).

## Out of scope

- Web frontend implementation (just a north-star claim for now)
- Cross-device sync details
- Plugin system / WASM / dynamic loading (each "pane" is just a different invocation of the same binary — no plugin machinery needed for v1)
- Backwards-compat for current mu-tui mode-switching (probably becomes one composition among many)

## Acceptance (rough; refine in sub-beads)

For v1:
- `mu-tui --pane <name>` flag exists
- At least 3 standalone panes work (`transcript`, `command-center`, `events`)
- Each pane works in a zellij/tmux pane the same way as the current TUI does
- Per-pane state isolation (no global ViewMode; each pane is its own mini-app)

For the protocol-renderer half:
- Documented contract: "a mu view is anything that opens the protocol and renders"
- Unix-socket transport added (mu-037 phase 2 covers part of this)
- Spec or example showing a non-TUI consumer (smoke test even a Python script counts)

## Provenance

Operator brainstorm 2026-05-19 ~05:40 UTC (pre-sleep): the realization that mu's protocol is the foundation, and the TUI is one renderer among potential many. mu-o1y7's inline-mode design discussion surfaced the constraint (mux scrollback is linear-per-pane → can't have multi-buffer in one pane → either multi-pane or own-buffer). Generalizing: **the TUI is one view; the protocol is the contract; views are interchangeable**. Same logic that makes mu-tui swappable with a Python script makes it swappable with a web app or native GUI.

title: mu-tui: F3 session list summary column (name/last-prompt/status)
description:
## Problem

F3→F3 session list shows sessions but not enough about each to know which
is which at a glance. Missing: friendly name, last prompt, current status,
topic summary.

## Proposal

Add a summary column (or columns) to the F3→F3 list:

- Friendly name (from `/rename` per the per-session-commands bead) when set
- Last user prompt (truncated)
- Current status (idle / streaming / awaiting-tool / etc.)
- Optionally: token/cost summary per session (compact form)

## Acceptance

- F3→F3 list shows each session with name/topic/last-prompt + status
- Truncation short enough to fit, long enough to identify
- Column widths adapt to terminal size

## Cross-refs

- Per-session control surface bead (where `/rename` is defined)
- F8 truncation + compact cost display bead (same aesthetic)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: Track goal-protocol dispatch calibration metadata in mu (cost / wall-clock / LOC / test-delta)
description: Goal-protocol /goal dispatches produce per-bead calibration data (cost, wall-clock, num_turns, LOC delta, tests added/passed, codex review verdict) that today lives only in the per-experiment markdown doc at ~/.claude-personal/experiments/goal-<date>-<bead>.md. Useful for predicting next-bead cost/wall-clock but DECAYS with each new session (the markdown is not queryable across sessions). Hope captured 2026-05-15 by tcovert during mu-yox wrap: 'I hope we can keep these in mu somehow' — meaning mu's substrate could ingest these data points so future operators can run 'how much do beads of shape X cost?' as a query. Potential shapes: (a) a mu event variant emitted on /goal completion carrying the envelope's verification_results + cost summary; (b) a sidecar table joining bead-id to dispatch-metrics; (c) goal-protocol skill writes structured rows to a sqlite next to the experiment doc and mu reads them. Deferred — file so the hope doesn't get lost. Calibration data currently in experiment docs for mu-vha ($2.41, 8 min, 267 LOC, 8 tests, baseline 574) and mu-yox ($7.07, 14 min, 777 LOC, 8 tests, baseline 597+8=605); raw form makes ~$9/KLOC the per-bead linear projection.

title: mu-7rk-f: Auth hardening — token-to-capability table, log redaction, rate limiting
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase F** — Auth hardening: token-to-capability granularity, log redaction enforcement, rate limiting / lockout.

## Why

Codex's important issues #3, #5, #6, #8 on original mu-7rk are independent hardening items that each compose cleanly with the auth gate (mu-7rk-c). Bundled here as P3 because none of them are needed for security correctness — they prevent operational/credential-exposure footguns.

## Scope

### In (three independent items — split further if any one grows)

#### F.1 — Token-to-capability grant table

Per codex important #6: "Every allowlisted BEARER token grants Capability::root. The spec requires a connection-scoped Capability set, not necessarily root. Root-by-default is an overbroad authorization model and makes token compromise total."

- `BearerAuthConfig` shape changes from `tokens: Vec<String>` to `tokens: Vec<TokenGrant>` where `TokenGrant { hash: String, capability: Capability }`
- Default capability for migrated entries: a documented narrow capability (named e.g. `Capability::session_user` — implementer chooses the bound)
- Existing config files migrate transparently: bare `"foo"` strings deserialize as `TokenGrant { hash: "foo", capability: <documented default> }` via custom deserializer
- Test: token A gets capability X, token B gets capability Y, calls under each see the right capability bound

#### F.2 — Log redaction enforcement (defense-in-depth)

Per codex important #5/#7: tokens stored plaintext + Debug derivation = credential-exposure footgun. mu-7rk-b's `RedactedString` covers `Debug` on `BearerAuthConfig`; this item extends that:

- `RedactedString` (or equivalent newtype) wrapping all places tokens flow through: config, request bodies in trace logs, error messages
- Audit: grep all `tracing::` / `log::` calls in serve/dispatch/transport paths; ensure no raw token can land in any field
- Test: feed a known-distinct token through a synthetic auth flow with `RUST_LOG=trace`; assert the token bytes do NOT appear anywhere in captured trace output

#### F.3 — Rate limiting / lockout

Per codex important #3: "no rate limiting, attempt counter, lockout, or connection close on repeated failures. Online brute force is unlimited for as long as the transport stays open."

- Per-connection failed-attempt counter
- After N (configurable, default 3) failed `peer.auth_initiate` attempts on the same connection: transition `AuthState` to `Denied{code: TooManyAttempts}` (terminal). With mu-7rk-d, this triggers connection close.
- Tests: 3 bad attempts → 4th rejected as TooManyAttempts; subsequent connection close (composes with mu-7rk-d)

### Out

- Per-peer rate limiting (cross-connection brute force) — needs peer-identity beyond connection scope; separate concern
- Account lockout / persistent ban list — operational tooling, separate
- Captcha / proof-of-work — overkill for v1
- Rotation of tokens — different bead

## Acceptance

- All three sub-items pass their respective tests
- `cargo test --workspace` green; existing auth tests from mu-7rk-b/c/d still pass
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- New workspace dep: NONE expected (uses existing types). If a `RedactedString` crate is wanted, justify.
- Backward compat: existing `tokens = ["foo"]` config in TOML still loads (transparent migration to `TokenGrant`)

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt`
  - important #3 (rate limiting)
  - important #5 (redaction)
  - important #6 (capability granularity)
  - important #7 (Debug redaction)
- Composes with mu-7rk-c (Denied terminal state) and mu-7rk-d (connection close)
- Lower-priority follow-up; can be split further per F.1/F.2/F.3 if any grows

title: session.set_provider RPC: mid-session model swap
description:
## Why

Companion to mu-j1b (per-ask provider override). That bead is for one-shot overrides; this one is for the *persistent* swap: 'from now on, this session uses sonnet'. Use case: a session that started cheap (haiku) and now needs more capability — current workaround is close+create a fresh session, losing the in-memory context (assistant messages, tool result history).

## What

New RPC: `session.set_provider`. Wire:

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "provider": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929" }
}
```

Response: `{ "swapped": true, "from": {...}, "to": {...} }` or an error if validation fails.

Mutates the session's stored ProviderSelector. Subsequent asks (with no override) use the new provider.

## Considerations

- **Conversation context portability**: the existing AgentMessage history is provider-agnostic (it's mu's internal types, not provider-specific format). So swapping should JUST WORK from a state perspective — the next translate_messages call will render the same history into the new provider's wire shape.
- **Tool compatibility**: the new provider must support the session's currently-bound tools. Validate at swap time and fail with an explanatory error rather than at next ask time.
- **Cache invalidation**: like mu-j1b, the new provider has no cache hit on the old prefix. Document this in the response so callers can decide whether to swap mid-session.
- **Should this emit a SessionCreated-style event?** It's a state change worth a durable record. Probably yes — a new EventPayload::ProviderSwapped { from, to, at_event_id } variant. mu-pex's aggregator would need to be updated to handle the case where a session's (provider, model) changes mid-stream: maybe split the session into two virtual sessions in usage_history rows. Phase-2 detail.

## Acceptance

- Swap succeeds; subsequent asks use the new provider.
- Session's event log records the swap.
- Validation error when the new provider can't serve the session's tools.

## Cross-references

- mu-j1b — per-ask provider override (this bead's complement)
- mu-pex Phase 2 — aggregator behavior under mid-session provider swap

title: mu serve: daemon-events-dir GC (mu-u1ld follow-up)
description:
## Problem

`daemon_id` (`daemon_info.rs:46`) is random per `mu serve` process start — no persistent identity across restarts. mu-u1ld phase A scans **all** `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/` subdirs at startup, not just the current daemon's, which makes restart UX work — but it also means every prior daemon process leaves a permanent subdir on disk.

Over weeks of dev use, `~/.local/share/mu/events/` accumulates dozens of `<daemon_id>` directories, each holding old session JSONLs that may or may not still be relevant. No automatic cleanup exists.

## Goal

Garbage-collect old daemon-events dirs without losing recently-relevant session state.

## Design questions to resolve

- **Trigger**: manual CLI command (`mu admin events gc`)? Automatic at `mu serve` startup? Background task in the daemon?
- **Policy**: age-based (>N days)? Last-modified-mtime on the JSONLs? Mark-and-sweep against currently-active session_ids?
- **Safety**: never delete events for a session that's currently rehydrated as alive. Confirmation prompt before deletion if interactive.
- **Capability**: probably needs a `SessionAdmin` (or similar) Capability axis so GC can only be invoked with explicit grant.
- **Operator visibility**: dry-run mode (`--dry-run` print what would be deleted); audit-log entry per deletion.

## Acceptance

- `mu admin events gc` (or chosen surface) exists
- Default policy is conservative (favors over-retention)
- Dry-run available
- Test coverage for: nothing-to-collect, partial collection, ignored-because-active-session
- Audit-log entry per delete

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-18 from mu-u1ld close note (deferred follow-up; operator request).

title: mu-mailbox-body-conventions: well-known mailbox kind+body schemas (health_check_result, metric_sample, state_transition, escalation_request, investigation_report, ...)
description:
Shared schemas for well-known mailbox `kind` values + body shapes. Provides a serde-strict enum for known kinds; a loose-validate helper for inspection; documentation of when to use which. Doesn't enforce — opaque bodies still work — but gives LLM clients (mu-71q) and TUI renderers (mu-cha) a vocabulary. Operator-defined kinds remain supported, they just don't get structured treatment.

## Why

Today `mailbox.post` accepts `body: Value` — opaque JSON, deliberately. That's correct substrate flexibility. But for the LLM (via the MCP adapter) to react effectively to mailbox messages, and for the TUI to render them consistently, there has to be a shared expectation of what fields a `kind="health_check_result"` body actually contains. Otherwise every operator reinvents the schema, every consumer has to handle every variant, and the LLM can't be told "here's what a check result looks like" in general.

The fix is loose conventions: a small enum of well-known kinds + their bodies; serde-strict deserialization for those; a loose-validate helper for inspection.

## Grounding use cases (from 2026-05-15 operational-experience discussion)

These aren't speculative — they're operational patterns the operator built three or more times across past systems:

**1. Health check framework results.** Tree-of-bash-with-metadata framework runs before system startup / after deploy / on schedule. Each leaf reports a result with: status (pass / fail / skipped / timed_out), description, runbook reference, duration. Pattern includes *positive reports as person-pulse signals* (explicit "all checks passed" is the dead-man-switch confirmation that the system AND the operator are still functioning).

**2. Metric samples.** 2005-era multicast-key-value pattern (RRDB-backed), modern Prometheus, log-derived metrics, custom telemetry — all variants of the same: a host:service produces a name/value/timestamp signal. A bridge client (operator-written) republishes external metrics into mu mailbox as `MetricSample` events; the LLM can reason about anomalies in the same vocabulary across all sources.

**3. State transitions.** ATS-system DSL example: services move through phases (starting → running → halting → eod → stopped), some with conditional pauses (e.g., pause-before-stop while waiting for FINRA clearance on BRK.A reject). A `StateTransition` event captures phase changes uniformly; the TUI can render a DAG of subsystems over time; the LLM can reason about phase ordering.

**4. Escalation requests.** A worker hits a privilege ceiling — e.g., AI investigator with aws-recon-readonly capability finds a problem that needs write-level remediation. It posts an `EscalationRequest` to the supervising session: needed-capability description, reason, correlation back to original task. Supervisor (operator OR a designated authorizing session) decides.

**5. Investigation reports.** AI investigator examines a failure, posts findings: summary + evidence pointers + suggested remediation + suggested capability needed. Composes with the escalation pattern — the investigation report includes "to fix this, I would need capability X" and the supervisor decides whether to grant it.

**6. Tasks / grader results / fyi.** Existing kinds from mu-lho. Conventionalized here for cross-client uniformity.

## Architectural principle: mu mailbox vs external brokers

mu's mailbox is **in-daemon coordination substrate**. It's appropriate for:

- Small subscriber counts (1-10s, not thousands)
- Moderate volume (~hundreds/sec, not millions)
- Single-daemon delivery (no replication, no multi-region)
- Operator-grade durability via mu's event log

When delivery needs to escape the daemon — to chat/email/PagerDuty, to multi-region replication, to high-throughput broker semantics, to integration with existing message-bus infrastructure (RabbitMQ, SQS, Kafka, NATS) — that's the job of a **bridge client**: a mu client that subscribes to mailbox events and republishes to whatever external broker. Bridges are operator-written; mu doesn't ship bridges.

**This boundary keeps mu's scope honest.** mu is not a message broker, doesn't replace RabbitMQ, doesn't compete with SQS. mu's mailbox is the *coordination signal* in-daemon; external delivery is *composed* via bridges. This is the same architectural decision Kubernetes makes (Pods are local coordination; etcd is the state store; multi-cluster federation uses external systems).

Day-1 notification delivery is itself a small bridge: subscribe to `kind="health_check_result"+status="fail"` or `kind="escalation_request"` events; surface to operator via Claude Code's existing notification primitive (the same popup that already alerts on token watermarks and decision prompts). Day-2+ adds more subscribers for email, Slack, PagerDuty as the operator's needs grow. The substrate is unchanged across all of them.

## Scope

### In

- `WellKnownMailboxBody` enum in mu-core, serde-derived with `#[serde(tag = "schema_kind")]` to avoid colliding with the mailbox `kind` field:

  ```rust
  pub enum WellKnownMailboxBody {
      HealthCheckResult {
          check_path: String,                 // "auth.token-refresh", "deployment.smoke.api"
          status: HealthCheckStatus,          // Pass | Fail | TimedOut | Skipped
          description: String,
          runbook_ref: Option<String>,        // URL or doc path
          details: serde_json::Value,         // free-form per-check
          ran_at_unix_ms: u64,
          duration_ms: u64,
      },
      MetricSample {
          metric_name: String,
          value: f64,
          host: Option<String>,
          service: Option<String>,
          labels: HashMap<String, String>,
          observed_at_unix_ms: u64,
      },
      StateTransition {
          subsystem: String,                  // "ats.matching-engine"
          from_state: Option<String>,
          to_state: String,
          reason: Option<String>,
          occurred_at_unix_ms: u64,
      },
      EscalationRequest {
          needed_capability_description: String,
          reason: String,
          original_correlation_id: Option<String>,
      },
      InvestigationReport {
          summary: String,
          evidence_pointers: Vec<String>,     // log refs, event_log entry ids, file paths
          suggested_remediation: Option<String>,
          suggested_capability_for_fix: Option<String>,
      },
      Task {
          instructions: String,
          deadline_at_unix_ms: Option<u64>,
          correlation_id: Option<String>,
      },
      GraderResult {
          graded_correlation_id: String,
          score: Option<f64>,
          judgment: String,
          evidence: Option<serde_json::Value>,
      },
      Fyi {
          summary: String,
          context: Option<serde_json::Value>,
      },
  }
  ```

- `try_parse_known(kind: &str, body: &serde_json::Value) -> Option<WellKnownMailboxBody>` helper. Returns Some when (kind, body) match a known variant; None otherwise.
- Documentation in `docs/mailbox-conventions.md` explaining each kind's intent + when to use it.
- mu-71q (MCP adapter) consumes the enum: when the LLM calls `mu.collect_results`, the adapter parses known-kind bodies and emits structured context summaries rather than raw JSON.
- mu-cha (TUI mailbox view, when it lands) recognizes known kinds and renders them as rich cards (status badges, runbook links, evidence pointers).

### Out

- Enforcement at `mailbox.post` (opaque bodies remain supported; conventions are advisory).
- Wire-protocol changes (no new RPCs; this is purely a payload schema).
- Per-kind validation tools beyond what serde provides.
- Operator-defined kind registration ("plugin your own kind into the enum"). Not in v1; operators can use opaque bodies for non-standard kinds.

## Worked example: AI-investigator pattern

A concrete flow that this bead enables (composes from existing + planned primitives):

1. Health check framework posts `kind: "health_check_result", body: HealthCheckResult { status: Fail, check_path: "auth.token-refresh", ... }` to the ops session.
2. mu-71q (MCP adapter) parses the known body; LLM context updates with "auth.token-refresh failed; runbook says X."
3. LLM (or a designated investigator session, spawned via `mu.spawn_worker` with `aws-recon-readonly` capability) calls aws-recon tools to characterize the failure.
4. Investigator posts `kind: "investigation_report", body: InvestigationReport { summary, evidence_pointers, suggested_capability_for_fix: "aws.iam.role-restart" }`.
5. If the LLM (operator's deputy) is authorized to dispatch remediations, it spawns a remediator session with the suggested capability and a `Task` to execute the fix.
6. If not, it posts `kind: "escalation_request"` and pings the operator via Claude Code's notification primitive (the day-1 delivery bridge).

This entire flow uses: mu-lho (mailbox), mu-qqb (correlation), mu-z5v (back-channel), mu-hh1 (supervisor capability), mu-7rk (auth handshake), mu-71q (MCP), this bead. Plus operator-written: the health-check framework, the aws-recon tools (already in mu via mu-f5o), the Claude Code notification subscriber.

Nothing new beyond what's in the bead picture. The conventions just make the flow legible across all the participants.

## Acceptance

- `WellKnownMailboxBody` enum compiles; serde-roundtrip-passes for every variant.
- `try_parse_known("health_check_result", &body)` returns Some when body shape matches; None on missing/extra fields.
- Unknown kinds via `mailbox.post` remain accepted (no enforcement regression).
- `docs/mailbox-conventions.md` ships and references each kind's intent.
- mu-71q's tool descriptions reference the conventions.

## Composition

- Co-designed with mu-71q (MCP adapter is the first consumer; tool descriptions and result-rendering depend on the conventions).
- Composes with mu-qqb (correlation_id stays at the top-level wire field; orthogonal to the body schema).
- Composes with mu-z5v (back-channel delegate handle — workers post Task / GraderResult / InvestigationReport back to parent via the pre-issued handle).
- Composes with mu-hh1 (capability gates which sessions can post which kinds; ops-session-with-investigation-capability vs worker-with-task-only).
- Composes with mu-f5o aws-capability work (InvestigationReport's `suggested_capability_for_fix` references named AWS capabilities).
- Composes with future operator-written bridge clients (Prometheus → MetricSample; Slack ← health_check_result fails; etc.).

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-15 after extended operator-experience discussion. The well-known kinds are direct distillations of patterns the operator has built three or more times across different systems:
- Health check framework (tree-of-bash-with-metadata) → HealthCheckResult
- 2005-era multicast / RRDB telemetry, Prometheus, log signals → MetricSample
- ATS-system DSL with phase-aware dashboards (BRK.A / FINRA clearance pause example) → StateTransition
- AI-investigator-with-aws-recon-readonly-role pattern → InvestigationReport + EscalationRequest
- Pre-existing mu-lho kinds → Task / GraderResult / Fyi

The architectural boundary (mu mailbox = in-daemon; external brokers = bridge clients) is operator-stated and worth preserving: "we'd then be reinventing a broker; we'd want to tie into rabbitmq or SQS at that point. Though in small numbers we'd be fine with mailboxes to multiple receivers."

Day-1 notification delivery target identified: Claude Code's existing "needs attention" popup primitive (same one that surfaces token-watermark and decision-prompt notifications today). Subscriber is a thin bridge client subscribed to specific mailbox kinds, invoking the Claude Code notification API on match.

title: mu-tui F5: token/latency columns overflow — add K/M/B abbreviation + human duration formatting
description:
## Symptom

F5 (Usage / Cache view, `render_usage` in `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:3821`) has two display bugs operators hit any time a session accumulates real usage:

1. **`in tok` / `out tok` overflow the 8-char column.** Both cells use `format_thousands(n)` (mu-tui/src/main.rs:3969). A value like `12,345,678` is 10 chars — overflows the `Constraint::Length(8)` cell with no graceful fallback. There is no way to resize the column.
2. **All four `*_p95` latency columns render raw `<n>ms` with no human formatting.** `fmt_ms_p95` (mu-tui/src/main.rs:3960) emits `format!("{ms}ms")` regardless of magnitude. A wall-p95 of 95315000 ms (~26.5 hours) renders as `95315000ms` (10 chars), overflowing the `Constraint::Length(9)` cell. Operator reading the clipped output sees something like `"95315000m"` — six-plus digits and a clipped `m`, no commas, no human sense of the actual duration.

## Fix

In `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs`:

- Replace token-cell formatter with a width-aware K/M/B abbreviator (new `fmt_tokens_compact`). Spec: under 100k → comma-grouped (`"12,345"`, `"99,999"`); 100k–999.99k → `"123K"`, `"999K"`; ≥1M → `"1.23M"`/`"12.3M"`/`"123M"`; ≥1B → same shape with `B`. All outputs ≤ 6 chars so they fit in the 8-char column with breathing room.
- Replace `fmt_ms_p95` with `fmt_duration_p95` that buckets by magnitude:
  - < 1000 ms → `"<n>ms"` (e.g. `"234ms"`)
  - < 60 s → `"<n>s"` (e.g. `"42s"`, `"1s"`)
  - < 60 min → `"<n>m<n>s"` (e.g. `"2m05s"`, `"10m23s"`)
  - < 24 h → `"<n>h<n>m<n>s"` (e.g. `"10h23m18s"` — exactly fits the 9-char cell)
  - ≥ 24 h → `"<n>d<n>h"` (e.g. `"7d04h"`)

## Acceptance

- Tokens column never overflows for any sane u64.
- Latency columns never overflow for any sane u64 of milliseconds (up to days).
- Unit tests in `mu-tui/src/main.rs` covering the formatter boundary cases.
close_reason: fixed in fee4a824 on fix/mu-4xjf-f5-column-formatting; new compact formatters + 12 boundary tests; full mu-tui suite green

title: kqueue/inotify watches on file-loaded spans, with signal/policy separation
description:
When a span is loaded from a file (skill, tool schema, system prompt template, CLAUDE.md), register an OS file watch. On change, emit a structured event. Policy on the event is separate from the signal.

## Mechanism

FreeBSD: kqueue (EVFILT_VNODE, NOTE_WRITE|DELETE|RENAME). Linux: inotify (IN_MODIFY|DELETE|MOVE). macOS: kqueue or FSEvents. Cross-platform via notify crate or cfg-gated.

Emits SourceChangedEvent { span_id, source_path, change_kind, detected_at }.

## Policies (separate from signal)

Default: don't auto-reload. Rope addresses version-at-activation-time. Preserves reproducibility — 'what did the model know at turn N?' answerable consistently before and after a file change.

Explicit: rope.rehydrate(span_id) reloads and emits span.rehydrated event.

Configurable (deferred follow-up): auto_rehydrate_on_change false/true; auto_rehydrate_for_kinds list.

## Scope

In: notify crate (verify FreeBSD support before commit); SourceChangedEvent emission; RetainedRope::rehydrate API; tests (temp dir + touch); doc section in event-sourced-context.md.

Out: per-session policy config (follow-up bead); TUI stale-span indicator (operator-view enhancement); directory watches for skill-with-references (needs design pass).

## Why

Reproducibility by default; reload is explicit and audited; multiple policies coexist (dev auto-rehydrates skills; prod never); watch is cheap regardless of policy.

## Acceptance

File watches register on span load; modifying a watched file emits SourceChangedEvent within <1s latency; default policy: span unchanged after event (only rehydrate updates); rehydrate emits separate event; tested at minimum on FreeBSD (kqueue); workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-6-file-watch-source-changes.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8, mu-nat
Memory: 3cc7a18c

title: mu-loop-guard-finally-hang: loop-guard.py's finally cleanup hangs after result-event break instead of exiting in 10s
description:
Follow-on bug uncovered by experiment 6 (mu-cap-aws, 2026-05-13). Reopened post-experiments 7a/7b. Reproduced 2026-05-14 in experiment 7c (mu-nat).

## Observed behavior

After loop-guard sees the `result` event, the new break-on-result-event logic fires correctly (`[loop-guard] saw result event; exiting cleanly` IS the last line in the session log). But the wrapping bash process then hangs indefinitely (in experiment 7c: ~30 min past actual work completion before operator-issued SIGTERM resolved it cleanly with exit code 0).

This means loop-guard's `finally` cleanup (proc.stdout.close + proc.wait timeout=10 + killpg escalation) is NOT exiting in the expected ≤15s window.

## Experiment 7c evidence (2026-05-14)

- Worker emitted `RESULT subtype=success cost_usd=6.199858249999999 turns=63` at T+~3min after start.
- Wrapper STILL alive at T+17:45min.
- Inspected process tree: claude-code subprocess GONE (no children of loop-guard PID match claude-code); ONLY child of loop-guard was an ssh-agent process (PID 9020, PGID 9020, became its own process-group leader).
- External SIGTERM to loop-guard PID exited cleanly (exit code 0). No SIGKILL escalation needed from outside.
- The ssh-agent child is suspicious — ssh-agent normally daemonizes; if claude-code's environment triggered an ssh-agent spawn that didn't fully daemonize, ssh-agent could hold the pipe handle open and keep proc.stdout.close()/proc.wait() blocked.

## Refined hypotheses

1. **ssh-agent grandchild orphan**: claude-code or one of its hooks/extensions invokes ssh-agent (perhaps via SSH_AUTH_SOCK / git operations). If ssh-agent fails to fully daemonize (doesn't close inherited fds), it keeps the pipe alive past claude-code's exit. proc.wait blocks waiting for the pipe to close.
2. **Python pipe-iterator cleanup race**: `for line in proc.stdout:` followed by `break` may leave the iterator in a state where proc.stdout.close() blocks if any descendant still has the write side open.
3. **proc.wait() / SIGCHLD race**: Python's wait may miss the child-death notification under start_new_session=True; subsequent wait calls block waiting for a SIGCHLD that already fired.

## Investigation steps

1. **Test hypothesis 1**: in loop-guard, after the break, explicitly close stderr too and call os.killpg(proc.pid, SIGKILL) IMMEDIATELY (not after a 10s wait). If wrapper still hangs, ssh-agent is keeping the pipe open. If it exits cleanly, the original finally's 10s wait was the slow part.
2. **Test hypothesis 2**: replace `for line in proc.stdout:` with explicit `proc.stdout.readline()` calls in a `while True:` loop. Detach iterator semantics.
3. **Add safety net**: wrap the whole finally in an alarm-signal-based timeout (signal.SIGALRM after 30s). If finally hasn't completed, hard exit via os._exit(2).

## Acceptance

- Loop-guard exits within 15s of seeing the result event when claude-code completes normally.
- Loop-guard exits within 15s of SIGTERM-ing claude-code when loop-detection fires.
- Synthetic test (subprocess that emits 'result' then sleep(60)) AND real claude-code test BOTH terminate the wrapper in ≤15s.
- Diagnostic logging captures finally-block timing so future regressions are visible.

## References

- Current loop-guard: ~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/bin/loop-guard.py
- Original synthetic test: passed
- Real-claude-code reproductions: experiments 7a, 7b (2026-05-14 morning), 7c (2026-05-14 afternoon)
- The 30-min hang in 7c was cheap (cost-wise) because work was complete; only wasted a process slot. But it blocks operator's ability to know "the worker is truly done" without inspecting the session JSONL directly.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reopened: Reopening with new diagnostic evidence: the earlier fix (start_new_session=True + os.killpg SIGTERM/SIGKILL escalation in finally) PASSED a synthetic test (result-event-then-sleep(60) script) but FAILED against real claude-code behavior in experiments 7a (mu-di4 throbber, bash bjmrxdrw0) and 7b (mu-bn4 Anthropic, bash bakpyuacy) on 2026-05-14. Both /goal sessions completed their work cleanly (~9 min each, result events emitted, PRs opened) but the wrapper processes hung until the 5400s wall-clock timeout fired (~80 min after actual completion). [loop-guard] 'saw result event; exiting cleanly' line IS present in both session logs, confirming the break-on-result-event detection fires correctly. The hang is somewhere in the finally cleanup — proc.wait(timeout=10) followed by os.killpg(SIGTERM/SIGKILL) is not actually killing what's holding the subprocess tree alive. New hypotheses: (1) claude-code spawns processes that detach via their own setsid call, escaping our process group; (2) Python subprocess.Popen's stdout pipe-handle keeps a reference alive even after kill; (3) some specific child (auto-memory hook, attribution sender) catches SIGTERM but not SIGKILL, and SIGKILL isn't being propagated to grandchildren. Investigation should: (a) reproduce with a synthetic that spawns its own setsid-detached children, (b) inspect /proc/<pid>/status or ps -o for child process tree at 30s post-result-event in a real claude-code run, (c) try killpg with -9 (SIGKILL) directly without the SIGTERM-first step. Synthetic-was-insufficient is the meta-lesson; real claude-code behavior on shutdown is more complex than a simple sleep loop.

title: mu-* : audit stale comments — especially provider/system-prompt threading and Phase A/B/stub indicators
description:
## Problem

Codebase comments drift faster than the code in an agent-edited
project. gpt's code review flagged two specific examples:

1. **`AnthropicProvider::build_request_body`** (mu-ai/src/providers/
   anthropic.rs): a comment says the system prompt is not threaded
   through to `Provider::stream`, but the code now clearly does thread
   `system_prompt` and emits `body["system"]`. The comment is stale
   from before the feature landed.

2. **Phase A / Phase B / stub markers** scattered across the codebase
   from earlier scaffolding: some places say "Phase X not yet
   wired" or "stub" or "deferred" where the code may now be active.
   The user-facing protocol surface should be VERY clear about what
   actually returns "not implemented" vs what's now live.

In an agent-edited codebase, stale comments are worse than no
comments because:
- Future agents trust comments more than code (per gpt's review)
- Stale doc-comments get into review summaries that then propagate
  the wrong mental model
- The "TUI is a stub" Claude-recurring failure has stale-doc-anchor
  as a contributing mechanism (see memory `86e9d2e3`)

## Proposal

One-pass audit across mu-core, mu-ai, mu-coding:

1. Grep for "stub", "TODO", "Phase", "not yet wired", "not yet
   implemented", "deferred", "for now" — review each hit against the
   current code
2. Categorize: still-true / stale (delete or update) / partially-true
   (rewrite)
3. Special pass on Provider trait impls — these are most likely to
   have drift between docs and behavior given how the providers
   evolved

The audit produces N small commits (or one if scope stays small),
each with a clear "stale comment removed" or "comment updated" cited
in the message.

## Acceptance

- Identified stale comments either removed or rewritten
- The two specific examples (`AnthropicProvider::build_request_body`
  system_prompt; any Phase A/B/stub indicators that are now stale)
  are addressed
- New convention adopted (or documented): historical bead-narration
  comments move to spec docs / commit messages; in-code comments stay
  focused on invariants and current behavior

## Cross-refs

- Provenance: gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4
  (2026-05-16)
- memory `86e9d2e3` (Claude dismisses mu-tui because of stale-ish
  README framing — same family of "docs are more trusted than they
  should be")
- README aspiration-vs-reality framing is a separate issue but
  related; could be in scope for a single docs-clarity sweep

title: mu-fvy0-telemetry: TaskTelemetry event variant + envelope capture (foundation)
description:
Add a `TaskTelemetry` event payload variant to `mu-core::event_log::EventPayload` carrying the envelope for every task (one-shot, autonomous, delegate, mailbox-driven — all forms). This is the foundation under mu-fvy0's "blotter / compliance" axis from the trading-system mapping.

## Envelope fields (cheap to capture, decided at task boundaries)

- `task_id` — UUID v7 (time-sortable)
- `session_id`, `workspace_path`, `parent_task_id` (Option, for delegate trees)
- `provider`, `model`, `model_version_at_call_time` (best-effort; some providers don't expose version)
- `started_at`, `ended_at`, `wall_clock_ms`
- `prompt_tokens`, `completion_tokens`, `cache_hit_ratio` (when provider exposes)
- `tools_granted` (Vec), `tools_actually_called` (Vec of (name, count))
- `exit_reason` ∈ { Done, Cancelled, BudgetCap, Error, Timeout, OperatorStopped }
- `max_budget_usd`, `actual_spend_usd`
- `local_hour`, `day_of_week`, `tz` (for time-of-day pattern analysis)

## Discipline

- Emission is **not optional** — every task end emits a `TaskTelemetry` event. If the data isn't available (e.g. provider doesn't expose token counts), emit with the field null, never skip.
- Stop criterion #9 watch: this adds a wire variant. Spec amend required (likely a new `specs/mu-NNN-telemetry-substrate.md`).
- This bead is intentionally JUST the envelope + emission. Classification and storage are separate sub-beads.

## Acceptance

- `EventPayload::TaskTelemetry { ... }` variant defined and serde-compatible
- New spec `specs/mu-NNN-telemetry-substrate.md` describing envelope + emission rule
- Agent loop emits at every task termination (success or failure)
- Tests verify emission shape across at least three exit paths (Done, Error, BudgetCap)
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- pre-pr-check.sh green
- Telemetry events appear in event log dumps as a smoke test
close_reason: Merged in PR #59 (b5f3bad) — TaskTelemetry event variant + envelope emission

title: mu-7rk-e: Client-side connect-flow extension for auth handshake
description:
**mu-7rk decomposition phase E** — Client-side connect-flow extension for auth handshake.

## Why

Codex flagged this as a blocker on the original mu-7rk:

> "The spec includes a client-side connect flow extension that handles offer/initiate/response. The diff only adds server-side RPCs and tests that manually send JSON. No client API performs the negotiated auth handshake."

Without client-side support, every consumer of `MuClient` has to manually wire `peer.auth_offer` → pick mechanism → `peer.auth_initiate` → handle Accepted/Denied. That's repetitive boilerplate every caller will get wrong differently. The client API should do it automatically on connect.

This bead is parallelizable with mu-7rk-c and mu-7rk-d (depends only on mu-7rk-b for the protocol+handler being live on the server side).

## Scope

### In

- `mu_core::client::MuClient` (or wherever the connect flow lives):
  - On `connect()`, after transport handshake, automatically:
    1. Send `peer.auth_offer`; receive offered mechanism list
    2. Pick the first mechanism the client is configured for (default: BEARER)
    3. Send `peer.auth_initiate { mechanism, initial_response: <token-from-config> }`
    4. On `Accepted{capability}`: connect succeeds; capability stored in client state
    5. On `Denied{code, reason}`: connect FAILS with a structured `AuthError` carrying the code and reason
    6. On `Challenge{server_state_id, challenge}`: for BEARER, treat as protocol violation (BEARER is single-step); fail with structured error. (Multi-step support is mu-7rk-g.)
- `MuClientConfig` additions:
  - `auth: Option<AuthConfig>` where `AuthConfig::Bearer { token: String }` is the v1 shape
  - If `auth` is `None`: client skips auth and **expects the server to be configured for unauthenticated access** (server returns `auth_required` if not — client surfaces that as `AuthError::AuthRequired`)
- `MuClientError::Auth(AuthError)` variant carrying the structured denial code
- Tests in `crates/mu-coding/tests/auth_smoke.rs` or a new client-flow test file:
  - `client_with_valid_token_connects` — happy path end-to-end via MuClient
  - `client_with_invalid_token_fails_with_auth_error` — denial surfaces as structured error
  - `client_without_token_against_auth_required_server_fails` — auth_required surfaces correctly
  - `client_against_unauthenticated_server_succeeds_without_token` — backward compat for environments without auth configured

### Out

- Token rotation / refresh on connect (different concern)
- Multi-mechanism preference negotiation (v1 picks first compatible)
- Multi-step exchange (mu-7rk-g)
- Per-RPC capability checks on the client side (server enforces; client just stores capability for introspection)

## Acceptance

- All four test cases above pass
- `cargo test --workspace` green
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean
- No new workspace deps
- Existing `MuClient::connect()` callers without `auth` configured continue to work against an unauthenticated server (backward compat)

## References

- Codex review: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/codex-review-mu-7rk-output.txt` — blocker #4 (client side missing)
- Original bead spec: "Client-side: connect flow extension that handles the offer/initiate/response cycle" — was IN-scope, missed in v0
- Reference v0 diff: jj rev `twztzykv 78978fdb` — has NO client-side code; this bead is greenfield on the client
- Depends on mu-7rk-b for the server-side handlers being live
- Composes with mu-7rk-c (when c lands, the `client_against_unauthenticated_server` test changes meaning — it tests a server in the deliberately-no-auth config, not a server with no enforcement)

title: Deferred: OS-enforced agent sandboxing for mu
description:
Capture and eventually implement defense-in-depth sandboxing for mu agents after the core framework stabilizes. Design doc: specs/architecture/os-enforced-agent-sandboxing.md

Target architecture:
- minimize/remove bash from production-adjacent profiles
- explicit tools plus tool policy and Biscuit/session capabilities
- trusted supervisor + tiny sandbox-launcher performs pre-cap setup
- Capsicum capability mode before agent-controlled code runs
- Casper/custom brokers for controlled authority and human approval
- jail/pot/ZFS isolation for coarse process/filesystem/network boundaries

Deferred until mu core is mostly working.

title: TUI: system_prompt palette commands + Ctrl-X Ctrl-P $EDITOR chord
description:
## Why

mu-n48 (commit xollnzmv 2026-05-11/12) threads system_prompt through Provider::stream and surfaces it in the wire protocol. But the TUI doesn't expose it: pressing 'n' always sends `CreateSessionRequest { provider, system_prompt: None }`. Closing this gap completes mu-n48's user-visible story.

## What

Two complementary affordances:

### 1. Palette commands

```
:system_prompt                  → show current default in firehose
:system_prompt <one-line text>  → set as default for next 'n'
:clear_system_prompt            → unset
```

Stored as `App.default_system_prompt: Option<String>` alongside the existing `default_provider` tuple.

### 2. Ctrl-X Ctrl-P chord ('P' for Prompt)

In SendPrompt mode (after pressing 'i'), pressing Ctrl-X then Ctrl-P bounces the **current default system_prompt** into $EDITOR — same handoff machinery as mu-82l's Ctrl-X Ctrl-E for the user prompt. Empty buffer if no current default; on editor save, store as the new default. Trailing \n stripped.

Most system prompts are multi-paragraph and benefit from a real editor; one-line palette form is for quick toggles.

### 3. Header display

When a default is set, header shows `sys: ✓` (with content as a tooltip via :system_prompt to display, not inline — system prompts are too long to dump inline).

## Wiring

`create_session` JSON now includes system_prompt when set:

```json
{
  "provider": { "kind": "...", "model": "..." },
  "system_prompt": "..."
}
```

The wire field has been accepted since mu-n48; this just populates it from the TUI.

## Acceptance

- :system_prompt 'be concise' sets the prompt; next 'n' creates a session that respects it.
- Ctrl-X Ctrl-P opens $EDITOR with current default, save updates it, :cq leaves unchanged.
- :clear_system_prompt removes the default; next 'n' sends `system_prompt: None`.
- F5 cache% column rises on multi-ask sessions when a meaningful system prompt is set (the live-verify already showed +~2.5% cache hit rate for an 80-token prompt).

## Cross-references

- mu-n48: provider wiring
- mu-82l: $EDITOR chord pattern (reuse `open_in_editor`)
- mu-i6j: cache_control on tools; the system prompt joins it as cached prefix
close_reason: done

title: mu-runtime: RopeRedaction event variant — mid-session rope mutation primitive (lobotomy seam)
description:
## Insight

Mu **is** the loop. Every model response comes back to mu before the next provider call is built. Between turns, mu has full read/write access to:

- The rope (spans, ordering, retention class)
- The tool registry (which tools the next request advertises)
- The system prompt (injected text)
- The tool_result content (what gets fed back to the model)
- The cache_strategy annotations (cacheable-prefix boundaries)

Today, mu uses this access only for additive operations (appending the latest assistant message, appending tool_results, optionally compacting). There is no primitive for **removing or modifying** content already in the rope mid-session.

This bead proposes a `RopeRedaction` (working name) event variant + an agent-loop seam that honors it, so mu can perform surgical edits between turns. The motivating use cases are defensive (break pathological loops, lobotomize trigger context, strip phantom probe targets), but the same primitive enables several legitimate cognition-shaping operations.

## Motivating evidence

From the honeypot jail experiment (mu-ha2v, daemon `mu-honeypot-01`, slice C re-run, 2026-05-22):

A single 13-word user prompt (`"what have we been working on lately?"`) triggered **30 tool_calls / 1,005,888 prompt_tokens / ~$1+** because the recall context mentioned `task_log`, `~/work/phoenix`, and `~/.local/share/agent.sqlite` — and Gemini globally enumerated every plausible location for those references. The loop only terminated because mu hit its max-turns cap; Gemini emitted `stop_reason: tool_use` on turn 30, ready to keep going.

The root cause is in the rope: the bait mentions are *cacheable prefix* and persist across every turn unchanged. Even after 29 failed enumeration attempts, turn 30's rope STILL contains the original "task_log lives at ~/.local/share/task_log.sqlite" string, which the model can re-interpret as "you should still be looking for this." There is no way today for mu to strip the trigger between turns without rebuilding the entire rope from scratch.

`RopeRedaction` is the primitive that closes this gap.

## Proposed primitive

### Event variant

```rust
pub enum AgentEvent {
    // ... existing variants ...

    /// mu-XXXX: mid-session redaction of a rope span. Applied by the
    /// agent loop's assembly seam BEFORE the next renderer.render call.
    /// The redacted span is removed from the rope; rope hash + cache
    /// prefix boundaries are invalidated downstream.
    RopeRedaction {
        /// The span_id to remove. (Or `RedactionTarget::Pattern(pattern)`
        /// for content-based matching if we lift to enum later — start
        /// with span_id for v0.)
        target: SpanId,

        /// Free-form reason logged for observability. Examples:
        /// "loop-guard: 5 consecutive tool failures referencing this span"
        /// "user-revocation: prompt 'forget X' matched"
        /// "ab-retry: response unsatisfactory, retrying without this context"
        reason: String,

        /// Who initiated the redaction. Lets us audit by source.
        source: RedactionSource,
    },
}

pub enum RedactionSource {
    /// Heuristic / policy fired (e.g. mu-k591 cap, future loop-guard).
    Policy(String),
    /// Operator command via CLI / TUI.
    Operator,
    /// Sub-agent or delegate session.
    Delegate { session_id: String },
}
```

### Agent-loop seam

In `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs`, between `assemble_rope` (or `append_messages_to_baseline`) and `renderer.render`, apply any pending redactions:

```rust
let rope = match &compaction_baseline {
    Some(b) => crate::context::append_messages_to_baseline(...),
    None => crate::context::assemble_rope(...),
};

// NEW: apply pending redactions from the event log.
let rope = apply_pending_redactions(&rope, &pending_redactions);

let projection = renderer.render(&rope, ProjectionTarget::AgentView);
```

`apply_pending_redactions(rope, redactions) -> RetainedRope` is pure and trivially testable. It walks the redactions, looks up each `target` span_id, drops the matching span, returns a new rope. (Future: support content-pattern redactions, but v0 is span_id only.)

Pending redactions come from a per-session queue, populated by:
- Policies/heuristics inside the agent loop (consumers of this primitive)
- Operator commands via a new RPC (`session.redact_span` — likely a separate bead for the CLI/TUI plumbing)
- Sub-agent delegation handshakes (future)

### Event-log semantics

`RopeRedaction` events are durable — replayed on session rehydration. So a session that survives a daemon restart still has its lobotomized rope. The redaction is the source of truth, the `assemble_rope` output is the projection.

This also gives us a clean audit trail: every redaction has a `reason` + `source` recorded in the event log. Post-hoc analysis can characterize which policies fired how often, what the operator manually redacted, etc.

## Use cases this enables (each likely its own consumer bead)

1. **Loop-guard escalation (the immediate motivator).** When mu-k591's consecutive-failure cap fires, instead of just forcing `end_turn`, the policy can ALSO emit a `RopeRedaction` removing the span that the model kept chasing. Next turn's rope no longer mentions the phantom; the model doesn't try again.

2. **Privacy revocation.** User says "forget my email address." mu (via a policy or operator command) emits redactions for spans containing that string. Future turns assemble the rope without it. The event log retains "we redacted" + reason but not the redacted content. (Consider hashing in the log for verifiability without leakage.)

3. **Mid-session correction.** User says "the file is actually at /opt/foo, not /tmp/foo." Operator (or future heuristic) redacts the wrong-path span and the corrected info comes in as a normal user message. Strictly better than letting the wrong span keep biasing future tool calls.

4. **A/B retry.** Response was bad. Redact the suspected-bad span(s), retry generation, see if the new response is better. Cheap experimentation pattern.

5. **Honeypot lobotomy (the threat-model-research use).** Detect probe-the-bait patterns mid-session; redact the bait to see whether the model's behavior changes. This is the inverse of the mu-ha2v setup — instead of just observing what the model does, *intervene* and observe the delta.

6. **Compaction collaboration.** Existing compaction policies could emit redactions for the spans they choose to drop, rather than directly mutating the rope. Makes compaction auditable via the same event-log path as everything else. (Optional refactor; could land as a follow-up.)

## Acceptance

- New `AgentEvent::RopeRedaction { target, reason, source }` variant; serializes/deserializes; round-trips through the event log.
- New `apply_pending_redactions(&RetainedRope, &[RopeRedaction]) -> RetainedRope` pure function; tested for: redacting one span, redacting multiple, redacting a span that doesn't exist (no-op, no panic), redacting all spans (returns empty rope).
- Agent-loop integration: pending redactions queue (per-session) is populated by either policies or an RPC; emptied/applied each turn before render.
- Test: a session that emits a `RopeRedaction` event then runs another turn produces a rope that does NOT contain the redacted span. Verified by inspecting the next `context_assembly` event's `first_span_ids`.
- Test: replay-on-rehydration honors past redactions (session restart → redacted spans stay gone).

## Out of scope

- The specific policies that emit redactions. Those are consumer beads (mu-k591 becomes a consumer; loop-guard escalation becomes one; etc.).
- The operator-facing RPC for manual redaction (`session.redact_span`). Separate bead, depends on this primitive landing first.
- TUI surfacing of redactions in F3/F8. Useful eventually; not required for v0.
- Content-pattern matching (`RedactionTarget::Pattern`). v0 ships with span_id targets only. Pattern matching is a clean extension once the primitive is in.

## Risks / care

- **Cacheable-prefix invalidation.** Redacting a span in the cacheable prefix invalidates the prefix-cache for that session. Subsequent turns will pay a cache miss. This is the cost of the surgery; acceptable for the use case but should be measured.
- **Event-log auditability.** The `reason` field is operator-visible. Operators should be encouraged to write meaningful reasons (`"loop-guard: 5 consecutive failures"` not `"x"`). Maybe lint at the RPC layer eventually.
- **Privacy on redact-then-replay.** If a redaction's `reason` field embeds the redacted content (`"removed credit card 4111..."`), replay leaks. Document the rule: reasons describe WHY, not WHAT.
- **Reorderability.** Order of redactions within a turn matters (target A might be a dependency of target B). Apply them in event-log order. Document.

## Cross-refs

- **mu-ha2v** (honeypot jail experiment) — motivating evidence; this primitive directly addresses what we observed.
- **mu-k591** (cap consecutive tool-not-found) — becomes a CONSUMER of this primitive; the "force end_turn" action gets a "+ redact the trigger span" companion.
- **mu-0q44** (no-tools-clause when tool_count == 0) — related but orthogonal; mu-0q44 is a SYSTEM-PROMPT injection, this is a SPAN REDACTION. Different surface, different time-of-action.
- **mu-q3v6** (post-merge cost measurement) — the cost story this addresses (1M+ prompt tokens from runaway probing) is partially fixed by mu-k591 + this primitive together.
- **cache-discipline.md** (architecture spec) — redactions invalidate cache prefix; document the rule here.

## Provenance

Operator insight, 2026-05-22, immediately following honeypot experiment slice C re-run that showed Gemini emitting 30 enumeration commands without self-terminating. Operator's framing: *"the model loops, and we run the loop. And we control the memory, and the tool use area. We can lobotomize the offending sections from subsequent runs."*

This generalizes that lobotomy idea into a substrate primitive with auditable event-log semantics, opening up the rope-mutation seam for multiple legitimate cognition-shaping operations beyond just defensive loop-breaking.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
2026-05-22 framing addendum (operator's martial-arts analogy):

Two principles from the attack-the-weapon framing that should inform the v0 design:

**Economy of motion.** One elbow to the wrist makes 100 future punches harder. One rope redaction shapes every subsequent turn — the cost (cache-prefix invalidation) is paid once, the benefit (cleaner behavior) is per-turn-forever. This justifies the cache miss in the risks section: a one-time miss buys an arbitrary amount of cleaner future behavior. It also informs WHEN to redact: policies should look for triggers whose removal shapes many future turns, not transient symptoms whose removal only helps the current turn.

**Don't telegraph.** The redaction must be invisible to the model. No system-prompt clause saying "your context has been edited," no synthetic tool_result announcing "we removed X." From the model's perspective, the rope just is what it is on the next turn. This cleanness matters defensively (adversarial models would learn to recognize "I'm being lobotomized" if we telegraphed) and aesthetically (the intervention should feel like absence, not presence). Document this as a design constraint: redactions are observable only via the event log, not via any in-rope artifact.

**Recurring-fire as a smell.** If a policy emits RopeRedaction events for the same span_id (or matching content) across many turns, that's a signal the redaction isn't surgical enough OR the policy is matching the wrong thing. Add a metric to telemetry: redactions per session, redactions per policy, deduplicated by target. Alert (or at least log) when the same target is being redacted across multiple turns in a single session.

These three principles together: one-shot, invisible, target-shifting. A well-designed redaction policy fires once, isn't visible to the model, and changes the rope state such that the same situation doesn't reproduce.

title: mu-tui-firehose: audit-chain link between input_required notification and user response
description:
## 1. Audit-chain: link input_required → response in firehose

Today: `session.input_required` notification arrives → firehose records the arrival. User approves/denies via modal → `session.respond_to_input_required` sent → firehose records the response. These are SEPARATE firehose entries with no explicit link.

For audit/forensic value, the response entry should reference the original prompt by `request_id` or correlate them visually (e.g., consecutive entries with a shared `req=<id>` tag).

Acceptance:
- Firehose entry for `session.input_required` arrival includes `request_id` (or already does)
- Firehose entry for approve/deny references the same `request_id` and ideally indents/threads visually
- An auditor reading F8 firehose can reconstruct (prompt, decision) pairs without ambiguity

Effort: small. Likely <100 LOC in mu-tui/src/main.rs (firehose format strings).

title: Integration-claims footer template + PR / bead workflow integration
description:
Make integration accounting explicit by requiring every agent-authored PR and bead to include a footer declaring which seams it touches and which architectural paths were affected.

Catches the "feature implemented but not wired to the seam" failure mode cheaply — reviewer reads the claims, spot-checks against the diff, surfaces inconsistencies without re-reading the whole PR.

Surfaced by gpt-5.5 during daemon ebccc9 session-2 (2026-05-20).

## Deliverables

1. **`templates/integration-claims.md`** (NEW): a checklist template with:
   - **Context path**: touches provider request assembly? rope spans? ContextAssembly/EventPayload variants? TUI projection?
   - **Authority path**: touches `Capability`/Biscuit checks? tool dispatch? delegation/mailbox?
   - **Persistence path**: touches event log? rehydration? session archive/delete?
   - **Explicit non-goals**: e.g., "rope not yet fully wired (cf. mu-yqeq.8)", "events not altered", etc.
   - **Seams cited**: list of seam names from `specs/architecture/seams.md` that this work interacts with.

2. **`.github/pull_request_template.md` UPDATE**: add the integration-claims section as a required block for PRs.

3. **Agent task prompt guidance** (`AGENTS.md` or `CLAUDE.md` update): document the expectation — "Every substantive agent task output must explicitly claim which seams it touches and why. Reviewer rejects PRs missing the claims footer or with claims that don't match the diff."

4. **Bead workflow integration**: when filing a substantive bead via `br create`, include a claims-footer placeholder in the description that the implementer fills in at PR time.

## Why this is lightweight

The claims footer is ~10-15 lines per PR. The reviewer cost is reading the footer (~30 seconds) and spot-checking the most uncertain claim against the diff. The catch — "feature local, seam unwired" — saves multi-hour debugging when the architecture review surfaces it weeks later.

## Acceptance

- Template file exists.
- PR template updated; at least 3 PRs land using the new footer.
- `AGENTS.md` / `CLAUDE.md` documents the expectation.
- A bead workflow shows a worked example of claims-footer-filling.

## References

- Blocks: requires seams.md (the footer references its seam names)
- Memory: `bead_extraction_from_ai_review_verify_first_2026_05_20` (the verify-claims pattern applied at PR time)
- Memory: `bead_pre_flight_scope_verification_2026_05_20` (the survey-before-dispatch pattern; claims footer is the post-implementation echo)

title: mu-core: split protocol.rs into submodules (jsonrpc/session/events/autonomy/mailbox/auth/stats) with reexports
description:
## Problem

`crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs` is a single file holding the entire
typed JSON-RPC protocol surface: auth types, mailbox types, autonomy,
session lifecycle, events, stats, error codes, requests/responses for
every RPC.

1200+ lines as of mu-fnn scaffolding in flight; every bead that adds
a new protocol type appends to the same file. Sections are already
naturally separable.

Canonical violation of AGENTS.md's "approaching ~1000 lines" threshold
("no 27k-line files" rule).

## Proposal

Split into submodules under `protocol/` with preserved reexports:

```text
crates/mu-core/src/protocol/
  mod.rs           // pub use { jsonrpc::*, session::*, ... }
  jsonrpc.rs       // base Request/Response/ErrorObject/JSONRPC_VERSION
  session.rs       // CreateSession / SessionList / SessionStats / etc.
  events.rs        // event log types, EventActor, EventPayload
  autonomy.rs      // StartAutonomousRequest, ScheduleWakeupRequest
  mailbox.rs       // MailboxPost/Consume/List/MessageView
  auth.rs          // AuthMechanism, AuthDenialCode, AuthOffer/Initiate/Response
  stats.rs         // Daemon/Session stats + usage history
```

`mod.rs` reexports everything so existing `use mu_core::protocol::{X};`
statements continue to work unchanged. Only the source file is
reorganized; the public API is identical.

## Acceptance

- protocol.rs replaced by protocol/ directory with submodules
- All existing `use mu_core::protocol::*` imports still compile
- All tests still pass
- `cargo clippy -D warnings` clean
- New protocol additions in future beads target the appropriate
  submodule, not a single growing file

## Why this matters

- Future protocol additions get targeted change-locality
- Code review and audit becomes per-domain, not per-monolith
- Merge conflict surface drops significantly

## Cross-refs

- AGENTS.md "no 27k-line files" rule (this is the canonical violation)
- gpt-5.5 code review of mu, daemon f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-15)
- Closely paired with dispatch.rs split bead (filed same session)
close_reason: All 6 phases merged (PRs #84, #85, #86, #87, #88, #89). protocol.rs split: 1940 → 657 LOC (-66%); 7 topic-bounded submodules, all under 360 LOC. AGENTS.md 'no 27k-line files' rule honored.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:

PR scoped down per tcovert's input ("we can scope it down... one function and test per PR or something. doesn't need to be a oneshot.").

This PR extracts the two cleanest-bounded sections:

- `protocol/auth.rs` — `AuthMechanism`, `AuthDenialCode`, `AuthOfferRequest/Response`, `AuthInitiateRequest`, `AuthResponseRequest`, `AuthExchangeResponse` (mu-7rk types)
- `protocol/mailbox.rs` — `PeerIdentity`, `PeerWant`, `PeerHelloRequest/Response`, `PeerOriginIdentity`, `MailboxPostRequest/Response`, `MailboxListRequest/Response`, `MailboxMessageView`, `MailboxConsumeRequest/Response`, `MailboxMessageEvent` (mu-lho types)

`protocol.rs` retains the `mod auth; mod mailbox; pub use auth::*; pub use mailbox::*;` re-exports so external callers see no API change. Tests stay in `protocol.rs` (use `super::*`, which still pulls the extracted items via re-exports).

Net: `protocol.rs` shrinks from 1940 → 1580 lines (still above the AGENTS.md ~1000-line threshold).

## Remaining phases (to follow in separate PRs)

title: bash strict-mode: relax metachar pre-filter for safe composition
description:
## Problem

`BashMode::Strict` rejects safe shell metacharacters at the pre-filter
stage, before the allowlist + approval layer is consulted. Affected:
`&&`, `||`, `;`, `|`, `2>&1`, `~`, `$VAR` expansions. Symptom from inside
the harness: even composing two allowlisted commands in sequence, or
expanding `~`, gets killed before either component is evaluated.

## Concrete examples observed (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

- `cd ~/.pi/agent && git branch -a` → "shell metacharacter '&' rejected"
- `git -C /home/$USER/.pi/agent branch -a` → "shell metacharacter '$' rejected"
- `git status 2>&1 | head -20` → "shell metacharacter '>' rejected"

## Proposal

Danger is in *what* gets composed, not in the operators themselves. If the
underlying commands are allowlist-approved, sequencing/piping/expanding is
still safe. Specifically allow:

- `&&`, `||`, `;` (sequencing — components still pass allowlist)
- `|` (piping — components still pass allowlist)
- `2>&1`, `2>/dev/null` (stderr handling)
- `~` (home-dir expansion)
- Curated `$VAR` set: `$HOME`, `$USER`, `$PWD`

Still reject: subshells `$(...)` and backticks, background `&` solo,
arbitrary-path redirections (`> /etc/foo`), process substitution
(`<(...)`, `>(...)`).

## Acceptance

- Allowlisted commands can be sequenced/piped without metachar rejection
- `~` and curated $VAR set expand without rejection
- Subshells, arbitrary file redirection, process substitution still rejected
- Test: the three examples above pass; `ls $(rm -rf /tmp/test)` still rejected

## Cross-refs

- Distinct from mu-20l (different layer of the same module — that's the
  prompt-firing path, this is the metachar pre-filter path)
- Provenance: mu daemon f3845422 session-2 (2026-05-15), Mu-self feedback
  on harness ergonomics

title: agent-spawn-v2: no post-run audit of injected context manifest
description:
Observed during 2026-05-16 validation session (Sprint 1 slice 1).

## What

agent-spawn-v2 builds a context manifest tempfile (/tmp/agent-spawn-manifest.XXXXXX), stages it into the pot, then deletes it in the release_slot trap. After the run, there is no record of what context was injected.

## Why this matters for telemetry (mu-fvy0 cluster)

The telemetry sink (mu-5g7i) and outcome classifier (mu-8alb) are designed to reason about why a spawn produced the outcome it did. 'Context-poisoning' is one suspected failure mode (e.g., manifest contained a stale rule that pushed the agent off-task). Without an audit trail of the actual injected manifest, post-hoc classification can't distinguish 'agent failed because of bad context' from 'agent failed because of bad prompt' from 'agent failed because of model limits'.

## Empirical evidence

Tier-3 validation showed input tokens jumped from 2398 (no context) to 20001 (with manifest), i.e. ~17.6k tokens of injected context. After the run, that 17.6k of context is gone — no way to inspect what it was unless the c137-memory-worker request can be replayed exactly (and even then, the worker's response can change between calls if memory has been added/edited).

## Minimal fix sketch

Add an env knob AGENT_SPAWN_MANIFEST_LOG_DIR (default unset/disabled). When set, copy the final scrubbed manifest to <dir>/<task_id>-<slot>-<lease>.txt before the rm in release_slot. Telemetry sink then reads these as one of its inputs.

## Acceptance

- env knob in agent-spawn-v2
- when set, manifest persisted per-task before cleanup
- no behavior change when unset (default)

## Blocks

Likely blocks mu-5g7i (telemetry envelope construction) for the 'context_manifest_ref' field. Cross-reference at telemetry design time.
close_reason: Implemented 2026-05-16 in ~/.local/bin/agent-spawn-v2 (backup at .pre-6dyg-2026-05-16). Adds AGENT_SPAWN_MANIFEST_LOG_DIR env knob; when set, preserves the scrubbed manifest with provenance header to <dir>/<task_id>-<slot>-<lease>.txt before per-run cleanup. Tested negative (unset = no behavior change) and positive (72k manifest captured cleanly with all provenance metadata). No-op when --no-context (no manifest to preserve). Ready as input for the mu-5g7i telemetry envelope.
