Six clippy lints in mu-coding, all with clear mechanical fixes that clippy itself suggests (`try this:` blocks). None involve design decisions; this is straight refactoring to the suggested forms.

## Items

1. `sort_by_key` (×2 locations) — replace `sort_by(|a, b| a.field.cmp(&b.field))` with `sort_by_key(|x| x.field)`.
2. `clamp_like_pattern` (×1) — replace `x.max(lo).min(hi)` with `x.clamp(lo, hi)`.
3. `wildcard_in_or_patterns` (×2) — replace `Foo | _` with just `_` (wildcard already covers the named variant).
4. `obfuscated_if_else` (×1) — `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/grep.rs:293` — replace `x.then_some("a").unwrap_or("b")` chain with `if x { "a" } else { "b" }`.

Exact locations are emitted by `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features`; suggestion blocks in each error message are drop-in.

## Acceptance

- All 6 mechanical lints resolved per clippy's suggested forms
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints) — overall clippy still fails on the other cleanup beads' items
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision qppzqwyp. Six mechanical lints applied via clippy's suggested forms (sort_by_key x2, clamp, wildcard x2, if-else). 361 tests green; remaining warnings 20 → 10 (mu-lm8 + mu-j97).

title: mu admin import-claude-history — convert claude-code JSONL transcripts into mu event log + provider-agnostic event schema extension
description:
## Two-part scope

This is one bead because the parts are tightly coupled. You can't faithfully import claude-code's transcripts without somewhere to put the richer event data; the schema extension IS the prerequisite for honest import.

### Part 1: importer

`mu admin import-claude-history <path>` subcommand (or standalone script if a subcommand surface change is too invasive for v1). For each claude-code JSONL transcript file under the input path, emit a mu-format JSONL at `~/.local/share/mu/events/<imported_daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl`.

Mapping (verified by reading actual claude-code JSONL on 2026-05-19):

| claude-code JSONL | mu EventPayload | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| `type=user` | `user_message` | direct 1:1 |
| `type=assistant` text/thinking content | `assistant_message_event` + `assistant_thinking` (new — see Part 2) | split content array |
| Assistant `content[N].type=tool_use` | `tool_call` | extracted out of the assistant message; assistant remains for the text portions |
| `type=tool_result` | `tool_result` | direct 1:1 |
| `stop_reason` on terminal assistant | `done` (synthetic) | computed at turn boundary |
| `usage` on assistant | partial `task_telemetry` | token + cache counts map directly |
| `permission-mode`, `bridge-session`, `system`, `task_reminder`, `hook_*`, etc. | drop or emit as `audit_event` variant (Part 2) | UI chrome; not session-data |
| `edited_text_file` | enrich `tool_result` for Edit calls, OR emit as `file_edit_record` (Part 2) | claude-code records per-file edit metadata; mu currently doesn't |

Session-id mapping: claude-code UUID → mu display name `claude-<uuid-prefix-8>` so they're distinguishable from native mu sessions in F2/F3 listings.

Model attribution: claude-code records the model name (e.g., `claude-sonnet-4-6`); imported sessions get `provider=anthropic_api, model=<recorded>`.

Timestamp: ISO → `timestamp_unix_ms u64` conversion.

### Part 2: event-schema extension (provider-agnostic)

The import surfaces what mu's EventPayload variants are missing today. Adding these variants makes mu's event log a SUPERSET that can faithfully represent any provider's session — including future codex/gpt JSONL imports.

New variants (proposed):

- **`assistant_thinking`** — extended-thinking blocks (claude-code's `content[i].type=thinking`). Includes the thinking text + signature. Mu's loop today drops thinking before emit; this variant preserves the data when it's there.
- **`audit_event`** — generic for chrome events (permission mode changes, bridge-session linkage, queue operations, etc.). Tagged with `subtype` for filtering. Lets imports preserve everything without inventing one variant per claude-code event type.
- **`file_edit_record`** — per-file edit metadata (path, before/after hash, line count delta). claude-code already emits this; mu could too on its Edit tool.
- **`parent_event_id`** field on existing variants (optional) — preserve claude-code's parentUuid DAG structure for forensic walk-back. mu's events are linear today; this is metadata only, doesn't change replay logic.

Once these exist, the import becomes lossless: every claude-code event has a target.

## Why now-ish (P3, not P2)

- Operator value is large: every past claude-code session becomes a queryable historical mu session. Existing operator has ~6+ months of conversations.
- Importing claude-code first informs the codex / gpt importer second — same shape, different source JSONL.
- The schema extension makes future providers (Gemini, etc.) plug in more naturally — mu becomes the convergence-format for agentic session data.

## Dependencies

- **mu-u1ld phase A** (shipped — PR #90): the read-only rehydration pickup that makes imported sessions appear in `session.list` automatically. Imports place files in `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/` and next `mu serve` start picks them up.
- **mu-1flg** (open — phase C writable rehydration): the natural upgrade path so imported sessions can be RESUMED, not just viewed.
- **mu-w73n** (open — `session.delete_persistent`): paired affordance so imported sessions can be retired when they age out of relevance.
- **mu-lcf7** (open — paused-vs-closed distinction via timestamp): timestamps on imported events let the UI surface 'last active 3 weeks ago' instead of just 'done' for stale imports.

## Out of scope

- Bidirectional sync (writing changes back to claude-code's storage). One-way import only.
- Live-tailing claude-code's JSONL as it writes. Initial v1 is batch-only.
- Image content / file attachments in messages. Drop or stash separately.
- Tool name normalization across mu/claude-code/codex (claude-code's `Task`, `Read`, `Edit`, etc. → mu's tool ids). Pass through as-is initially; standardize later.

## Acceptance

- `mu admin import-claude-history <path>` (or equivalent script) reads claude-code JSONL files and emits valid mu event JSONL
- All four (or however many we land) new `EventPayload` variants exist with serde-defaulted fields so older code doesn't break
- `Outcome::Unclassified` doesn't fire for any imported session whose claude-code session was `stop_reason=end_turn` (forensics regression test)
- TUI F3 renders an imported session's transcript using the existing per-turn loops (you/assistant/tool: ...) — operator can browse historical conversations
- F8 events explorer can filter imported events by their new variants

## Provenance

Operator 2026-05-19 ~03:15 EDT: 'Do those entries provide enough information that we could convert them into our eventlog?' + 'extra information like the thinking sections will also be useful to make our events more robust across different providers.' The latter half is the key insight — import isn't just a viewer for claude-code history; it's a forcing function that makes mu's event schema honest about the full agentic-session domain.

Initial investigation done in conversation: claude-code JSONL has 19 distinct `type` values; structural mapping covers all of them; richer-than-mu fields (thinking, edited_text_file) become new mu variants. The shape works.

title: Authority-coloring as refinement on top of state-coloring (mu-pex)
description:
Refines mu-pex's per-session state-coloring model with an authority/role layer. Memory `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 → mu mapping) and memory `5ff7511e` (color palette stays coarse — ~3 colors, never more than 5).

## Motivation

mu-pex's state-coloring distinguishes session states (idle / streaming / tool-running / done). claude-code 2.1.139's role/tier concept layers on top: which AUTHORITY does a session carry (root capability, attenuated user capability, delegate capability)? These two axes are independent — a streaming root-authority session looks different from a streaming attenuated-delegate session.

## Scope

In:
- Authority tier enum (Root / User / Delegate / Restricted). 3–4 tiers max — coarse per `5ff7511e`.
- Tier rendered as a coloring overlay (border color, label) ON TOP of the state coloring. Two visual axes: state (fill/animation) + authority (border/label).
- Wiring: SessionStatusSummary gains `authority_tier` derived from `Capability`. TUI list view picks up the tier.

Out:
- Authority tier transitions (delegation already handles this).
- Beyond-coarse palettes (memory `5ff7511e` caps at ~5 colors).

## Acceptance

* `Capability` → `AuthorityTier` projection function with unit tests.
* TUI session list shows authority tier visually distinct from state.
* No more than 4 authority tiers; rationale documented.

## References

- Refines: mu-pex
- Memory: `fb5cd5be` (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping), `5ff7511e` (coarse palette)

title: agent/loop_/mod.rs autonomous-mode arm: clean up dead let bindings or comment intent
TUI: expose provider/model selection in 'n' new-session flow
description:
## Why

Sessions in mu's architecture are independent — each carries its own ProviderSelector at create_session time. So having multiple concurrent sessions in one daemon, each with a different provider+model, is already supported wire-side. But the TUI's 'n' (new session) keybind always uses self.default_provider, which is a single tuple set at startup.

Net effect: a feature mu already supports is locked behind a CLI flag (--provider / --model on mu-tui launch) instead of being an in-TUI affordance.

## What

Options, in increasing scope:

A. Palette commands `:provider <kind>` and `:model <id>` that update default_provider for the next 'n' press. Smallest change; trades discoverability for terseness. ~30 LOC.

B. Two-step 'n' flow: 'n' opens a small picker (provider on top, model below, both with keyboard navigation), then create_session fires on Enter. ~80-150 LOC depending on picker polish.

C. Both: palette for keyboard-fast use; picker for discoverability.

Recommend (A) first — it gets the feature usable in ~30 LOC; (B) can layer on later when the rest of the TUI gets palette-/picker-friendlier.

## Acceptance

- Running mu-tui without --provider/--model, then issuing :provider openai_codex / :model gpt-5.5 in the palette, then pressing 'n', creates a session against openai_codex/gpt-5.5.
- Existing default-provider behavior preserved when no palette command is issued.
- Session list (F2 when it lands, or the session row in F1) shows which provider+model each session ran against.

## References

- Default flow today: crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs::App::create_session uses self.default_provider.
- ProviderSelector wire type: crates/mu-core/src/protocol.rs::ProviderSelector.
close_reason: done

title: Implement mu-035: session.provider_status + cancel_outstanding
description:
Spec at specs/mu-035-session-provider-status.md (jj spouxlsp d69719c3).

Three deliverables:
1. session.provider_status periodic notification with kind (awaiting_first_token | streaming | thinking | tool_executing | awaiting_tool_result | idle), started_at_unix_ms, elapsed_ms, optional bytes_received / tool_call_id.
2. session.cancel_outstanding narrow cancel (kills provider call, session stays alive).
3. daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view.

INV-4 is load-bearing: the emit-tick runs on a tokio timer independent of the provider future, so a stalled provider still produces visible status.

Once landed:
- TUI's phase line gets authoritative ground truth (no more session.list-derived heuristics)
- mu_client.py defensive layer becomes belt-and-suspenders
- Refinement experiment's silent stalls become instantly diagnosable

Estimated 1 week of focused Rust work.
close_reason: Phase D landed: ProviderStatusTracker promoted into Sessions registry; daemon.outstanding_calls dispatch handler added; session.cancel_outstanding now reads live tracker for was_in.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Phase A landed in jj okqunkry ef544959 — emits ProviderStatus on state transitions (AwaitingFirstToken / Streaming / ToolExecuting). Wire types for cancel_outstanding + daemon.outstanding_calls also added. Remaining: Phase B periodic timer (INV-4 — load-bearing), Phase C session.cancel_outstanding RPC handler, Phase D daemon.outstanding_calls handler.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Phase B landed in jj yznoysqw 28ace52b — periodic emit every 1000ms via tokio::time::interval, INV-4 (load-bearing) now actually holds. TUI's phase line can now render 'awaiting first token 4.2s' or 'tool: read 8.1s' with growing elapsed_ms. Phase C (session.cancel_outstanding RPC handler) and Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls) still to go.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Phase C landed in jj lmoxwqvs 7733782d — session.cancel_outstanding RPC. AgentInput::CancelOutstanding + Outcome::OutstandingCancelled sentinel; dispatch handler in handle_cancel_outstanding; abort propagates through stream/tool/approval recv sites; outer loop catches and emits Done(Aborted), keeps session alive. was_in field is placeholder until Phase D plumbs the live tracker.

Phase D (daemon.outstanding_calls fleet view) is the last remaining sub-issue — requires promoting the per-session ProviderStatusTracker state into the Sessions registry so dispatch can read it without going through the agent loop's stack frame.

title: AskSessionRequest: per-ask provider/model override
description:
## Why

Today, a session's (provider, model) is fixed at create_session time. To use a different model for one ask — e.g., "use haiku for this cheap subtask in the middle of a sonnet session" — there's no path short of creating a new session (losing context).

Surfaced 2026-05-11 during a multi-iteration discussion about model routing. The agent-router script (~/src/claude-personal/scripts/agent-router) already does this externally; doing it natively in mu means a session can mix model choices without going through a wrapper.

## What

Add an optional `provider_override` field to AskSessionRequest. When present, the agent loop builds the provider for THIS ask using the override instead of the session's stored provider, but doesn't persist it — the session keeps its original (provider, model). Subsequent asks use the session default again unless overridden.

The 'persistent swap' variant is its own bead (see mu-??? session.set_provider) — this one is per-call only.

## Wire shape (proposal)

```json
{
  "session_id": "session-1",
  "user_message": "...",
  "provider_override": { "kind": "anthropic_api", "model": "claude-haiku-4-5-20251001" }
}
```

## Considerations

- Cost: opens the door to 'route each ask to the cheapest viable model' patterns. This is what the agent-router script does today externally.
- Capability surface: must validate that the override provider supports the tools the session has bound; otherwise error early.
- Cache impact: overriding the model means the previously-cached prefix (system prompt + tools) becomes a cache miss on the override target. The dashboard's cache% column will reflect this naturally.

## Acceptance

- Unit test: AskSessionRequest with provider_override hits the override provider, not the session's stored one.
- The override doesn't mutate session state — subsequent asks without override use the session's original (provider, model).
- F5 dashboard shows the override as its own (provider, model) row (since the daemon.usage_history aggregator groups by provider_kind + model on each call, this should fall out naturally).

title: mu-core: emit approval-lifecycle events (input_required_emitted, input_required_responded) for audit-trail completeness
description:
## Problem

mu's bash tool with `--bash-prompt` mode triggers an approval modal via
`session.input_required` notifications. The user clicks approve/deny in
the TUI. The notification → approve → tool-run → tool_result chain is
real, but the only persistent trace in the per-session JSONL is:

- The `tool_call` event (request made by agent)
- The `tool_result` event (final outcome)

The intermediate `input_required` notification, the wait for user
response, and the user's decision (approve / deny / reason) are NOT
recorded as discrete events. For denials, the tool_result content
includes the text `tool denied by user via session.respond_to_input_required`
— that's the only fingerprint. For approvals, there's no fingerprint
at all; the approval is invisible in the persistent log.

This was discovered 2026-05-16 when debugging daemon f3845422 session-9:
answering "did popups fire?" required reconstructing from timing gaps
(800ms–47000ms between tool_call and tool_result on bash invocations)
and careful inspection of error text, because the events themselves
don't record the approval lifecycle.

## Proposal

Add two new event kinds to mu's persistent event log:

1. **`input_required_emitted`** — fires when daemon sends a
   `session.input_required` notification. Records:
   - request_id (for correlation with the response)
   - target tool name + arguments (what's being approved)
   - rationale (why approval is required: `--bash-prompt` mode,
     capability check, etc.)
   - timestamp

2. **`input_required_responded`** — fires when daemon receives the
   client's `session.respond_to_input_required` RPC. Records:
   - request_id (correlates with the emitted event)
   - decision: `Approve` | `Deny`
   - decided_by: `user` | `timeout` | `auto-policy` (if mu ever adds
     auto-approval)
   - reason (optional, for denials with explanation)
   - timestamp

Both events appear in the per-session JSONL the same way other events
do, with the standard `id` / `session_id` / `timestamp_unix_ms` /
`actor` / `payload` envelope.

## Acceptance

- Approval modal firings are visible in the persistent event log
- Approve and deny decisions are both visible (not just denials via
  tool_result text)
- A query like "how many bash invocations were approved vs denied vs
  auto-rejected for this session?" is answerable from the event log
  alone
- Existing tool_call/tool_result events still work as before
- Timing analysis is no longer the only way to detect approval-modal
  firings

## Why this matters

mu's design philosophy is "observability as product feature" (per
gpt's code review). Silent approval flows contradict that.

With the events recorded, automated post-hoc analysis (e.g., "this
model hits 5x more approval prompts than that model in the same
daemon") becomes a one-query observation rather than the timing-
archaeology exercise it currently is.

## Cross-refs

- Source incident: session-9 in daemon f3845422 (2026-05-16), where
  Claude's bash-heavy strategy triggered numerous popups but the
  approval lifecycle was invisible in the JSONL
- mu-gih (modal approval dialog implementation — this bead adds the
  audit side of what mu-gih landed the UX side of)
- mu-6hl (bash policy legibility — companion observability work)
- mu-pex (metrics framework — approval rates feed into quality proxies)
- Provenance: live debugging of TUI session asymmetry, 2026-05-16

title: mu-clip-coding-too-many-args: 3 functions in mu-coding with 9-10 args (clippy::too_many_arguments)
description:
Three functions in mu-coding trigger clippy::too_many_arguments (signatures with 9-10 args; clippy's default threshold is 7). These are signature-design decisions, not mechanical fixes.

## Items (exact locations from clippy)

- 1× function with 10/7 args
- 2× functions with 9/7 args

(Specific locations + names in clippy output; not pre-listed here because the decision per-function may differ — bundle into a config struct, builder pattern, or just `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.)

## Per-function decision space

For each: choose one of —

A. **Bundle into a config struct.** Most common for ergonomic call sites. Adds one struct + Default. Best when args naturally group (e.g. "render options" struct, "server config" struct).
B. **Builder pattern.** When some args are optional and call sites vary. More boilerplate; usually overkill for 3-arg overflow.
C. **`#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` with rationale.** When refactoring would harm clarity at call sites (e.g. internal dispatch fns where every arg is mandatory + unrelated). The allow must include a one-line comment naming why.

## Acceptance

- Each of the 3 functions has either: (a) refactored to ≤7 args via struct/builder, OR (b) explicit `#[allow]` with rationale comment
- Decision rationale documented in commit body per-function
- cargo clippy passes (for these specific lints)
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead

## Why this bead is higher-judgment than the mechanical one

Signature changes ripple to every call site. If we go option A or B, we must update all callers. If option C, we admit the function has irreducible complexity. The right answer per function depends on call-site usage patterns — worth a quick survey of each function's callers before deciding.
close_reason: Landed in jj revision omulvzmu. Three config structs (AskOptions, NewSession, BuildSessionRequest<'_>) replace 9-10 arg signatures. 7 call sites updated. Workspace clippy -D warnings + fmt --check + 361 tests all green. CI gate should now pass on the next push.

title: F5 dashboard: add tool_total_ms p95 column
description:
## Why

The mu-pex Phase 1 RPC (`daemon.usage_history`) returns `tool_total_ms` (per-(ToolCall, ToolResult) pair durations, aggregated as median + p95) in every row, but the F5 dashboard's Phase A renderer (mu-xln, commit yqoktozo 0d3a3f23) omits this column. Discovered 2026-05-11 when a 29s/14-tool ask showed 17s of streaming + 116 output tokens — most of the wall was tool execution, and the dashboard couldn't show it.

"Where is the wall time going?" is one of the canonical metrics-dashboard questions. tool_total_ms is the answer for tool-heavy workloads and TTFT / streaming together are the answer for prose-heavy ones. Without the tool column, the dashboard only answers half the question.

## What

In crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs render_usage:

1. Add a 'tool p95' column header between 'wall p95' and 'in tok'.
2. Pull row["tool_total_ms"]["p95"] and format via the existing fmt_ms_p95 helper.
3. Update the widths array to include the new column (8 char width fits 'XXXXms' + spacing).
4. Verify the table still fits in a standard 120-col terminal (current rendered width is ~106 chars + 2 borders; adding 8+1 = ~115, fits).

## Acceptance

- F5 pane shows a 'tool p95' column.
- Empty rows (no tool calls) render '—' or '0ms' via fmt_ms_p95 (it returns '—' on None).
- No layout regression on narrow terminals (test at 80, 100, 120 cols).

## Note on column ordering

Current order favors timing-decomposition reading left to right: ttft p95 → stream p95 → wall p95. Inserting tool p95 between stream and wall preserves that 'what happened during the wall' progression: AwaitingFirstToken time → streaming time → tool time → total wall.
close_reason: done

title: mu-tui: view for associated claude sessions (via operator/mailbox/etcd)
description:
## Problem

mu can observe its own sessions but doesn't surface associated claude-code
sessions (the supervisor/operator/orchestrator pattern from the 3-layer
hierarchy, memory 64b4a3a6). When mu is the substrate for claude
orchestration, the user should see what associated claude sessions are
doing without leaving the mu TUI.

## Proposal

Add a view (F-key panel or extension of F2/F3) showing associated claude
sessions via one or more channels:

- Direct relationship via mu-as-supervisor-of-claude (operator pattern)
- Via mailbox messages (mu-lho phase 1 + planned mu-71q MCP adapter)
- Via etcd-tracked tasks (if orchestrator-mcp + claude-proxy wired)

Display: session ID, current status, last activity, brief task description.

## Acceptance

- Panel/view lists known associated claude sessions
- Data refreshed via whichever channel(s) are wired
- Selecting one shows enough detail to know what it's doing
- No need to leave the TUI

## Cross-refs

- mu-ib5 (F9 mailbox view — mailbox channel discovery overlaps)
- mu-cha (F2 session tree — could be extended to show external sessions)
- mu-71q (MCP adapter)
- memory 64b4a3a6 (3-layer orchestration pattern this surfaces)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: task_id correlation field on intermediate event payloads
description:
Stamp `task_id` (the same UUID-like identifier carried in `EventPayload::TaskTelemetry`) on every intermediate event emitted during a task: `ToolCall`, `ToolResult`, `ContextAssembly`, `AssistantMessageEvent`, `ProviderStatusUpdate`, `Callout`.

## Why

Operator observation 2026-05-16 after mu-5g7i landed: 'if a session was task-oriented and working on multiple simultaneous tasks, associating a task identifier with each operation would allow for other accounting.'

Today's events carry `session_id` + monotonic `event_id` only. Per-task attribution requires walking forward from a `UserMessage` to the next `Done` and gathering everything in between. Two consequences:

1. **Classifier work (mu-8alb)** has to do that reconstruction walk for every classification. Cheap in isolation, but every analytics query that wants per-task aggregation pays the same cost.
2. **Future intra-session concurrency** (parallel tool calls, mailbox-driven side-tasks, autonomous-loop concurrent iterations) breaks the walk-based reconstruction entirely — interleaved events become indistinguishable from one task with weird timing.

## Today's concurrency model (for context)

mu sessions are sequential today: `ask_session` blocks until Done, mailbox messages process in normal turn cycle, autonomous loops iterate sequentially. So the reconstruction walk works correctly NOW. The argument for adding task_id is forward-looking + ergonomic, not bug-fixing.

## Design sketch

- Forwarder tracks 'current task_id' between `UserMessage` and the matching `Done` / `Error`. The same id ultimately appears in `EventPayload::TaskTelemetry.task_id` at termination.
- Each `to_log_event` projection injects the current task_id into the payload.
- Backwards-compat: serde with `#[serde(default)]` so older log entries deserialize as `task_id: None` (or a sentinel). Classifier code can treat None as 'pre-correlation-id era' and fall back to walk-based reconstruction.

## Scope of changes

- `EventPayload` variants gain a new `task_id: Option<String>` field (or wrap in a common envelope; latter is cleaner but more invasive). Touching every variant is small mechanical work.
- Forwarder gains task-id tracking state (a single `Option<String>` field per session).
- AgentLoop doesn't change (forwarder injects).
- mu-8alb (classifier) can be simplified once this lands — direct query instead of reconstruction.

## Acceptance

- New field defined and serde-compat (with default for old logs).
- Forwarder populates it for every event emitted between UserMessage and the matching Done/Error.
- Tests verify task_id consistency across a multi-event task.
- Classifier (mu-8alb) opportunistically reads it when present; falls back to walk otherwise.

## Cross-references

- mu-5g7i (TaskTelemetry envelope, where task_id is defined at task termination)
- mu-8alb (classifier — currently does reconstruction walk; would benefit from this)
- mu-8ypx (analytics sink — same)
- mu-mk9l (backfill — would set task_id on synthetic entries)

title: mu-ci: add GitHub Actions CI workflow (fmt + clippy -D warnings + test) and clean pre-existing warnings
description:
Add v1 CI workflow at .github/workflows/ci.yml gating PRs and pushes-to-main on three jobs:
- cargo fmt --all -- --check
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings

Atomic with cleanup of pre-existing warnings surfaced today (event_log.rs:545 unused ToolCall import; mu-tui dead-code warnings in main.rs/mu_client.rs/mod.rs/serve_smoke.rs) so the first CI run is green.

## Scope

IN:

OUT:
- macOS / FreeBSD matrix (Linux only for v1; FreeBSD verified locally)
- cargo-audit / cargo-deny (separate beads if/when wanted)

## Acceptance

close_reason: Landed in jj revision nxuowqor. .github/workflows/ci.yml + cargo fmt + cargo clippy --fix + manual Usage::Add impl + doc comment reword. CI gate active with -D warnings; failure expected until cleanup beads (mu-clip-*) close. Test suite unchanged at 361 green.

title: mu-agent-loop: cap consecutive tool-not-found responses (force end_turn after N=3 default)
description:
## Problem

The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs` has no termination guard for repeated `"tool not found"` responses. When a model emits `stop_reason: "tool_use"` for a tool that mu hasn't registered, the dispatcher returns a tool_result with error message `"tool not found: <name>"`, and the loop continues. Some models will then try a different (also-nonexistent) tool name, and the loop spins until the model gives up on its own.

This is the same loop observed in mu-q3v6's validation reproducer: 6 consecutive tool_use turns chasing hallucinated tools before Gemini-3.5-flash finally emitted end_turn. Each round burned ~30k prompt tokens.

## Why a defensive guard even after the sibling bead lands

Sibling bead [the no-tools-clause one] addresses the root cause for the `tool_count == 0` case. But:

1. Some providers/models may emit tool_use even when explicitly told not to (prompt non-adherence is not zero).
2. The same pattern can arise with `tool_count > 0` if the model hallucinates a tool name that isn't in the registered set. Today: a session with one registered tool (e.g. `bash`) and a model that emits `execute_bash` will get "tool not found" → loop continues.
3. Even with a clean system prompt, providers under load may produce degraded outputs that retry-loop.

A defensive cap is a belt-and-suspenders safety net. It says: "regardless of what the system prompt said, if the model has emitted N consecutive tool_calls that don't resolve, force end_turn."

## Proposal

In the agent loop, track consecutive `"tool not found"` responses per session. After **3 consecutive** unresolved tool_call attempts:

1. Synthesize a final tool_result that says: "You have attempted to call 3 tools that do not exist in this session: {list of attempted names}. Respond to the user without tool calls."
2. Force the next provider call to use a synthesized `stop_reason: "end_turn"` constraint (if the provider supports it) OR detect end_turn in the model's next response and break the loop.
3. Emit an `AgentEvent::ToolNotFoundLoopAborted` event so observability captures this happened.
4. Telemetry (`task_telemetry`) gains a `tool_not_found_loop_aborted: bool` field so post-hoc analysis can count occurrences.

## Acceptance

- Reproducer: with the no-tools-clause from sibling bead REVERTED (so the model still tries to call tools), a `mu ask` against a known-hallucinator model breaks out of the loop after 3 attempts instead of going to 6+.
- `AgentEvent::ToolNotFoundLoopAborted` is emitted at the abort point with the list of attempted tool names.
- `task_telemetry.tool_not_found_loop_aborted` is `true` for the aborted session.
- Sessions with legitimate tool use (registered tools, valid calls) continue to work normally.
- Configurable threshold (`MU_TOOL_NOT_FOUND_LIMIT` env var or daemon config; default 3) so operators can tune.

## Out of scope

- Heuristics that try to map hallucinated tool names to registered ones (e.g. fuzzy-match `execute_bash` → `bash`). Tempting but introduces a different class of bug; leave for a future bead if the data justifies.

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-22 by tcovert. Sibling to bead [the no-tools-clause one]. Both surfaced from mu-q3v6's post-merge validation reproducer (daemon f7b1715a207c7d97, session-1).

title: aws-recon tool skeleton: fixture-only aws_recon tool implementing mu-039 pre-dispatch path
description:
First implementation slice of the mu-039 aws-recon skill (spec at `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md`). Tool-only; no skill-activation wiring yet. No live AWS, no runner subprocess.

## Why this slice (not the whole skill)

mu-039 is large: skill activation, three tool surfaces (`aws_recon`, `aws_plan_from_recon`, `aws_cloudtrail_session_events`), CloudTrail audit-join metadata, runner version capture. Doing it as one bead is a $40+ /goal and crosses too many concerns. This slice exercises the dispatch-gate landed in mu-stw end-to-end on the simplest surface (`aws_recon`) while keeping the boundary that mu-core/mu-coding never reads live AWS credentials.

## Scope

### In

- New tool `aws_recon` registered in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`.
- `ToolPolicy::required_aws_capability = Some("aws.scout.readonly".into())` so the mu-stw gate refuses dispatch when the session lacks the grant.
- Input schema (Tool::spec()): `{ capability: String, call_timeout_secs: u32, output_dir: Option<String> }` with defaults consistent with the spec (`specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md:154-162`).
- Pre-dispatch input validation:
  1. session capability contains AwsCapability with matching name (already enforced by mu-stw; this is belt-and-suspenders / better error-shape);
  2. `AwsCapabilityCatalog::resolve_materialized()` succeeds for `input.capability`;
  3. catalog entry has `mutation_allowed == false`.
  Fail-closed: any check failure → `Tool::execute` returns a structured refusal, no runner invocation, audit-shape event emitted.
- **Dry-run/fixture execution mode**: tool reads `MU_AWS_RECON_FIXTURE_DIR` env var. If set, tool returns a stubbed `ReconReport` (artifact path = fixture path); if unset, tool returns `not-yet-implemented` refusal with a structured error. This proves the dispatch path + catalog resolution without standing up the runner.
- Test cases:
  - dispatch refused when session lacks `aws.scout.readonly` (already tested via mu-stw; mirror here at tool granularity);
  - catalog miss → structured error;
  - `mutation_allowed: true` entry → structured error;
  - fixture-mode happy path → ReconReport returned with expected shape;
  - fixture-unset → not-yet-implemented refusal (this branch will be replaced when runner wiring lands).
- Audit metadata captured on every dispatch (success or refusal): mu session id, capability name, catalog digest, local AWS profile name from catalog, role ARN from catalog. No STS metadata yet (fixture mode has none).

### Out

- Skill activation surface (the `SkillActivation`/`ToolSchema` spans that the full mu-039 spec produces). Tool stands alone; operator manually grants `aws.scout.readonly` for tests.
- The other two mu-039 tools (`aws_plan_from_recon`, `aws_cloudtrail_session_events`) — each gets its own bead.
- Actually invoking `scripts/mu-aws-capability-run.sh` + `scripts/aws-recon.sh`. Runner wiring is a follow-on bead so the boundary is clean.
- STS / CloudTrail audit-join. Audit-shape struct lands here but the optional fields stay None in fixture mode.
- biscuit-auth, cross-daemon attenuation.

## Acceptance

- New tool registered; mu-coding exposes it.
- All test cases above pass.
- `cargo test --workspace` green.
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green.
- `cargo fmt --check` green.
- Commit cites `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md:<lines>` for the spec references it implements.

## Design notes (decide at impl time)

- Where does the catalog live? mu-core owns the types (mu-ysh). Tool needs an instance. Options: (a) operator pre-loads via env var path; (b) tool takes a catalog handle injected at registration time. Lean (b) so tests can inject in-memory catalogs; env-var path resolution is the operator-facing default that produces a catalog handle at startup.
- ReconReport shape: minimal v1 = `{ artifact_dir: PathBuf, catalog_digest: String, capability: String }`. Real fields land when runner wiring lands.
- Error type: re-use the structured error shape from mu-stw's dispatch refusal so the audit fields are uniform across pre-dispatch refusal and Tool::execute refusal.

## Why this matters

mu-stw landed the dispatch gate but nothing exercises it yet. This bead is the smallest concrete tool that proves: capability gate fires correctly, catalog resolution path works, fixture-mode lets us iterate on tool ergonomics + audit shape without touching live AWS. After this lands, mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption) + the runner-wiring bead can compose independently.

## References

- Spec: `specs/mu-039-aws-recon-skill.md`
- Dispatch gate: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` (mu-stw, PR #8)
- Capability catalog: `crates/mu-core/src/aws.rs` (mu-ysh, PR #2)
- Tool surface convention: existing tools in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`
close_reason: Merged in PR #10 as 3aec957

title: CompactionPolicy trait — pluggable context-compaction strategies, benchmarkable
description:
Add a `CompactionPolicy` trait as a sibling to `CacheStrategy` and `ProviderRenderer`. Per-session config picks the policy. The rope substrate (post-mu-fb0) is the substrate it operates over.

## Why

Claude Code's compaction on 1M-context Opus 4.7 takes ~5 minutes — a full-context model pass to re-summarize the conversation. mu can do better because:

1. The typed event log lets compaction reason about WHAT to compact at the structural level (span families: file-loads where files have changed, tool-call clusters whose results already landed in the rope, expired skill activations) BEFORE any model call.
2. Compaction creates a natural new stable prefix → composes with `CacheStrategy` so the next ask pays one cache-creation cost then enjoys cache hits. Claude Code's "rewrite transcript" path always invalidates cache.
3. As a separate POLICY (not baked into the loop), strategies can be swapped/tested/benchmarked independently. Same architectural pattern as mu-ktq's `CacheStrategy`.

Operator (tcovert) note: "I don't claim to be an expert on compaction correctness; now that we have the primitives we can take research literature or our own testing and try to optimize."

## Scope

### In

- New `CompactionPolicy` trait in `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction.rs`.
- Trait shape (proposed): `fn compact(&self, rope: &RetainedRope, target_tokens: usize) -> CompactionResult`. `CompactionResult` carries: new rope, decision log (which spans dropped/merged/summarized), token-savings estimate, optional model-call required (e.g. for natural-language summarization).
- Provider trait method (analogous to `cache_strategy()`): `fn provider.compaction_policy() -> Arc<dyn CompactionPolicy>` so providers can declare their preferred default.
- At least one concrete policy: `NoCompactionPolicy` (no-op, the v1 default — preserves current behavior).
- At least one heuristic policy (probably `SpanFamilyDropPolicy`): drops/summarizes by span-kind priority (skill-activations > recent tool-results > stale file-loads > old assistant turns), zero model calls.
- Benchmark harness: take a corpus of stored ropes (could seed from mu-upb's per-session JSONLs), apply each policy, record (input_tokens, output_tokens, time_ms, optional model_calls, retention_metrics).
- Integration with the agent loop: trigger compaction when input_tokens crosses a per-session threshold (configurable; sensible default = 70% of model's context window).

### Out

- The intelligent-summarization model-call policy. Whether to invoke a cheap model to compress narrative spans is its own bead — `mu-compaction-llm-summary` once research informs design.
- Streaming compaction (compact during streaming rather than between turns).
- Persisting compacted ropes to disk (separate concern from compute path).
- Quality-grader infrastructure — that's its own bead but mu-pex (metrics framework) is the right home; this bead notes the dependency.

## Design questions to resolve (at impl time)

1. **Compaction trigger**: per-turn check, or only when token threshold crossed? Lean: threshold-crossed, since per-turn checks add latency.
2. **In-place vs. produce-new-rope**: should `compact()` mutate the rope or return a new one? Lean: new (immutable substrate per the architecture's design).
3. **Decision-log shape**: which spans were dropped + reasons. Operator-view consumes this for "what did we lose?" visibility. Lean: structured `Vec<CompactionDecision>` where each decision is `{ span_id, action: Drop/Merge/Summarize, reason }`.
4. **Heuristic priority ordering**: which span-kinds get dropped first? Spec-aware ordering (file-loads with mtime mismatch > old tool-results > old assistant-text > skill-activations > tool-schemas > system) seems right but should be empirical.

## Correctness model

Unlike `CacheStrategy` (which has byte-for-byte correctness — boundary on last stable cacheable span), compaction correctness is FUZZY:

- Outcome-based: did the model produce a correct answer after compaction?
- Proxy-based: did the post-compaction prompt later reference span IDs that were dropped? (audit-shape regression test).
- Quality-proxy: structural-grader from protocol-eval framework, comparing full-context-response vs. compacted-context-response on a shared corpus.

Benchmark infrastructure has to support outcome/proxy/quality measurements, not just timing+token-count.

## Composition

- **mu-fb0 (closed)**: rope is now the live working state. CompactionPolicy operates on rope. Required prerequisite — satisfied.
- **mu-pex (open)**: metrics framework is the natural home for compaction quality dashboards. Compaction emits per-call metrics into mu-pex's pipeline.
- **mu-ktq (closed)**: trait pattern (CacheStrategy, ProviderRenderer) — CompactionPolicy follows the same shape.
- **mu-upb (closed)**: per-session JSONL logs on disk become the natural corpus for benchmark replay (load a real session's events, project to rope, compact, compare).

## Research/literature dependencies

Compaction is a live research area. Worth surveying before committing to design:
- Anthropic's documented context-engineering best practices (claude.com/docs/context-engineering)
- LangChain/LlamaIndex's `ConversationSummaryMemory` patterns
- Recent papers on long-context model retention (memory module ideas)
- Claude Code's own compaction trigger/strategy (visible via the public CLI behavior — 5-min compaction on 1M context is the observable artifact)

Operator's framing: "now that we have the primitives, we can take research literature or our own testing and try to optimize." This bead unblocks that research-driven optimization phase.

## Acceptance

- `CompactionPolicy` trait + `NoCompactionPolicy` + at least one heuristic policy land in `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction.rs`.
- Per-session config selects the policy; defaults to `NoCompactionPolicy` (preserves v1 behavior).
- Agent loop invokes `policy.compact()` when token threshold crossed.
- Compaction emits a structured event (similar to ContextAssembly) so the operator can audit decisions.
- Benchmark harness with at least 3 corpus ropes; outputs (input_tokens, output_tokens, time_ms, decision_log).
- Tests: heuristic policy preserves "load-bearing" spans (tool-schemas, recent assistant turns); drops correctly identified stale spans; cache-boundary composition works (compacted prefix becomes a new cacheable boundary).
- `cargo test --workspace` green, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green, `cargo fmt --check` green.

## Notes

Filed 2026-05-14 after mu-fb0 merged the rope-as-live-state infrastructure. Conversation context: operator noted Claude Code's ~5-minute compaction on 1M Opus 4.7; suspected mu's would be faster. The trait-based separation is what enables benchmarking, A/B testing, and research-driven evolution — without coupling each new policy to the agent loop's internals.
notes:
## Anthropic's published mechanism (verified 2026-05-14)

Per Anthropic's API docs (`platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/compaction`):
- Mechanism: **full model inference call** ("the same model specified in your request performs the summarization").
- Default trigger: 150k input tokens.
- Output shape: free-form natural-language summary wrapped in a `compaction` content block.
- Replacement: on subsequent requests, messages before the compaction block are auto-dropped; conversation continues from the summary.

NOT a mark-spans / structured-marks approach. NOT a heuristic. NOT documented: the internal prompt or any preservation rules.

## Design ladder (operator-designed, three rungs of judge+executor sophistication)

The ladder splits the model's job (judgment) from mu's job (rope surgery). Each rung trades output-size for cross-pass identity.

### Rung A: full-hash output (mu-kgu.3, landed in PR #22)

```text
INPUT to judge:
  <hash> [span_kind] preview...
  <hash> [span_kind] preview...

OUTPUT from judge:
  { "keep": ["<hash>", "<hash>", ...], "summary": "<paragraph>" }
```

- Output: ~700-2000 tokens (keep-list hashes ~300 tokens + summary ~500 tokens)
- ~5-10x cheaper than Anthropic's free-form-summary approach
- Hashes are content-stable across compaction passes (useful for audit/replay)

### Rung B: index-based keep-list (mu-kgu.7, follow-on optimization)

Operator design (2026-05-14): "you're sending an index for the active context → hash → string, so all that needs to come back is the short id of the index items to keep. That's even smaller than the last idea."

```text
INPUT to judge:
  #1 [span_kind] preview...  hash=abc12345
  #2 [span_kind] preview...  hash=def67890

OUTPUT from judge:
  { "keep": [1, 4, 7, 12], "summary": "<paragraph>" }
```

- Output: ~200-700 tokens (keep-list integers ~50 tokens + summary ~500 tokens)
- ~6x cheaper than Rung A's keep-list portion
- Indices are pass-local; mu maps `#N → span` and preserves hashes server-side for provenance
- The judge model doesn't need cross-pass identity (it's one-shot judgment); hashes only matter to the operator and the rope's audit log

### Rung C: future research — when "judge" itself wants memory

If a future iteration wants the judge to maintain cross-call state (e.g. "this span has been kept across three compactions; lower the bar to drop it"), hash-based output regains the audit-stability advantage. Defer until evidence motivates it.

## Recommendation

Ship rung A in mu-kgu.3 (current PR #22). File mu-kgu.7 as the rung B optimization once mu-kgu.3 has landed and benchmark numbers exist to compare against. The benchmark harness from mu-kgu.5 is the right home for measuring the rung A → rung B delta.

## New policy: HashAndSummaryPolicy (rung A, judge + executor, single call)

How it works (single model call) — preserved here for archeology, full algorithm in mu-kgu.3's bead body:

1. Build span-index: each span gets stable 8-char hex hash + span_kind tag + ~80-160 char preview + full content.
2. Judge prompt: given the span sequence, return `{keep, summary}`.
3. Surgery: kept hashes preserve verbatim with provenance; others absorbed into one new CompactionSummary span.
4. Result: kept spans (verbatim) + one summary span at the position of the earliest absorbed span.

## Why this is a strategic differentiator

If rung A benchmarks confirm wall-clock under ~10 seconds (vs claude-code's 5 minutes), that's a categorical UX difference. Rung B compounds the win further. Combined with the verbatim-retention property (auditable, replayable), the "mu has better compaction than claude-code" message lands clearly.

## Ladder of compaction policies (existing positions)

1. NoCompactionPolicy — do nothing (mu-kgu.1, landed).
2. SpanFamilyDropPolicy — heuristic span-kind drops, zero model calls (mu-kgu.2, landed).
3. **HashAndSummaryPolicy rung A** — full-hash output (mu-kgu.3, in PR #22).
4. **HashAndSummaryPolicy rung B** — index-based keep-list (mu-kgu.7, follow-on).
5. AnthropicLikePolicy — full-model summarize-and-replace (matches claude-code today).
6. Future rung C — judge-with-memory.

Each rung is a separate policy implementing the CompactionPolicy trait. Benchmarks compare them via the mu-kgu.5 harness.

title: mu-compaction-foundation: CompactionPolicy trait + NoCompactionPolicy + stubs
description:
Phase 1 of the mu-kgu prototype. Lays the trait surface that Phase 2's three parallel workers fill in.

## Scope

### In

- New module `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction.rs` with:
  - `pub trait CompactionPolicy: Send + Sync` — method: `fn compact(&self, rope: &RetainedRope, target_tokens: usize) -> CompactionResult;`
  - `pub struct CompactionResult { rope: RetainedRope, decisions: Vec<CompactionDecision>, tokens_before: usize, tokens_after: usize, wall_clock_ms: u64 }`
  - `pub enum CompactionDecision { Kept { span_id: String }, Dropped { span_id: String, reason: String }, Summarized { absorbed_span_ids: Vec<String>, summary_span_id: String } }`
  - `pub struct NoCompactionPolicy;` — `compact()` returns the rope unchanged + an empty decisions Vec
- Stub modules at `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction/{heuristic,hash_summary}.rs`:
  - `pub struct SpanFamilyDropPolicy;` and `pub struct HashAndSummaryPolicy { judge_provider: Arc<dyn Provider> }` (or similar)
  - Both impl `CompactionPolicy` with `todo!(\"see mu-compaction-heuristic / mu-compaction-hash-summary\")` in `compact()`
- `Provider` trait addition (in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/provider.rs`):
  - `fn compaction_policy(&self) -> Arc<dyn CompactionPolicy> { Arc::new(crate::context::compaction::NoCompactionPolicy) }` with default impl preserving v1 behavior
- Wire into `crates/mu-core/src/context/mod.rs` and `crates/mu-core/src/lib.rs`
- Unit tests:
  - `CompactionResult` serde round-trips
  - `NoCompactionPolicy::compact()` is idempotent (input rope == output rope)
  - `Provider::compaction_policy()` default returns NoCompactionPolicy (verify via TypeId or Arc::ptr_eq pattern)

### Out

- Any real compaction logic (Phase 2)
- Agent-loop integration (separate bead, mu-compaction-loop-integration)
- Benchmark harness (separate, mu-compaction-benchmark-harness)

## Why this is the gatekeeper

Phase 2's three workers each need an agreed-upon trait surface to fill in. If the trait shape isn't locked here, the three workers will diverge and merge becomes painful. Invest in clean Phase 1 → save 2+ hours of conflict-resolution later.

## Acceptance

- All listed types + trait + stubs compile.
- `cargo test --workspace` green (no regression on existing tests).
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green.
- `cargo fmt --check` green.
- Commit message cites mu-kgu + this bead.
close_reason: Merged in PR #19

title: mu-kgu.10: replace chars/4 token estimator with real tokenizer (tiktoken-rs) for headline-comparable numbers
description:
Replace mu's `estimate_tokens(spans) -> usize` (currently `chars().count()` per span) with a real-tokenizer implementation. Without this, mu's reported "tokens_before / tokens_after" numbers in benchmarks and `ContextAssembly` events use a different ruler than Anthropic's measured token counts — which prevents direct headline comparisons in the README.

## The miscount

In the mu-kgu.9 calibration (2026-05-14), mu's estimator reported the test corpus at ~81k tokens; Anthropic's real tokenizer (via `usage.iterations[].input_tokens` on the same corpus) measured 124,091 tokens. **~50% under-count**. Ratios (`tokens_after / tokens_before`) are preserved internally, but absolute magnitudes can't be cross-referenced with external sources.

## Why P1

This is the load-bearing piece for any README-level claim about token reduction or cost savings. We need mu's "the heuristic reduces 240k tokens to 18k in 19µs" to use the SAME 240k number Anthropic would report on the same input. Otherwise the math doesn't compose cleanly and skeptical readers can't verify our claims independently.

## Scope

### In

- Add dependency: `tiktoken-rs = "0.x"` (well-maintained MIT-licensed Rust port of OpenAI's tiktoken).
- Replace `estimate_tokens(spans)` body to use tiktoken-rs (the `cl100k_base` encoding is the closest public approximation of Anthropic's tokenizer — both are BPE schemes with similar compression ratios).
- Document the substitution: tiktoken-rs is not Anthropic's actual tokenizer (Anthropic's is undocumented), but the count is close enough for budget-shape decisions and headline-comparable numbers. Acceptable error: ±5%.
- Update `span_size()` in `heuristic.rs` to delegate to the shared estimator (it already does, post mu-metrics-cleanup).
- Re-run the benchmark; update the cleanup-writeup numbers in `~/.claude-personal/experiments/path-a-compaction-calibration-2026-05-14.md` if material.

### Out

- Per-provider tokenizers (OpenAI vs Anthropic vs Gemini all use slightly different schemes). v1 uses one tokenizer for all; per-provider can be a follow-up if needed.
- A *true* Anthropic tokenizer port. Anthropic doesn't publish theirs; we approximate via tiktoken.
- Server-side count_tokens API call. Too slow for in-bench-loop use. Useful for one-off calibration; not for the estimator.

## Acceptance

- `estimate_tokens(spans)` uses tiktoken (cl100k_base).
- mu's reported tokens are within ±5% of Anthropic's `usage.iterations[].input_tokens` on the same input.
- Existing tests still pass (test fixtures use ASCII content where char/4 ≈ tiktoken count; minor assertion updates may be needed).
- Benchmark output is now headline-comparable: "mu reduced 124k tokens to 6k in 19µs" can be cited next to "Anthropic compacted 124k tokens to 2.3k in 38s" with consistent units.
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.

## Cost calibration: re-run

After landing, re-run the Path A calibration script + mu's bench to produce a clean joint table with consistent token units. Update README candidates.

## References

- mu-kgu.9 calibration writeup: `~/.claude-personal/experiments/path-a-compaction-calibration-2026-05-14.md` (notes the ~50% divergence)
- Current estimator: `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction.rs::estimate_tokens`
- tiktoken-rs: https://github.com/zurawiki/tiktoken-rs

## Provenance

Operator (2026-05-14): "it might be worth looking at the tokens part now. This seems like a headline for our readme that we'd want any potential visitors to see."

Filed 2026-05-14, immediately after mu-kgu.9's measurement surfaced the divergence as the load-bearing accuracy gap for any external claim.
close_reason: Merged in PR #34 as 78cf81a

title: mu-kgu.11: live-judge wiring for HashAndSummaryPolicy — Provider-backed Judge adapter
description:
Wire mu's `HashAndSummaryPolicy` (mu-kgu.3) to use a real `Provider`-backed `Judge` so the bench harness's `--judge live` flag stops erroring out. Unblocks the apples-to-apples comparison (mu hash-summary[Haiku] vs Anthropic Opus auto-compaction) that mu-kgu.9 couldn't run because the live judge path didn't exist yet.

## Scope

### In

- New `ProviderJudge` (or `LiveJudge`) struct at `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction/hash_summary.rs` (or sibling file).
- Constructor: takes `Arc<dyn Provider>` + model name (or pulls model from provider).
- Implements the existing `Judge` trait from mu-kgu.3.
- `judge(&prompt)` body:
  - Build a single-turn `AgentMessage::User { content: prompt.to_string() }`.
  - Call `provider.stream(None, &[msg], &[], cancel_rx).await` (no tools, no system prompt).
  - Collect text deltas from the stream into a String.
  - Apply a configurable timeout (default 60s) — fail-closed return `Err` if it hits.
  - Return Ok(text).
- Bench harness `compaction-bench.rs` `--judge live` path: instantiate `ProviderJudge` using whichever provider is configured (default: Anthropic with `claude-haiku-4-5` model — cheap + fast).
- Tests: mock-provider that returns a canned response; verify the judge wires correctly.

### Out

- Multi-provider judge selection per-session (operator decides via config at startup).
- Streaming judge output (judge prompt is short; full collect is fine).
- Judge result caching across calls (interesting but separate concern).

## Why this is P2

Unlocks the headline measurement: "mu hash-summary[live Haiku] vs Anthropic Opus auto-compaction" — the apples-to-apples comparison that mu-kgu.9 left as a follow-up. Without this, the README's compaction story has a measurement-shaped hole.

Estimated cost when wired + run: ~$1 across 5 calibration runs (Haiku is much cheaper than Opus).

## Acceptance

- `cargo run --example compaction-bench -- --judge live` works against real Anthropic API (with ANTHROPIC_API_KEY set).
- Default judge model: claude-haiku-4-5.
- Existing mock-judge tests still pass.
- New integration test (or example invocation) confirms live-judge produces non-empty keep+summary output on a real corpus.

## References

- mu-kgu.3 bead body (the algorithm + Judge trait surface).
- crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction/hash_summary.rs (existing mock judge).
- crates/mu-core/examples/compaction-bench.rs (docstring already mentions "live mode errors out: a Provider-backed Judge adapter is not yet wired").
- Calibration writeup: ~/.claude-personal/experiments/path-a-compaction-calibration-2026-05-14.md

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 after mu-kgu.9 calibration produced a defensible Anthropic-side number but couldn't complete the matching mu-hash-summary side. Operator: "file the live-judge-wiring bead."
notes:
## Provider-agnostic routing (added 2026-05-14)

The `ProviderJudge` takes `Arc<dyn Provider>` so the operator picks all three routing axes per-session/per-event:

### Axis 1: Provider

| Provider | Path | Cost path | Use case |
|---|---|---|---|
| `AnthropicProvider` | API key direct | Anthropic billing | Haiku/Sonnet/Opus direct |
| `OpenRouterProvider` | API key direct | OpenRouter credits | Cheap small models + variety |
| `OpenaiCodexProvider` | OAuth | Codex sub (flat) | When operator wants codex billing |

### Axis 2: Model (cost per compaction event @ ~50k input + ~2k output)

| Model | Provider | Cost / event | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| DeepSeek-V2 | OpenRouter | **~$0.005** | Cheapest; quality TBD on judge task |
| Qwen2-7B-Instruct | OpenRouter | ~$0.005 | Similar; quality TBD |
| gpt-4o-mini | OpenRouter | ~$0.01 | Reliable small |
| Claude Haiku 4.5 | Anthropic | ~$0.05 | Most-trusted small; cheapest reliable |
| Claude Opus 4.7 | Anthropic | ~$2.03 | Matches Anthropic's auto-compaction |

### Axis 3: Auth path

- OAuth (Anthropic Max5, Codex sub) — preferred default for routine use
- API key (OpenRouter, direct Anthropic API) — required for cache-control / beta features / specific calibration tests

### Comparison to Anthropic's auto-compaction (per mu-kgu.9)

$2.03 / event, 38s wall-clock. Mu hash-summary with small-model OpenRouter judge: estimated ~$0.005 / event, ~1-2s wall-clock. Per-event ratio: ~400× cheaper, ~20-40× faster. The apples-to-apples comparison that completes the mu-kgu.9 calibration story.

## Blocked by mu-l1z (mu-config-system)

This bead is downgraded from "implementation P2" to "configuration-consumer P2" by the introduction of mu-l1z (mu-config-system). The routing decisions above belong in `[compaction.judge]` of `~/.config/mu/config.toml`, NOT hardcoded in the policy struct.

When mu-l1z lands:
- `HashAndSummaryPolicy::from_config(&config.compaction.judge)` returns a policy instance with the configured ProviderJudge ranking.
- Daemon walks the ranking on each compaction event; first available provider+model+auth wins.
- Fail-over chain: if DeepSeek (OpenRouter) is unreachable, try Qwen; if all OpenRouter fails, try Haiku-via-Anthropic-OAuth; if Anthropic OAuth quota exhausted, try Anthropic API key (if operator allows).

The judge wiring itself (the Arc<dyn Provider> → Judge adapter) is still scoped to this bead, but its CONFIG comes from mu-l1z.

### Pre-mu-l1z fallback path

If we want to land mu-kgu.11 before mu-l1z, the v0.5 shape uses env-vars + code defaults:
- `MU_JUDGE_PROVIDER=openrouter`, `MU_JUDGE_MODEL=deepseek-chat`, `MU_JUDGE_AUTH=api_key`
- Daemon reads at startup, constructs the single provider; no ranking, no fallover.

This unblocks the apples-to-apples calibration NOW. mu-l1z can come later to add the ranking + fallover.

## Test plan addition

When live-judge runs against the same corpus as mu-kgu.9, capture:
- Provider + model identifiers
- Input tokens (judge prompt) + output tokens (judge response)
- Wall-clock (request-send → final delta)
- Surgery output: spans kept vs dropped, summary length

Compare against mu-kgu.9's Anthropic-side row in the same writeup. This is the measurement that turns the README "20x faster" estimate into a defensible measured number.
close_reason: ProviderJudge adapter landed in mu-core (sync→async via dedicated runtime thread); compaction-bench --judge live now wired to AnthropicProvider via  (default model claude-haiku-4-5-20251001, override via $MU_BENCH_JUDGE_MODEL). Bench example moved to mu-ai/examples to avoid cargo dev-dep cycle. 5 new tests cover delta-collection, Done-fallback, error surfacing, no-Done failure, and policy integration.

title: mu-compaction-heuristic: SpanFamilyDropPolicy (zero-model-call drop-by-span-kind)
description:
Phase 2 of mu-kgu prototype. Implements `SpanFamilyDropPolicy` — drop spans by span-kind priority. Zero model calls.

## Drop priority (lowest to highest survival)

Within a single compaction pass, evict spans in this order until tokens_after ≤ target_tokens:

1. **Stale `FileLoad` spans** — if mtime checks indicate the file changed since load.
2. **Old `ToolCall`/`ToolResult` clusters** — paired by call_id, drop oldest-pair first.
3. **Old `AssistantText` turns** — older-than-N back.
4. **Stale `SkillActivation`** — skills explicitly deactivated.
5. **Recent `AssistantText` / current `SkillActivation` / `ToolSchema` / `System`** — preserve.

## Scope

### In

- Implement `compact()` on `SpanFamilyDropPolicy` at `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction/heuristic.rs`.
- `CompactionResult.decisions` records every drop with the priority-tier reason.
- Tests:
  - Drop priority preserves tool-schemas + system spans
  - Stale file-loads dropped before old tool-results
  - Idempotent when rope already under target
  - Decision-log matches expected drops on a fixture rope

### Out

- Mtime-based staleness detection (separate concern; v1 uses span-age as proxy)
- Configurable priority (operator-tunable). v1 hardcodes priority; future bead can add config.

## Depends on

mu-compaction-foundation (trait surface).
close_reason: Merged in PR #20

title: mu-compaction-hash-summary: HashAndSummaryPolicy (single judge-call, keep+summary output)
description:
Phase 2 of mu-kgu prototype. The strategic differentiator: single judge-model call, output is `{keep: [hash], summary: string}`.

## How it works (single model call)

1. Build span-index: each span gets stable 8-char hex hash + span_kind tag + ~80-160 char preview + full content.
2. Judge prompt (to cheap model — Haiku 4.5 default): given the span sequence, return:
   ```json
   { "keep": ["abc12345", "def67890", ...], "summary": "natural-language summary of spans NOT in keep" }
   ```
3. Surgery:
   - Hashes in `keep` → preserve verbatim with provenance
   - All other spans → discarded; `summary` becomes one new `CompactionSummary` span at the position of the earliest absorbed span
   - Result: kept spans (verbatim) + one summary span

## Scope

### In

- Implement `HashAndSummaryPolicy::compact()` at `crates/mu-core/src/context/compaction/hash_summary.rs`.
- Hash function: blake3 truncated to 8 hex chars (12 chars if collision detected in the rope).
- Judge provider configurable; default = whatever Anthropic provider is registered with claude-haiku-4-5 (use `mu-ai::providers::output_limits::max_tokens_for_model` for the call's max_tokens).
- JSON-mode / structured-output where supported; fall back to regex extraction with schema validation otherwise.
- New `SpanKind::CompactionSummary { absorbed_span_ids: Vec<String>, generated_at_unix_ms: u64, policy_id: String }` for the summary span.
- Tests:
  - Mock judge returns valid keep+summary → surgery produces expected rope
  - Mock judge returns malformed JSON → fail closed (return original rope + CompactionDecision::Failed)
  - Mock judge picks empty keep-list → all spans absorbed into summary span
  - Hash stability: same content → same hash; collision detection extends to 12 chars

### Out

- Live judge call against real Anthropic API (test-only mock). Real API calls happen at Phase 3 benchmark.
- Streaming-judge support (v1 collects full judge response before surgery).

## Depends on

mu-compaction-foundation (trait surface).

## Critical correctness property

If the judge fails (malformed JSON, timeout, API error), the policy MUST return the original rope unchanged + a CompactionDecision::Failed entry. NEVER silently drop spans on judge failure.
close_reason: Merged in PR #22

title: mu-compaction-loop-integration: threshold-triggered policy dispatch in agent loop
description:
Phase 2 of mu-kgu prototype. Hook policy dispatch into the agent loop.

## Scope

### In

- In `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`, before each `assemble_rope` + `renderer.render(...)` call:
  - Compute `tokens_before = renderer.estimate_tokens(&rope)` (or similar — may need a new ProviderRenderer method).
  - If `tokens_before > config.compaction_threshold` (per-session config, default: 70% of model's context window or 150000, whichever is lower):
    - Invoke `provider.compaction_policy().compact(&rope, target_tokens)` where target_tokens ≈ 50% of context window.
    - Replace rope with `result.rope`.
    - Emit new `AgentEvent::CompactionAssembly { policy_id, tokens_before, tokens_after, decisions_count, wall_clock_ms }` (NOT the full decision log — that goes into the event-log via the rope itself).
- New `AgentConfig` field: `compaction_threshold: Option<usize>` (None = use default).
- Tests:
  - With `NoCompactionPolicy` (default), no `CompactionAssembly` event ever fires.
  - With a mock `EvictHalfPolicy`, exactly one `CompactionAssembly` fires when the threshold is crossed; subsequent calls don't re-trigger because tokens_after < threshold.
  - The mock policy's output rope is what the next `renderer.render` consumes.

### Out

- Live policy implementations (those are siblings mu-compaction-heuristic and mu-compaction-hash-summary).
- Cache-boundary verification after compaction (separate Phase 3 bead: mu-compaction-cache-composition).

## Depends on

mu-compaction-foundation (trait surface).

## Critical correctness property

If compaction fails (policy returns error or original rope unchanged), the agent loop MUST still proceed with the original rope. Never block a turn on compaction failure.
close_reason: Merged in PR #21

title: mu-compaction-benchmark-harness: load mu-upb corpus, compare policies
description:
Phase 3 of mu-kgu prototype. Benchmark harness comparing all CompactionPolicies on a corpus of real ropes.

## Scope

### In

- New binary `crates/mu-core/src/bin/compaction-bench.rs` (or `crates/mu-core/examples/compaction-bench.rs`).
- Loads session JSONLs from `~/.local/share/mu/events/<daemon_id>/<session_id>.jsonl` (mu-upb's write-side persistence).
- Projects each session's events into a `RetainedRope` (use mu-fb0's assembly path).
- For each policy in a configurable list: run `compact()`, record metrics:
  - `input_tokens`, `output_tokens`, `wall_clock_ms`, `model_calls` (0 for heuristic, 1 for hash-summary), `decisions_count`
  - For hash-summary: also `judge_provider`, `judge_model`, `judge_input_tokens`, `judge_output_tokens`
- Output: CSV / JSON to stdout for analysis.
- Tests: mock-corpus + mock-policy returns expected metrics shape.

### Out

- Quality grader (retention proxy, response-quality comparison). Separate bead — needs mu-pex landed first.
- Live judge API calls. Real calls use the test-corpus tcovert provides; benchmark harness supports both mock and live modes.
- Dashboard rendering. Output is CSV/JSON; downstream tools (mu-pex, jupyter, etc.) render.

## Depends on

mu-compaction-foundation (trait), mu-compaction-heuristic (one impl), mu-compaction-hash-summary (other impl), mu-compaction-loop-integration (compaction event shape — useful for understanding output schema).
close_reason: Merged in PR #27

title: mu-compaction-cache-composition: verify CacheStrategy boundary on post-compaction rope
description:
Phase 3 of mu-kgu prototype. Verify that `CacheStrategy` (mu-ktq) places its boundary correctly on a post-compaction rope.

## Why

Compaction creates a new stable prefix (the surviving kept spans + summary span). The next ask should benefit from a fresh cache boundary at the end of that prefix — so subsequent asks pay one cache-creation cost and then enjoy cache hits.

Without this verification, compaction could land a rope whose CacheStrategy mis-places the boundary (e.g. before some kept span that's truly stable), invalidating the cache savings we expect.

## Scope

### In

- Integration test in `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic_tests.rs` (or a new `compaction_cache_tests.rs`):
  - Build rope with N spans, run `AnthropicCacheStrategy::annotate()`, capture boundary position.
  - Run a compaction policy (use a mock that drops some spans, keeps others), then re-run `annotate()`.
  - Assert: boundary lands AT or AFTER the position of the last kept-and-stable span.
- Document the invariant in `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (cache-strategy section).

### Out

- Multi-compaction-pass scenarios (compaction → compaction → compaction). Future bead if observed in practice.
- Cache lifecycle modeling (TTL, eviction). Anthropic's cache has 5-min TTL; that's policy-orthogonal to compaction-cache composition.

## Depends on

mu-compaction-loop-integration (the event shape + invocation pattern).
close_reason: Merged in PR #26

title: mu-compaction-hash-summary-rung-b: index-based keep-list (compact judge output, follow-on to mu-kgu.3)
description:
Rung B of the HashAndSummaryPolicy ladder. Reduces judge output size by ~6x on the keep-list portion by emitting integer indices instead of full 8-char hashes. Pass-local indices replace cross-pass-stable hashes; mu retains hashes server-side for provenance.

## Operator design (2026-05-14)

> "you're sending an index for the active context → hash → string, so all that needs to come back is the short id of the index items to keep. That's even smaller than the last idea."

## Wire shape change

Compared to mu-kgu.3's rung A:

### Before (rung A, current)

```
INPUT line:  <hash> [span_kind] preview...
OUTPUT:      { "keep": ["<hash>", "<hash>", ...], "summary": "..." }
```

### After (rung B)

```
INPUT line:  #1 [span_kind] preview...  hash=abc12345
             #2 [span_kind] preview...  hash=def67890
OUTPUT:      { "keep": [1, 4, 7, 12], "summary": "..." }
```

## Token math

Output size for 100 kept spans:
- Rung A (hashes): ~12 chars × 100 = ~300 tokens
- Rung B (indices): ~2-3 chars × 100 = ~50 tokens
- **~6x reduction on keep-list portion**

Summary portion (~500 tokens) unchanged. Total output ~700-2000 → ~250-700 tokens.

## Implementation

- Add second policy variant: `HashAndSummaryPolicy::with_index_output(judge)` or a parallel `IndexedHashAndSummaryPolicy`. Lean: extend existing policy with `output_mode: HashKeep | IndexKeep` enum field, default `HashKeep` for backward compatibility, opt-in to `IndexKeep` per-session config.
- Build prompt with `#N` prefix on each line in addition to the hash. The hash stays attached to each input line for the judge's reference, but the judge is told "emit indices in `keep`, not hashes."
- Parse judge output: validate `keep` entries are integers in range; map each back to the corresponding span.
- Surgery: same as rung A — kept spans preserved verbatim with full hash-based provenance; everything else absorbed into one CompactionSummary span.
- Hash remains server-side for audit/provenance even though the judge doesn't emit it.

## Tradeoff

- **Hashes (rung A)**: content-stable across compaction passes. Useful for future research where the judge wants memory across calls ("this span survived 3 compactions; raise its bar").
- **Indices (rung B)**: pass-local. The judge does one-shot judgment per call, doesn't need cross-pass identity. Smaller output, lower cost, faster wall-clock.

For the v1 judge (one-shot Haiku 4.5 inference), indices are strictly better. For future "judge with memory" workflows (rung C), revisit hashes.

## Acceptance

- `IndexKeep` output mode + `output_mode` config + parse + surgery work end-to-end.
- Tests: valid index output → expected surgery; out-of-range index → fail-closed (return original rope + `CompactionDecision::Failed`); typographic robustness (#1 vs `1` vs `keep [1]`).
- Benchmark harness (mu-kgu.5) compares rung A vs rung B on the same corpus; CSV output includes both rows.
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.

## Depends on

mu-kgu.3 (rung A landed; this extends the existing policy).
Composable with mu-kgu.5 (benchmark harness — the natural site to validate the win).

## References

- Original ladder context: mu-kgu's "Design ladder" notes.
- Provenance: operator design 2026-05-14 conversation, refining the hash-output approach to index-output after reviewing PR #22's implementation.
close_reason: Merged in same PR — IndexKeep output mode added to HashAndSummaryPolicy

title: mu-compaction-background-worker: hide LLM-summary latency from foreground turns (pi-inspired)
description:
Optional background-worker mode for compaction policies that perform LLM calls. Inspired by pi_agent_rust's CompactionWorker (~/src/flywheel/pi_agent_rust/src/compaction_worker.rs) which runs compaction off the foreground turn path with quota controls.

## Why

Mu's planned synchronous compaction blocks the foreground turn while compaction runs. For mu-kgu.3's HashAndSummaryPolicy with a live judge (Haiku 4.5 round-trip ~500-1500ms), or any future "summarize via Opus" policy (potentially seconds), that's user-visible latency.

Pi's approach: run compaction in a background worker on the existing runtime; apply results on subsequent turns. Foreground turns 1-2 after threshold-cross run on un-compacted context, but the user never waits.

## Scope

### In

- New trait method `CompactionPolicy::is_async() -> bool` (default false). Synchronous policies (heuristic) keep current behavior.
- For policies returning `true`, the agent loop wraps the `compact()` call in a tokio task spawned at threshold-cross. The result lands as an `AgentEvent::CompactionAssembly` event when ready; subsequent turns pick up the new rope.
- `CompactionQuota` config: cooldown, timeout, max-attempts-per-session (port pi's shape).
- Quota gating: cooldown enforced; timeout returns the un-compacted rope; max-attempts protects against runaway.

### Out

- Per-policy fine-tuned quotas (v1 uses global config).
- Compaction-during-streaming (compact between turns, not during).
- Persisted compaction state across daemon restarts (separate concern; composes with mu-mh4).

## Tradeoff named

Background compaction means turns 1-2 after threshold-cross run on un-compacted context — model sees the full rope for those turns. Token budget exceeded → API request error or auto-truncation by Anthropic. So background-compact requires the threshold to be set BELOW the model's hard limit by at least 2-3 turns' worth of growth.

For Anthropic's auto-compaction (their beta `compact-2026-01-12` feature with `pause_after_compaction: True`), Anthropic does this synchronously and the operator sees the compaction wall-clock. mu's background-compact differs: invisible to operator but at the cost of 1-2 stale-context turns.

## Acceptance

- `CompactionPolicy::is_async()` trait method; default false; existing impls don't change behavior.
- `CompactionQuota` struct + per-session config.
- Background-worker mode wired into agent loop for async policies.
- Test: synchronous policy unchanged behavior; async policy fires off-thread, result lands on subsequent turn.
- cargo test --workspace green; clippy -D warnings; fmt --check.

## References

- pi_agent_rust's CompactionWorker: ~/src/flywheel/pi_agent_rust/src/compaction_worker.rs (422 LOC; cooldown=60s, timeout=120s defaults)
- pi's compaction algorithm: ~/src/flywheel/pi_agent_rust/README.md:1267-1310
- Composes with mu-kgu.3 (HashAndSummaryPolicy — primary async candidate)
- Composes with future "AnthropicLikePolicy" if mu ever adds one to the ladder

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 after pi_agent_rust survey during Path A measurement-experiment planning. Operator noted "background compaction sounds like a good idea."
close_reason: Background-worker compaction landed. CompactionPolicy::is_async() defaults false (sync) — HashAndSummaryPolicy overrides to true. BackgroundCompactionState manages per-session pending tokio task + quota (cooldown 60s / timeout 120s / max 100/session). Agent loop drains pending result on each turn, applies to baseline via append_messages_to_baseline. 5 new compaction tests cover quota defaults, cooldown/max-attempts blocking, and start-then-take roundtrip.

title: mu-compaction-calibration: measure Anthropic /compact wall-clock vs mu policies (Path A experiment)
description:
One-off measurement experiment: time Anthropic's compaction feature on a real-world ~180k-token input and compare to mu's three policies (heuristic, hash-summary[mock], hash-summary[live judge]).

## Method

Use Anthropic's beta compaction feature (released 2026-01-12):
- Beta header: `anthropic-beta: compact-2026-01-12`
- `context_management: {edits: [{type: "compact_20260112", trigger: {type: "input_tokens", value: 150000}, pause_after_compaction: true}]}`
- `pause_after_compaction: true` isolates compaction wall-clock — response contains ONLY the compaction summary, `stop_reason: "compaction"`, no answer text
- Stream via SSE; measure from request-send to final delta

Construct input: load a real mu session JSONL (from `~/.local/share/mu/events/`) trimmed to ~180k tokens. Same input across all measurement runs.

Repeat 5x per policy. Compute median + variance. Compare:
- Anthropic auto-compaction (Opus 4.7) — wall-clock + iterations.output_tokens
- mu heuristic — already measured (19µs median)
- mu hash-summary[mock] — already measured (198µs median)
- mu hash-summary[live Haiku judge] — needs `--judge live` wiring first (separate bead)

## Cost

5 runs × (180k input × $15/M + 3.5k output × $75/M) ≈ $14.80 for Opus 4.7 Anthropic measurements.
5 runs × (180k input × $0.80/M + 0.5k × $4/M) ≈ $0.74 for Haiku live-judge measurements.
**Total: ~$15.50.**

## Output

CSV/JSON of measurements + a writeup (probably in `~/.claude-personal/experiments/path-a-compaction-calibration-2026-05-14.md`) suitable for citation in PR descriptions / blog posts.

The key claim we want to defensibly support: "mu's heuristic is N orders of magnitude faster than Anthropic's compaction on equivalent inputs." Where N is measured, not guessed.

## Acceptance

- Measurement script written (Python preferred; one-off; no Rust ceremony needed).
- 5 runs each policy; medians + range reported.
- Writeup documents methodology + results + caveats.

## Provenance

Operator pushback (2026-05-14): "saying we're almost 20MM times faster than default is going to need some real metrics. My 'under 5 minutes' is because I looked over at 3m 48s and then went and did other things."

Filed before running the experiment so the bead serves as the audit-trail anchor for the measurement methodology.
close_reason: Measurement complete. Median Anthropic Opus 4.7 compaction wall-clock: 38.18s on 124k tokens (~.03 per event). Mu heuristic median: 19µs. Ratio: ~2M× faster. Writeup: ~/.claude-personal/experiments/path-a-compaction-calibration-2026-05-14.md
