title: mu-memory substrate roadmap: trait abstraction + in-tree → sled → (deferred) isolated rocksdb service
description:
## Context

The memory subsystem (mu-phl) needs a storage substrate. RocksDB has the
right shape (LSM compaction, merge operators, column families, snapshots,
WAL), but it'd be mu's first major C++ dependency. This bead captures the
roadmap so we don't paint ourselves into a corner — start simple, keep
options open via an abstraction.

## Observation: access patterns are substrate-similar

The access methods needed (append time-ordered entries, pointer-walk,
range scan, point read, batched compaction-with-rewrite) are similar
enough across candidate substrates (in-tree-event-log, sled, redb,
RocksDB) that they admit a **trait abstraction**. The abstraction is the
load-bearing element — it lets us start simple and migrate later without
rewriting callers.

## Phased plan

### Phase 1 — In-tree, simple, works against existing event logs

Implement the memory pointer index directly against mu's existing event
log infrastructure. No new dependencies. Optimized for "does the
abstraction make sense?" not "is this fast at scale?"

- Memory entries stored as events (per mu-phl's recursive design)
- Pointer-walk via direct event-log lookup
- Naive compaction: full scan, copy live, drop dead, rewrite
- Single-process, single-writer

**Why first:** unblocks mu-phl. No new deps. Fast iteration. Real workload
data to inform abstraction design.

### Phase 2 — Trait abstraction + sled adapter

Define the memory storage trait based on Phase 1 learnings. Implement a
sled-backed adapter. Run both in parallel briefly; verify the abstraction
is good enough that swapping backends doesn't leak.

- sled is pure Rust — no FFI, no C++ build complexity
- LSM shape with merge operators, similar to RocksDB
- Less mature than RocksDB but actively maintained
- Validates the trait is right; if sled-vs-in-tree behaviour diverges in
  ways the trait can't express, refine the trait

**Why sled second:** pure-Rust is the lowest-risk way to validate the
abstraction. If we later jump to RocksDB, we know the trait already works
against one alternative backend.

### Phase 3 (deferred, scale-gated) — Isolated RocksDB-fronted service

ONLY if scale demands actually arise (multi-machine, large fleets,
durability beyond what sled provides). Don't speculatively build.

When/if triggered, the shape:

- A separate Rust service fronts RocksDB — the C++ blast radius stays
  inside that service
- Mu talks to the service via a custom message queue with a binary
  protocol (bidir, fast)
- Queue transport pluggable: shared-memory / unix-socket for same-machine,
  TCP/QUIC for network — enables centralizing RocksDB across many mu
  instances
- The same memory trait used in Phase 1/2 backs onto a "remote-rocksdb"
  adapter that speaks the queue protocol

**Why this shape (if/when):**
- Keeps C++ dependency surgically isolated
- Transport-agnostic queue means same-machine vs. network is a config
  switch, not a rearchitecture
- Centralization-ready if a fleet of mu instances ever wants shared memory
- The message queue work becomes reusable for other cross-process needs

**Why deferred:** the costs (custom queue + binary protocol + separate
service + RocksDB ops burden) only pay off at a scale mu hasn't reached
and may never reach. Start with Phase 1, evolve to Phase 2 when the
abstraction is proven, only get to Phase 3 if scale forces it.

## Non-goals

- Writing our own LSM-tree from scratch. Interesting but we have enough
  irons in the fire; sled or RocksDB is preferable to a from-scratch
  storage engine.
- Embedding RocksDB directly in the mu binary. The C++ dep is fine inside
  an isolated service; not fine as a first-class mu link dependency.

## Acceptance (Phase 1 only — Phase 2/3 spawn follow-up beads)

- Memory trait defined, Phase 1 in-tree implementation works
- mu-phl's recall and relocation operations land on this substrate
- Abstraction documented well enough that a Phase 2 adapter is a
  contained exercise

## Cross-refs

- mu-phl (memory subsystem this provides substrate for)
- mu-ywr (discovery + permissions — separate concern, shares the substrate)
- mu-kgu (compaction policy — different layer; memory compaction here is
  pointer relocation, not context compaction)
- Provenance: user feedback session 2026-05-15 (rocksdb observation +
  abstraction-first plan)

title: mu-phl v0 Phase A — system_prompt: Option<String> → Option<SpanText> migration (mu-yqeq.2 cleanup)
description:
Standalone hygiene step in the mu-phl v0 goal session. Part of `~/.claude-personal/plans/happy-sprouting-sprout.md` step 1.

## What

Change `AgentConfig::system_prompt: Option<String>` → `Option<SpanText>` in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:316`. Update every construction site.

## Why

Gap left over from mu-yqeq.2 — the cutover migrated Span/ProviderMessage content to `Arc<str>` via per-type aliases (`SpanText`, `SpanId`, `MessageText`), but the config layer that feeds the System span was missed. Fixing it here keeps a clean `SpanText`-everywhere invariant from the daemon down through the rope.

## How

- `grep system_prompt:` to enumerate construction sites (expect 3–6).
- Mechanical: `system_prompt: Some(s)` → `system_prompt: Some(s.into())` (or `Some(Arc::from(s.as_str()))`).
- `cargo test --workspace` green before commit.

## Acceptance

- `system_prompt` is `Option<SpanText>` everywhere.
- All workspace tests pass.
- One commit, message ends with `(closes <this-bead>; mu-yqeq.2 cleanup)`.
- `## Files` block in commit message lists exact changed files.

## Independence

No dependencies on other beads in this goal session. Could land independently of all of them.
close_reason: Landed in jj commit ee837171833f on feat/mu-phl-v0 base. AgentConfig::system_prompt: Option<String> → Option<SpanText>. Mechanical at 3 sites (field declaration in mu-core/src/agent/loop_/mod.rs:328; conversion at mu-coding/src/serve/handlers/session.rs:247 via .map(SpanText::from); SpanText import added at both files). Default impl + test fixtures use None and need no change. cargo test --workspace green (755 tests, 0 failed). verify-claims green for this commit (Files block matches diff). Closes mu-yqeq.2 hygiene gap.

title: Measure: does code_index pre-load reduce agent turns/tokens for cold-repo tasks?
description:
## What's being measured

We have a tool — \`~/src/agent_tools/code_index\` — that walks a repo, chunks source files at definition boundaries via tree-sitter (Rust + Python), embeds chunks via OpenRouter, stores in sqlite with FTS5 lexical + semantic recall, and has a graph layer with PageRank centrality.

It was built for "give a sub-agent a focused slice of an unfamiliar codebase without re-reading every file each session post-compaction" — precisely the post-compaction context-recovery problem the recovery test in specs/measurements/compaction-recovery-test-2026-05-21.md surfaced. But it has never been wired into mu, and we don't have evidence it actually pays for its ingest cost.

This task runs the cleanest version of that measurement to decide whether to pursue integration.

## Hypothesis

Pre-loading code_index's top-N recall results into a mu session at start should let the agent:

- Use fewer turns to answer cold-repo questions (because it doesn't have to glob/grep/read-to-discover)
- Use fewer total tokens (less exploration tool calling)
- Possibly produce better answers (curated chunks vs whatever the agent happens to discover)

If the hypothesis holds, code_index integration becomes a real bead. If it doesn't, the tool exists but isn't load-bearing for mu and we shelve it.

## Method

For each of (5-10 carefully chosen) tasks × (2-3 repos the agent doesn't have in context):

- **Baseline**: spawn a mu session; ask the question; let the agent explore via tools.
- **Treatment**: run \`code-index recall <task-summary>\` first; inject top-N chunks as FileLoad spans (or a new SpanKind) at session start; ask the same question.

Measure per session:

- Turn count to converge on an answer
- Total input tokens
- Total output tokens
- Answer correctness (qualitative — human judgment vs ground truth)
- Whether agent re-read files that were already in the pre-loaded chunks

Net comparison: treatment-vs-baseline on the four axes.

## Suitable as background work

Each measurement is one agent-session-completion. Independent, parallelizable. Could run via the existing orchestrate.py infrastructure if labeled tasks have a "verifiable outcome" property. Otherwise, two sessions side-by-side and the operator scores.

## Cost estimate

- 5 tasks × 2 conditions = 10 sessions. At ~$1-2 each (Haiku- or Sonnet-class agent), $10-20 total.
- code_index ingest of each test repo: ~$0.50-2 in embeddings (OpenRouter, one-time per repo).
- Total: $15-30 expected. Within compaction-research budget shape from 2026-05-21.

## Pre-conditions

1. code_index works for the test repos. Currently supports Rust + Python only. If picking a JS/TS repo for the test, code_index needs to be extended (separate bead).
2. mu needs a hook to inject pre-loaded chunks at session start. Could be (a) a new \`SpanKind::IndexedChunk\` + assemble_rope changes, OR (b) just inject as the first user-prompt context block, OR (c) pre-pend as a system_prompt extension. Pick the simplest that doesn't require new substrate.

## References

- Tool: \`~/src/agent_tools/code_index/\`
- Use-case framing in code_index README: "without re-reading every file each session post-compaction"
- specs/measurements/compaction-recovery-test-2026-05-21.md — the recovery test that motivated this question
- Memory be72d65e: option-2 v2 finding (mu wins forensic recovery when tools available; the v2 finding's reliance on user-prompt path mentions is exactly what code_index could pre-load)

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Sharpening the experiment design — concrete benchmark via theme + forge-rs

Conversation 2026-05-21 (see ~/.claude-personal/notes/risk-check-design-2026-05-21.md): theme (~/src/proprietary_thaddeus/theme/, ~766k LOC C/C++ + Ruby) and forge-rs (~/src/forge-rs/, idiomatic-Rust landing repo) together give this measurement a real-world benchmark instead of an abstract one.

**Refined hypothesis (more measurable than the original):**

> A porter/auditor agent can complete the next Shares-family extension in forge-rs (e.g., the lease-or-equivalent landing) using only code_index queries against pre-indexed theme + forge-rs, at a fraction of the token spend that file-by-file Read exploration would require.

**Why theme makes this experiment statistically meaningful:**

- 766k LOC × 4,893 files is far beyond what fits in any reasonable context window — naive Read-all is not even an option, so the comparison is "with code_index" vs "fail without it."
- For smaller test repos, the variance in token spend is large relative to the indexing cost; the value of pre-indexing is ambiguous. theme is large enough that the value is unambiguous IF code_index delivers; the experiment can produce a clear number.

**Dependency:**

- Requires code_index C++ + Ruby grammar support (filed as a separate bead in ~/src/agent_tools — see "code_index: add tree-sitter-cpp and tree-sitter-ruby grammar support"). forge-rs (Rust) is already indexable.
- Could partially run today against forge-rs alone (Rust → Rust chunking), but the cross-language theme → forge-rs port-comparison workflow needs the grammar work first.

**Suggested first measurement (after grammar bead lands):**

1. Pick one concrete forge-rs work item the operator would land next.
2. Run it twice:
   - Treatment: code_index pre-loaded with theme + forge-rs; agent uses recall queries only, no Read on theme.
   - Baseline: no code_index; agent has Read access to both repos, explores naturally.
3. Cap each at the same wall-clock OR token budget; measure: turns to convergence, total tokens, output quality (operator judgment), and whether the agent re-read files it could have queried.

That produces a single concrete number that informs whether to integrate code_index into mu's session-start flow (mu-fb0 / mu-zh1 territory).

This doesn't change the bead's status (still open, P3, research-test). It tightens the experiment design and ties it to two existing concrete contexts.

title: Pluggable ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits
description:
Introduce two orthogonal traits for the rope-based context architecture:

```rust
trait ProviderRenderer { fn render(rope: &RetainedRope, target: ProjectionTarget) -> ProviderMessages; }
trait CacheStrategy { fn boundaries(rope) -> Vec<CacheBoundary>; fn annotate(messages, boundaries); }
```

ProviderRenderer is per-provider (Anthropic, OpenAI, FauxProvider). CacheStrategy is composable: where to put cache boundaries given the rope. Multiple strategies coexist for A/B testing.

## Scope

In: trait defs in crates/mu-core; AnthropicCacheStrategy (boundary at first volatile retained span; cache_control: ephemeral); NoCacheStrategy (no-op for OpenAI/Faux); AnthropicProviderRenderer; tests.

Out: A/B-testing harness; OpenAI impl; adoption in live agent loop (later bead).

## Why

Provider differences (cache, message format, system prompt placement) are scattered. Centralizing behind two traits makes: adding a provider mechanical; cache strategies A/B testable; rope semantics provider-independent.

## Acceptance

Traits compile with at least one impl each; existing Anthropic paths refactored without regression; workspace tests green.

## References

Full draft: ~/.claude-personal/drafts/mu-bead-2-provider-cache-strategies.md
Blocked-by: mu-qk8
Memories: 3cc7a18c, fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 validation)
Existing: mu-i6j (cache_control work)
close_reason: Foundational half landed (ProviderRenderer + CacheStrategy traits + stub RetainedRope + NoCacheStrategy + FauxProviderRenderer + 17 tests). Provider-specific impls (Anthropic, OpenAI) deferred to follow-on beads.

title: POSTMORTEM: orchestrator writes MORNING_2026-05-19.md after worker + reviewer complete
description:
## Postmortem task

After WORKER + REVIEWER complete (or any stop criterion fires), orchestrator writes `MORNING_2026-05-19.md` at repo root.

## Structure

Per goal-protocol skill's on-stop briefing format, plus protocol-refinement section:

```
# mu: goal session 2026-05-19
## What landed (or didn't)
| Commit/PR | Bead | One-line |
## Process timeline
- T+0:00 — orchestrator filed beads
- T+X:XX — worker dispatched
- T+X:XX — worker produced commit / hit budget
- T+X:XX — reviewer dispatched
- T+X:XX — reviewer verdict
- T+X:XX — PR opened or iteration started
- ...
## Test state
## Goal status
- Tracking bead: complete/partial/blocked
- Each sub-bead: status
## Stop criteria that fired (if any)
## Surprises observed
(Things that didn't match the pre-registered hypothesis)
## Protocol refinements (if any)
Concrete bullet points that should update:
- ~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/SKILL.md
- ~/.claude-personal/skills/goal-protocol/references/*
- specs/process/goal-protocol.md (mu's per-repo overrides)
For each refinement: which evidence justifies it. If the refinement is non-trivial, also file as a bead.
## Cost / turns / wall-clock
- Worker: predicted , actual ; predicted N turns, actual M; predicted T hours, actual S
- Reviewer: same
- Total: same
## Suggested next session
```

## Acceptance

- File exists at `/home/tcovert/src/public_github/mu/MORNING_2026-05-19.md`
- All sections filled
- At least one concrete protocol-refinement suggestion (even if minor)
- If protocol-refinement suggestions are non-trivial: corresponding beads filed in br against the skill or this repo
- Goal tracking bead (this experiment's parent) closed with the file as close_reason reference

## Budget

$3 cap (postmortem is reflection + writing, no compilation)

## Stop criteria

- Postmortem skips a required section — STOP, the structure is the artifact
- Protocol-refinement section is empty AND zero surprises observed — STOP, retro requires AT LEAST one observation worth surfacing (if literally none, that itself is the observation — write 'all predictions matched, no protocol changes needed' and explain why this case is worth noting)

title: mu-config-system: centralized config file + service state persistence (operator-controllable defaults, hierarchies, routing)
description:
A mu-wide config file (probably `~/.config/mu/config.toml`) that holds operator-controllable defaults the daemon reads at startup. Replaces today's pattern of env-vars + code defaults + scattered per-session ad-hoc configuration.

## Operator framing (2026-05-14)

> "we should consider the config file/save state for the service. That way I can pick up where I left off, it'll be possible to resume, any config we have will persist, and things like the judge selection/hierarchy, account use hierarchy/ranking, proxy use - they can all live in config rather than in the code."

The key insight: **decisions that vary by operator preference shouldn't live in source code.** They live in a config file, version-controllable, operator-editable without recompilation, with sensible code defaults if the file is absent.

## What goes in config (initial sections)

```toml
[compaction]
default_policy = "hash-and-summary"          # or "heuristic" or "no-compaction"
trigger_threshold_tokens = 100_000

[compaction.judge]
# Ordered preference list; daemon walks it on each compaction event.
# First entry whose provider+auth is available wins.
ranking = [
  { provider = "openrouter", model = "deepseek-chat", auth = "api_key" },
  { provider = "openrouter", model = "qwen2-7b-instruct", auth = "api_key" },
  { provider = "anthropic", model = "claude-haiku-4-5", auth = "oauth" },
  { provider = "anthropic", model = "claude-haiku-4-5", auth = "api_key" },
]
output_mode = "index_keep"                   # or "hash_keep"
timeout_secs = 60

[providers]
# Per-provider auth ranking. First available wins.
# (Maps to AnthropicProvider / OpenRouterProvider / OpenaiCodexProvider construction.)
anthropic_auth_ranking = ["oauth", "api_key"]
openrouter_auth_ranking = ["api_key"]
codex_auth_ranking = ["oauth"]

[providers.proxy]
url = "http://127.0.0.1:3180"               # claude_proxy, optional
use_for = ["anthropic"]                      # which providers route through proxy

[session]
persist_events_to_disk = true                # mu-upb behavior (already on)
resume_on_daemon_restart = true              # composes with mu-mh4 read-side
state_dir = "~/.local/share/mu"

[ui.tui]
default_provider = "anthropic"
default_model = "claude-opus-4-7"
predicted_next_user_message = false          # mu-skv — when it lands
markdown_render_mode = "rendered"            # mu-mvk — when it lands
color_theme = "moonfly"                      # mu-u8a — when it lands

[budget]
# Soft budgets for operator awareness; hard caps still come from
# --max-budget-usd flags.
api_key_daily_warn_usd = 10
api_key_weekly_warn_usd = 30
```

## Why P2 (not P1)

mu works today without it; env-vars + code defaults are the v1 fallback. But this bead unblocks several P3 follow-ons cleanly:
- mu-kgu.11 (live-judge): needs to know provider/model/auth — that's `[compaction.judge]`
- mu-mh4 (session persistence read-side): needs operator-controllable `state_dir` + resume policy
- mu-skv (predicted-next-user autocomplete): needs an enable/disable toggle
- mu-mvk (markdown render): needs the raw/rendered toggle as a persistent default
- mu-u8a (color polish): needs a theme selector

Without a config home, each of those would invent ad-hoc storage (env-vars, hardcoded paths). With it, they compose cleanly.

## Layered config resolution

In ascending priority:

1. **Code defaults** — baked into the source; always present.
2. **Site config** — `/etc/mu/config.toml` (system-wide, for shared hosts; optional).
3. **Operator config** — `~/.config/mu/config.toml` (per-user; the main one).
4. **Per-daemon override** — CLI flags to `mu serve` (e.g. `--state-dir ...`).
5. **Per-session override** — RPC param (`session.create { provider_override: ... }`).

Each layer overlays on the previous. Operator can confidently edit `~/.config/mu/config.toml` knowing per-session calls can still override for one-offs.

## Scope

### In

- TOML loader at daemon startup (`mu serve`). Uses `toml = "0.8"` (likely already a transitive dep via Cargo or via mu-coding's auth file handling).
- New struct hierarchy in `crates/mu-core/src/config.rs` mirroring the TOML sections.
- `Config::load(paths: &[Path]) -> Result<Config>` walks the layer stack, fails-cleanly if any file is malformed (warn + fall back to defaults; never crash).
- Each existing component that has ad-hoc-config-shaped behavior gets a small wire-up: read its values from the central Config at construction time.
- Tests: TOML round-trip; layered-override behavior; missing-file fallback to defaults; malformed-TOML fallback + warning.

### Out

- Live reload (SIGHUP or file-watch). Reload requires daemon restart in v1.
- Config-via-RPC (`config.get` / `config.set`). v1 is filesystem-only.
- Schema validation library (we'd want one eventually for operator-facing TOML editing; v1 uses serde's built-in field-typing).
- Migration from the current env-var-soup. Both can coexist; env-vars take precedence over config-file values (matches Unix convention).

## Acceptance

- `Config` struct compiles, loads from `~/.config/mu/config.toml`, falls back to code defaults if absent.
- At least three real consumers wired: `[compaction.judge]`, `[session]`, `[ui.tui.default_provider]`.
- Layered-resolution tests cover the standard case + override case.
- `cargo test --workspace` green; `clippy -D warnings` green.
- A documented example `config.toml` in the repo (e.g. `docs/example-config.toml`).
- mu-kgu.11 (live-judge) is the first non-mu-config-system bead that consumes this — its acceptance criteria reference the new config sections.

## Composition

- mu-mh4 (session persistence read-side, open): adopts `[session]` config natively.
- mu-kgu.11 (live-judge wiring): consumes `[compaction.judge]` ranking.
- mu-skv (predicted-next-user): consumes `[ui.tui.predicted_next_user_message]`.
- mu-mvk (markdown), mu-u8a (color), mu-cha (session tree): consume `[ui.tui.*]`.
- mu-kgu.8 (background-compaction worker): consumes `[compaction]` for cooldown/timeout/max-attempts quotas.

Without mu-config-system landing, these all need separate ad-hoc state. With it, they consolidate.

## Provenance

Filed 2026-05-14 after operator surfaced the gap during mu-kgu.11 routing-flexibility discussion. The trigger was "judge selection/hierarchy, account use hierarchy/ranking, proxy use — they can all live in config rather than in the code." mu-kgu.11 will block on this for clean wiring, OR can use env-vars + code defaults as a v0.5 placeholder until config-system lands.
notes:
## Config-as-events / event-sourced config (operator framing, 2026-05-14)

> "If our config become auditable objects, that also means we can probably parse the config and inject the objects into the same event loop and have a config handler process/audit/judge them or whatever. This would also make configuring sub-agents potentially easier."

The capability unification (above) made config carry the same correctness substrate as code authority. This insight extends it: **config CHANGES are events that flow through the same event log as everything else.**

### Event-sourced config model

The TOML file isn't the source of truth — it's an *initial event stream* that the daemon replays at startup. The actual source of truth is the durable event log.

```jsonc
// startup: TOML parsed → emits N events into the log:
{ "kind": "config_set", "path": "compaction.default_policy", "value": "hash-and-summary", "actor": "config-file", "ts": 1778800000 }
{ "kind": "config_set", "path": "compaction.judge.ranking", "value": [...], "actor": "config-file" }
// ...

// runtime: operator changes a value via RPC:
{ "kind": "config_set", "path": "budget.weekly_warn_usd", "value": 50, "actor": "operator:tcovert", "ts": 1778900000 }

// runtime: agent observes Deepseek timeout, proposes demotion:
{ "kind": "config_proposed", "proposal_id": "p1", "path": "compaction.judge.ranking", "new": [...without deepseek], "actor": "agent:mu-self", "reason": "deepseek timed out 3x in 5min" }

// optional operator-approval flow:
{ "kind": "config_approved", "proposal_id": "p1", "approver": "operator:tcovert" }
// → emits the actual config_set
```

The current config state is a **projection** over those events (same shape as transcript-is-projection-over-message-events). No separate config database.

### What this unlocks

1. **Audit history is free**: `session.events --kind config_set --path "compaction.*"` returns the timeline. No bespoke config-history feature.

2. **Replay**: any past config state is reconstructable by replaying events up to time T. Useful for "this used to work" debugging.

3. **A "config-judge" observer**: a process subscribes to `config_proposed` events and decides approve/reject:
   - Capability check (already enforced by the capability layer)
   - Safety check (e.g., budget threshold can't be lowered below ongoing-session consumption)
   - Operator-approval-required check (agent-managed paths may need explicit nod)
   - Same shape as mu-kgu.3's compaction-judge: one observer, narrow scope, audit-logged decisions.

4. **Sub-agent config delegation is just attenuated subscription**: parent issues sub-agent a narrowed `ConfigCapability`; sub-agent gets `config_set` events for paths in its `readable` set delivered as event subscriptions. Sub-agent's local config state is THE PROJECTION of those events. No bespoke per-sub-agent config-bundle handoff.

5. **Cross-daemon config sync via biscuit + event replay** (composes with mu-w4o): daemon A sends biscuit-signed config-event-stream; daemon B verifies + replays. Same mechanism as cross-daemon delegation.

### Architectural shape

This puts mu's config system inside the existing event-sourced substrate. The substrate diagram tightens:

```text
            ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
            │   Event log (typed, append-only)        │
            │   • message events                       │
            │   • tool call/result events              │
            │   • capability events (granted/revoked)  │
            │   • config events (set/proposed/approved)│ ← NEW
            │   • compaction events                    │
            │   • skill activation events              │
            │   • ...                                  │
            └────────────────┬────────────────────────┘
                             │ projects to
              ┌──────────────┼──────────────┬───────────┬─────────┐
              ▼              ▼              ▼           ▼         ▼
           Transcript    Capability      Config       Rope      ...
           (per session) (per session)  (per session) (working state)
```

Every projection is derivable from the event log. The TOML file is one input source among several (CLI flags, RPC calls, agent-proposed changes all also emit events).

### Implementation note: TOML-as-event-stream

At daemon startup:

```rust
let toml_str = std::fs::read_to_string("~/.config/mu/config.toml")?;
let parsed: ConfigToml = toml::from_str(&toml_str)?;
let events: Vec<ConfigEvent> = parsed.into_events("config-file", startup_ts);
for event in events {
    event_log.append(event);  // emits config_set into the rope's event stream
}
let config_projection = ConfigProjection::from_events(event_log.iter_kind("config_set"));
```

The `into_events` step naturally encodes the layered-resolution rules (code defaults emit first, then site, then operator, then CLI flags). The projection's "current value" is whatever the LATEST event for each path says.

### What this means for the v1 implementation

mu-l1z's v1 is now:
1. **Config struct + ConfigEvent enum** (kinds: Set, Proposed, Approved, Rejected, Revoked)
2. **Event-log-append-on-write** — every config mutation emits an event
3. **Projection-based getter** — `config.get(path)` walks the events backward to find the latest valid Set
4. **ConfigCapability** axis on `Capability` for read/write/delegate scope (per the prior section's design)
5. **TOML loader** as an event-emitter, not a state-set
6. **Three demonstrator consumers** (compaction.judge, session.*, ui.tui.*) reading through the projection

The capability + event unification means none of these need bespoke wiring — they compose with existing substrate.

### A separate observer ("config-judge") is now a clean fit

For agent-managed paths, a small daemon-side observer subscribes to `config_proposed` events and emits `config_approved` (or `config_rejected`). v1 can hardcode trivial policies (e.g., "always approve"); future iterations plug in real safety + capability checks. Same pattern as mu-kgu.3's compaction-judge — narrow trait surface, one job.

## Composition (revised)

mu-w4o (biscuit-auth): cross-daemon config sync = sign config-event-stream + transfer + verify + replay.
mu-fb0 (live agent loop, closed): rope substrate the config events flow through. Config projection is one more projection alongside transcript + capability views.
mu-mh4 (session persistence read-side): the same event-log persistence that survives daemon restart for sessions ALSO survives for config events. "Resume from where I left off" includes config state.
mu-pex (metrics framework, P2 ready): config_set events become natural metric source ("how often does the operator change X? how does mu adapt the judge ranking over time?").

## Provenance

Three architectural unifications added 2026-05-14 across one conversation:

1. **Properties → capability semantics** (immutable / operator-runtime / agent-managed / per-session-override = read/write/delegate scopes).
2. **Config = capability axis** (intersect() bounds config; biscuit-token serialization gives cross-daemon).
3. **Config changes = events** (same event log; projection-based current-state; audit/replay/observer all free).

Result: mu's config system is not a separate subsystem. It's another expression of the rope + capability + event substrate already in mu-core. The README story tightens: "one substrate, many projections."
close_reason: v1 foundation landed: Config struct + layered TOML resolution + three demonstrator consumers ([compaction.judge] data-only, [session].state_dir resolves events_dir, [ui.tui].default_provider/model in mu-tui). Capability-axis and event-sourcing extensions deferred to follow-up beads.

title: Bash tool: Box::leak in fmt_allowlist deduplication leaks String per rejected command
description:
## Bug

In crates/mu-coding/src/tools/bash.rs, the allowlist-formatting helper uses:

  if seen.insert(Box::leak(joined.clone().into_boxed_str())) { ... }

Intentionally leaks one String per de-duplication call. The set is rebuilt on every refused-command error message. For a long-lived mu serve with many rejected commands, this is a slow memory leak.

## Fix

Use HashSet<String> and seen.contains(&joined) / seen.insert(joined) instead of seen.insert(Box::leak(...)). Readability win too.

## Source

opus-4-7 review session 3262c036eaca7daa event 194, 'What needs work' item 4.

title: mu-core/protocol: distinguish 'paused between turns' from 'session truly closed' — terminal-state ambiguity
description:
## Problem

`SessionStatus::Done` (and the daemon's session phase `done`) is currently overloaded with two distinct meanings:

1. **The current ask completed** — `end_turn` fired, no streaming in flight, session is waiting for the next user_message. Sending another `ask_session` is the normal way to continue the conversation.
2. **The session is finished forever** — the operator explicitly closed it (e.g. via `close_session`), the provider hit a permanent error, the daemon evicted it, etc. Sending another `ask_session` either fails or silently no-ops.

These are the same wire-level state but have completely different semantics. From the TUI's perspective, a session you sent one prompt to 30 seconds ago looks identical to a session you sent one prompt to a month ago and never came back to.

## Provenance

Surfaced 2026-05-19 during mu-o1y7 phase 3a smoke test. Operator tried `i Hello?` Enter on a faux session that was in `done` state, got no response. Phase 3a added a 'send blocked' marker that fired on any Done session — which the operator correctly identified as wrong, because it would block legitimate session-resume.

Operator analogy (Monty Python S3E37 'Gestures to Indicate Pauses in a Televised Talk'): Mr. Orbiter explains he needs distinct gestures to indicate pause-while-still-speaking vs. fully-finished. mu has only one gesture for both.

## Proposal

### Wire-level

Add a separate terminal state to the session phase enum (or a flag on the row). Candidates:
- `closed` — explicitly retired; `ask_session` returns an error
- `abandoned` — daemon evicted (probably automatic after long idle)
- `crashed` / `errored` — terminal error from the provider; partial transcript preserved

Or one bit: `is_terminal: bool` alongside the existing phase. Simpler, less expressive.

### RPC

`session.close { session_id, reason? }` — moves a session into the terminal state. Composes with mu-770 (session lifecycle epic).

### TUI

Display the terminal-state sessions distinctly (e.g. grayed out in F3 picker; phase glyph changes). Re-add a 'send blocked' marker for those specific cases.

## Composes with

- **mu-770** — session.archive / session.restore / session.list_archived; archive ≈ 'persist but hide.' The terminal-state distinction is an underlying primitive for that.
- **mu-w73n** — session.delete_persistent (mu-u1ld follow-up); only makes sense if you can identify which sessions are terminal.

## Out of scope

- Auto-detect that a session has been idle 'too long' and mark it terminal. Initial pass should be operator-driven only.
- UI for the close action — defer to mu-770.

## Acceptance (rough — refine in implementation)

- Protocol type carries a distinct terminal indicator
- `session.close` RPC exists
- TUI renders terminal-state sessions visually distinct
- `ask_session` against a terminal session returns a typed error
- mu-o1y7's TUI can re-add a 'send blocked' marker, scoped to actual terminal sessions
notes:
## 2026-05-19 design refinement (operator)

The 'distinct terminal state' framing in the original description may be over-engineered. Simpler primitive: **the Done event already has a timestamp; the elapsed time between Done and the next ask IS the signal**.

- Done emitted at t0
- Next \`ask_session\` arrives at t1
- t1 - t0 = the operator's natural classifier:
  - Seconds → continuing a turn (the assistant just finished talking)
  - Minutes → continuing the conversation
  - Hours / next day → coming back after a break
  - Weeks / months → reactivating an old session

No new terminal-state flag needed at the protocol level. The data is already there in the event log. What's missing is **surfacing it in the UI**:

### TUI affordances

- In F2 / F3 session listings, replace 'done' with 'last active 3 days ago' / 'last active 2m ago' — a human-readable elapsed time. Glyph stays the same; the secondary info changes.
- On F3 entry to a Done session where t_since_done > some threshold (1 hour? 1 day?), surface a one-line marker in scrollback: \`─── resuming session — last active 3 days ago ───\` so the operator has context for the continuity gap.
- F3 picker tooltip / sub-line: show last-active so the operator can pick by recency.

### Doesn't replace mu-770 (session lifecycle)

This time-based approach handles 'paused vs. resumed' continuum cleanly. But there are still cases where a session is genuinely terminal:
- Operator explicitly chose to retire it (\`session.close\`)
- Daemon hit a permanent provider error
- Compaction policy decided the session is over

Those want mu-770's \`archive\` / \`close\` RPCs + a real terminal indicator. So this bead now covers: **surface elapsed-time-since-Done as a first-class affordance**. The terminal-state distinction is mu-770's job.

### Acceptance (refined)

- F2/F3 listings show 'last active <relative time>' instead of just the 'done' glyph (for done sessions)
- F3 entry to long-stale Done session emits a context marker in scrollback
- Picker sub-line surfaces last-active for ranking by recency
- Timestamp is already in the event log — just plumb it through

## Provenance

2026-05-19 conversation: 'if the Done has a timestamp at time of creation, just reading the next message will give an indication if it was just part of a conversation, picked back up after a weekend, or a year.' Cleaner than the original 'add a separate terminal state' framing — uses existing data instead of inventing new state.

title: TUI: consume OperatorView rendering in mu-pex / mu-jb3
description:
Follow-on to mu-ovl (closed by xszxyzyy 8920297c). The `FauxProviderRenderer` now produces distinct OperatorView output per SpanKind (`crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:221+`). The TUI surfaces in `mu-pex` (system_prompt indicator) and `mu-jb3` (transcript pane) currently render the agent-side message stream directly. They should source OperatorView output from a `ProviderRenderer::render(rope, OperatorView)` call instead.

## Scope

IN:
- Identify the rendering site(s) in `mu-pex` / `mu-jb3` (and any siblings) that currently show transcript content.
- Route them through `FauxProviderRenderer` (or whichever production renderer is in scope by then — mu-bn4 / mu-3aa land later) with `ProjectionTarget::OperatorView`.
- For tool-call / tool-result / skill-activation / memory-injection / compaction / file-load spans, surface the OperatorView badge / summary / collapsible inline.
- Drill-down: clicking / focusing a collapsed projection should reveal the underlying span content (AgentView) in a side pane or modal — this is the 'operator can see what the agent actually saw' affordance from spec line 643.
- Tests: snapshot-style assertions that the TUI's rendered string for a known rope matches OperatorView output, not AgentView.

OUT:
- New OperatorView projection shapes (those are mu-dt2 scope — already landed).
- Cache-strategy concerns (mu-ktq orthogonal trait).
- Production renderer adoption in the live agent loop (mu-fb0).

## Acceptance

- TUI pane(s) that previously echoed AgentView content now display the OperatorView projection for SpanKind variants where the two differ.
- Original conversational variants (System / User / Assistant) still render verbatim.
- A drill-down affordance exists (even if minimal) to surface the AgentView content for a collapsed span.
- `cargo test --workspace` green at the closing commit.

## References

- Closed parent: mu-ovl (Agent-view vs operator-view projections from the rope)
- Sibling: mu-dt2 (closed) — projection rules used here
- TUI beads: mu-pex (system_prompt indicator), mu-jb3 (transcript pane), mu-62s, mu-n48
- Spec: \`specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md:614-644\` (agent-view vs operator-view projections); line 643 (drill-down)
- Renderer site: \`crates/mu-core/src/context/renderer.rs:221+\` (FauxProviderRenderer::render_content)

## Notes

mu-ovl's bead body explicitly deferred TUI integration to a follow-on; this is that follow-on. It is independent of mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption) — that path threads the rope through the agent loop's prompt builder; this path threads it through the TUI's display layer.

title: mu-phl v0 Phase E — thread cwd through mu CLI entry points (mu ask)
description:
Step 6 of mu-phl v0. Threads the operator's invocation cwd through to the daemon so recall fires for the right project.

## What

`crates/mu-coding/src/bin/mu.rs` ask path: set `cwd: Some(std::env::current_dir()?)` on the `CreateSessionRequest`. (Similar for any other client entry points that build session-creation requests.)

## Why

Without this, the daemon falls back to its own process cwd, which is wherever `mu serve` was launched from — usually not where the operator is running `mu ask`. Memory recall would key off the wrong project.

## Acceptance

- `mu ask` from any directory threads cwd correctly.
- Daemon log / `ContextAssembly` event for the assembled rope shows recall keyed to the invocation cwd.
- One commit with `## Files` block and `(closes <this-bead>)`.

## Blocked by

- mu-phl v0 Phase D (step 5) — uses the new `cwd: Option<PathBuf>` field on `CreateSessionRequest`.
close_reason: Landed (Phase E) on feat/mu-phl-v0. ask.rs threads std::env::current_dir().ok() through create_session helper to CreateSessionRequest.cwd. 16/3 line change in one file. 769 tests pass; clippy clean. Daemon-side recall now scopes to the operator's actual cwd, not the daemon's process cwd. Phase F (smoke test) is unblocked.

title: Implement mu-037 phase 1: single-daemon peer.hello + mailbox
description:
Spec at specs/mu-037-peer-discovery-mailbox.md (jj ozyprmqn cc380197). Phase 1 only — single-daemon scope.

Two delegate sessions in the same daemon can exchange messages without going through the parent. Wire surface:
- peer.hello / peer.reply with capability advertisement + negotiation
- mailbox.post / mailbox.list / mailbox.consume
- New MailboxMessage event-log entries

Reuses mu-033 Capability entirely — peer handles ARE attenuated capabilities. No new authorization machinery.

Estimated ~1 week. Phase 2 (cross-daemon same-machine via unix socket + filesystem registry) is a follow-up issue.
close_reason: Phase 1 landed via PR #43. Wire surface (peer.hello + mailbox.post/list/consume + session.mailbox_message notification), MailboxMessagePosted/Consumed event-log variants, per-session MailboxState + PeerHandle registry, 6 unit + 3 integration tests. Opaque-token peer handles (intentional divergence from Capability-axis framing). PeerDiscovery trait+impl deferred to follow-up bead. Authorization is handle-required even in same-daemon (no trust-local shortcut).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Architecture sketch for Phase 1 (2026-05-14, claude-personal session).

## Locked

- **Peer handle = opaque token, not a `Capability`-axis extension.** v1 carries `{peer_session_id, allowed_methods: HashSet<String>, expires_at_unix_ms, max_calls_remaining}`. The spec's "peer handles ARE attenuated capabilities" framing is right at the substrate level but conflates two surfaces: existing `Capability` bounds tool/aws/autonomy invocation; peer handles bound RPC-method invocation against another session. Cleaner to ship the primitive first, unify in Phase 2 when cross-daemon authz makes the distinction load-bearing.
- **`peer.reply` is the response shape for `peer.hello`, not a second RPC method.** Single request/response, no challenge round-trip loop in Phase 1.

## Wire surface (mu-core/src/protocol.rs additions)

- `peer.hello`: `PeerHelloRequest{from, want} → PeerHelloResponse::{Accepted{peer_handle, allowed_methods, expires_at_unix_ms}, Denied{reason}}`
- `mailbox.post`: `{to_session_id, from, kind, subject, body: Value, expires_at_unix_ms?} → {posted, seq}`
- `mailbox.list`: `{session_id, since_seq?, include_consumed} → {messages: Vec<MailboxMessageView>}`
- `mailbox.consume`: `{session_id, seqs: Vec<u64>} → {consumed_count}`

## State model

**Event-sourced.** Two new EventPayload variants in mu-core/src/event_log.rs:
- `MailboxMessagePosted{seq, from_daemon_id, from_session_id, kind, subject, body, expires_at_unix_ms}` — append-only post record
- `MailboxMessageConsumed{seq}` — append-only consumption mark

`mailbox.list` is a projection: scan event log, return posted-minus-consumed. Same pattern as session-status derivation in serve/discovery/mod.rs. No state duplication.

**Per-session registry additions** in serve/sessions.rs::SessionState:
- `mailbox_next_seq: Arc<AtomicU64>` — monotonic seq generator (write coordination)
- `peer_handles_issued: Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, PeerHandle>>>` — handles this session has handed out, keyed by token

## Dispatch wiring (serve/dispatch.rs, 4 new arms)

- `peer.hello`: target session policy (v1 hardcoded: accept same-daemon peers with `allowed_methods = {mailbox.post}`); issue token; return Accepted.
- `mailbox.post`: append `MailboxMessagePosted` with next seq.
- `mailbox.list`: scan target's event log; project posts minus consumed.
- `mailbox.consume`: append `MailboxMessageConsumed` events.

## Open (my leans, not locked)

- **Single-daemon trust.** Phase 1 trusts the local daemon — `mailbox.post`/`.list`/`.consume` don't require a peer handle for in-process callers. `peer.hello` is mostly ceremonial in this phase, exercised only by the demonstrator test. Real teeth in Phase 2 (cross-daemon).
- **Wire notification on post.** Lean: emit `session.mailbox_message` notification from the forwarder when `MailboxMessagePosted` lands. ~10 LOC; lets Phase 4 TUI subscribe without a follow-up wire spec.

## Acceptance demonstrator

Two delegate sessions A and B in the same daemon: A → `mailbox.post` to B; B → `mailbox.list` sees the message; B → `mailbox.consume`; B re-lists with `include_consumed=false` → message gone.

## Composition

- Builds on mu-031 (delegate sessions provide the two-session-in-one-daemon scenario)
- Builds on mu-iwq Phase D's event-log + tracker patterns (sessions registry mutations)
- Does NOT consume mu-033 Capability for peer handles in v1 — see "Locked" #1
- Phase 4 (F9 mailbox view) unblocked by the wire notification lean above

comments.author: tcovert

title: mu-clip-tui-deadcode: address 3 dead_code lints in mu-tui (pre-existing, surfaced by CI -D warnings)
description:
Three dead_code warnings in mu-tui that block CI under -D warnings. All look like placeholders for in-progress TUI work; per-item decision needed (suppress with #[allow] + reason comment, delete, or wire up).

## Items

1. **`ProviderStatusSnapshot.bytes_received`** field (`crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:140-143`) — derived Clone/Debug but field is never read. Probably for the metrics framework (mu-pex). Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a comment pointing to mu-pex.
2. **`MuClient.child`** field (`crates/mu-tui/src/mu_client.rs:55`) — child process handle held for Drop semantics (subprocess kill on owner-drop). Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a Drop-discipline comment; the field IS used (held for RAII), clippy just can't see it.
3. **`MuClient::ping` and `MuClient::close`** methods (`crates/mu-tui/src/mu_client.rs:289-295`) — placeholder for protocol features. Decision: likely `#[allow(dead_code)]` with a TODO pointing to the relevant feature bead, OR delete if truly not coming.

## Acceptance

- All 3 dead_code warnings in mu-tui addressed (suppressed with comment OR deleted OR wired up)
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` advances past mu-tui
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision omulvzmu. 3 #[allow(dead_code)] annotations with rationale: bytes_received (mu-pex prep), child (RAII handle for close), ping+close (planned TUI exit-path wire-up). 361 tests green; warnings 10 → 5.

title: mu-tui: status-bar glyph legend (sys: indicator and similar)
description:
## Problem

The `sys:` glyph in the top-right of the status bar (✓ when default system
prompt set, · when not — line 1953 of mu-tui/src/main.rs, from mu-62s)
isn't discoverable. User report: "sys: — not sure what it's for."

## Proposal

Surface the meaning. Lightest-touch options:

(a) Press-and-hold key (e.g., `?` over the status bar) to show a legend
    popup explaining each glyph
(b) F1 → "status bar legend" section listing each indicator
(c) Brief hint in `:help` output

A direct contextual expansion would be too noisy in the status bar itself
(e.g., `sys:✓ (default system prompt set; :system_prompt to view)`), but
the legend popup or help entry achieves the same with on-demand access.

## Acceptance

- User can determine what `sys:` (and similar glyphs) mean without
  reading source code
- Discovery path doesn't require knowing the right command in advance

## Cross-refs

- mu-62s (added the indicator)
- Provenance: TUI feedback (mu daemon f3845422 session-2, 2026-05-15)

title: GOAL 2026-05-19: delegate-then-review experiment — multi-line prompt as target for protocol iteration
description:
## Tracking bead for /goal session 2026-05-19 (overnight)

Methodology experiment. The PRIMARY DELIVERABLE is **a working delegation-then-review-then-postmortem loop**, with multi-line-prompt-growing-upward as the example task.

The CODE deliverable is secondary. If the delegation infrastructure produces a usable PR but the methodology doesn't iterate cleanly, the experiment is a partial success. If the methodology lessons are sharp but the PR has bugs, the experiment is still useful.

## Crew

| Role | Backend | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Orchestrator | claude-code (this session) | Sets up, monitors, PR review, postmortem |
| Worker | claude-code via `agent-router --auth claude-oauth` | Implements |
| Reviewer | gpt-5.5-codex via `agent-router --auth codex-oauth --tools read,grep` | Reviews worker's output pre-PR |

(Pot isolation deferred — mu-fpgd / claude-in-pot is still blocked. Working without it tonight.)

## Sub-beads (tagged `goal:2026-05-19:<this-bead-id>`)

1. **Implementation**: multi-line prompt growing upward in F3 inline mode (filed below)
2. **Reviewer dispatch**: gpt-5.5 reads worker's commit + spec, returns approve/iterate verdict (filed below)
3. **Postmortem**: orchestrator reviews PR, writes postmortem on protocol + output, captures protocol-refinement suggestions (filed below)

Each sub-bead has stop criteria + acceptance + budget cap.

## Hypothesis (pre-registered)

- Worker can implement the multi-line prompt within ~$20 budget (~200 LOC at conservative 10:1)
- Reviewer can return a usable verdict within ~$2 budget
- Postmortem will surface 1-3 protocol refinement candidates
- End-to-end wall-clock: ~2-3 hours including the model latency for both worker + reviewer

## Stop criteria (in addition to default seven)

- Worker exceeds budget cap → halt + postmortem fires on partial work
- Reviewer's verdict is 'iterate' but worker hits 2 iteration cycles without convergence → halt + postmortem on the methodology, not the code
- Anything touches files outside crates/mu-tui/src/ → halt (scope creep tripwire)

## Anti-success modes the experiment can detect

- Worker silently produces a HollowCommit (claim mismatches diff) — forensics classifier would catch this; orchestrator verifies via verify-claims gate
- Worker silently produces a NarrativeNoAction (commit but no functional change) — verify-claims + cargo test catches
- Reviewer rubber-stamps without reading — protocol check: reviewer's output must cite file:line; orchestrator audits this

## Post-experiment artifact

`MORNING_2026-05-19.md` at repo root with:
- What landed (PR # + commit list)
- Process timeline (worker dispatch → review → iterations → PR)
- Surprises observed
- Suggested protocol refinements (concrete bullet points; if any, also file as beads against the goal-protocol SKILL.md)
- Cost / turns / wall-clock actuals vs predictions

## Provenance

Operator (2026-05-19, pre-sleep): 'take a task, like the expanding prompt box, and delegate to the work crew, set the goals, stop criteria, success, etc. After gpt reviews the code and says it's ready for PR. You review the PR. Then, you can do a postmortem on the process and output, and think about what you might change to the protocol. And repeat. Science with the system.'

title: Split mu-tui/src/main.rs (4530 LOC) into app/* + render/* modules
description:
## Problem

crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs is 4530 lines. The single App struct has ~35 fields. The App impl handles input, command-palette dispatch, RPC calls, render coordination, AND streaming-text accumulation. Tests at the bottom add ~800 lines. AGENTS.md guidance: '~1000 lines is the split point.'

## Proposed splits (per opus review)

- app/state.rs — App struct + field initializers
- app/input.rs — key handlers grouped by mode (Normal, Picker, ApprovalModal, Editing)
- app/notification.rs — handle_notification + per-event variants (session.provider_status, session.assistant_text_finalized, mailbox.delivered, etc.)
- render/header.rs, render/tabs.rs, render/command_center.rs, render/session_detail.rs, render/usage.rs, render/events.rs, render/modal.rs
- mu_client.rs — already a separate file
- tests move to app/tests/ or src/tests/

Target: <600 LOC per file.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'What needs work' item 3. Operator awareness: mu-nk3 already did the analogous split for crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs.

Per iter-10 reviewer (and prior iter-8 self-review), candidate extraction points worth tracking when this refactor happens:

title: mu-cap-aws follow-on: intersect_aws_sets HashMap O(n+m) + set-level INV-1 regression test
description:
Two small follow-on hardenings to mu-f5o (commit 773f0f64):

1. **`intersect_aws_sets` O(n+m) optimization** — pre-index the right-hand set by name into a HashMap for O(1) lookup instead of the current O(n*m) nested-loop scan. Practical N is small but the cleaner algorithm is easy and obvious (operator preference: sound-engineering > waiting-for-it-to-be-a-problem).

2. **Set-level INV-1 regression test for both-Some-Some drop path** — assert that when same-name caps have both-Some session policies (deferred to None per AwsCapability::intersect), the cap is dropped from intersect_aws_sets' output AND the set-level inclusion property holds (result ⊆ a AND result ⊆ b by name). Peace-of-mind: catches a future regression that might "fall back to keeping the original cap" on None.

## Scope

IN:
- Replace the nested loop in `intersect_aws_sets` (`crates/mu-core/src/capability.rs:181-195`) with HashMap-indexed lookup.
- Add one test `intersect_aws_sets_preserves_inv1_even_when_some_pairs_drop` adjacent to the existing `intersect_aws_sets_drops_unmatched_names` test.

OUT: anything else — no spec change (algorithm change doesn't alter the documented semantics; INV-1 property already documented).

## Acceptance

- intersect_aws_sets uses HashMap<&str, &AwsCapability> for O(1) name lookup
- Existing tests (intersect_aws_sets_drops_unmatched_names, intersect_aws_sets_is_narrowing_only_property) still pass
- New test asserts: set with both-Some-Some pair drops that name, output names ⊆ a.names AND ⊆ b.names
- cargo test --workspace green
- One commit closes this bead
close_reason: Landed in jj revision lnytzpuu. intersect_aws_sets pre-indexes the right-hand HashSet by name → O(n+m). New regression test intersect_aws_sets_preserves_inv1_even_when_some_pairs_drop asserts set-level INV-1 holds across the both-Some-Some drop path. cargo test --workspace green at 361 tests.

title: ContextAssembly v2: per-segment source-map
description:
Per specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md. ContextAssembly v1 records counts + provider info; v2 records the full source-map: for each message in the assembled prompt, which source event(s) it came from and what range.

Enables 'why was this in context' (the F4 Context Inspector view from mu-ui-mockups-2026-05-10.md).

Bigger lift — needs schema work + agent loop participation to actually track the source-event mapping during assembly. Write when the inspector UI work creates real demand.

title: mu-file-watch: implement rope source-change detection via kqueue/inotify (watchman as candidate impl)
description:
The spec amendment at `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` (section 'Span source-change detection via OS file watches', lines 681+) describes the design: spans that reference file content (`FileLoad` SpanKind, landed in mu-1n3) should be invalidated/rehydrated when the underlying file changes. No implementation bead existed; this fills the gap.

Operator preference (2026-05-14): use libevent/kqueue/epoll directly for the primitive. If we later need cross-platform with reliability beyond what direct kqueue gives, **watchman** (Facebook's, used by jj) is the candidate higher-level abstraction.

## Scope

IN:
- Per-FileLoad-span watch registration when the span enters the rope
- OS-native watcher: kqueue on FreeBSD/macOS, inotify on Linux, ReadDirectoryChangesW on Windows
- Watcher emits change events; rope handles via the policy/signal separation already specced (lines 701-707)
- Optional: watchman backend behind a feature flag for environments where the OS-native path has issues

OUT:
- Eager rehydration (re-read every changed file immediately). The signal/policy separation says: watcher emits 'changed' signal; policy decides whether to rehydrate (could be immediate, debounced, or lazy-on-next-projection).
- Watching directories or globs. v1 watches specific files referenced by FileLoad spans.
- Cross-machine file sync (NFS quirks etc.).

## Acceptance

- New FileLoad span registers a watcher on its source path
- File modification fires a 'changed' signal on the rope
- Test: write a file, FileLoad into rope, modify the file, observe the rope emits the change signal
- Policy stub (initially: log + mark stale; full rehydration in a follow-on bead)
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Open design questions

1. **Watcher lifetime**: one global watcher per session, or per-rope, or per-span? Lean: per-session, multiplexing all file watches; cheaper than per-span.
2. **Watchman vs direct kqueue**: start direct; switch to watchman only if scale problems surface (watchman has its own state, runs as a daemon; direct kqueue has zero infrastructure cost).
3. **Cross-platform abstraction**: probably the `notify` crate from crates.io (well-maintained, abstracts kqueue/inotify/Windows). watchman as opt-in fallback.

## References

- Spec: `specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md` lines 681+ ('Span source-change detection via OS file watches')
- mu-1n3 / mu-ovl: FileLoad SpanKind variant (landed)
- watchman: https://facebook.github.io/watchman/
- notify crate: https://crates.io/crates/notify
- Operator note 2026-05-14: 'I'm fine with libevent/kqueue/epoll for monitoring the files; if we find we have more to use, watchman is a tool that seems to work quite well for jj.'

title: AuthState mutex poisoning produces lying Accepted response
description: On poisoned Arc<Mutex<AuthState>> lock in outcome_to_response (dispatch.rs:1438-1451), AuthExchangeResponse::Accepted is returned while AuthState stays Unauthenticated. Liar response. Harmless under mu-yox (no protected RPC consumes AuthState yet); becomes operationally ugly under mu-fnn enforcement. Fix: switch Arc<Mutex<>> to parking_lot::Mutex (non-poisoning) or treat PoisonError as auth-failure. Surfaced by mu-yox implementer envelope notes; confirmed by codex review (minor M2). Provenance: codex-review-mu-yox-output.txt.
close_reason: Merged in PR #67 — handles poisoned-lock case in outcome_to_response; returns Denied{MalformedExchange} instead of lying Accepted.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reopened: Audit drift: bead closed citing PR #48 (efc209d) but neither exists in main. Real work is orphan nmvprymr efc209d (now rebased to 36b81bca). Recovery in progress 2026-05-17.

title: bash tool: add B-7 (output-cap) test — currently missing from bash.rs tests
Session persistence across daemon restart
description:
Today sessions live in mu serve's in-memory Sessions map. When mu serve exits (TUI quits → stdin closes → transport exits → sessions drops), every session and its event log are lost.

For a daily-driver TUI that's used across many work sessions, that's a real limitation: you can't 'come back to' the conversation you had yesterday.

What's needed:
  - Event-log persistence (SQLite or JSONL — the event_log.rs design doc references both)
  - SessionDiscovery (mu-038) backend that reads the persisted logs on startup, resurrects SessionInfo entries (the agent loop doesn't need to be re-spawned for a 'cold' session — it spawns on the next ask_session)
  - Cap on retention (per-session age limit? per-session size limit? user choice)
  - Capability state persistence (a session with attenuated capability should keep that on cold reload)

Mentioned as deferred work in mu-036 spec (autonomous loops). Write its own spec when implementation pressure builds — likely after mu-035 Phase B/C/D and mu-037 phase 1 are done.

Workaround for now: keep mu serve running detached; have the TUI connect-to-existing rather than spawn-new. That's not implemented but is a much smaller piece of work than full persistence.
