title: bare-equivalent boot mode for mu (minimal-context boot for delegates and sub-sessions)
description:
Implements a minimal-context boot mode for mu, equivalent to claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` flag. Memory `fb5cd5be`.

## Motivation

Today every mu session boots with the full operator default: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory context injection, plugin sync, hooks. Most of this is wrong for delegate / sub-session use — a delegate that exists to answer one well-scoped question doesn't need the operator's accumulated memory or the hook configuration.

claude-code 2.1.139's `--bare` mode is the same idea: a different *initial retention set* (per the rope amendment in mu-qk8). The rope architecture says this should be declarative — a profile property, not a flag.

## Scope

In:
- SessionBootMode { Full | Bare } (or as a BootProfile carrying which spans to load).
- Bare mode skips: CLAUDE.md auto-discovery, auto-memory injection, plugin/hook sync.
- Bare mode keeps: tool registry, capability, transport.
- Plumbing: create_session and delegate_session accept the boot mode; default is Full for explicit user-driven creates, Bare for delegates by default (with override).
- Tests: a bare session has no CLAUDE.md spans and no auto-memory in its initial retained set.

Out:
- Per-source granularity (skip CLAUDE.md but keep hooks, etc.) — start with one coarse switch; refine if usage shows it's needed.
- Operator-view UI for boot-mode selection — assume CLI / RPC param for now.

## Acceptance

* Boot mode selectable via API (create_session + delegate_session).
* Bare-mode session's initial retained span set is observably smaller (test).
* Default for delegate_session is Bare; create_session default stays Full.
* No regression in existing Full-mode behavior.

## References

- Memory: fb5cd5be (claude-code 2.1.139 mapping)
- Architecture: rope amendment in specs/architecture/event-sourced-context.md, Cache-boundary alignment section (the --bare interpretation as a different initial retention set).
- Companions: mu-ikz (authority tier), mu-hlf (file isolation) — together these three constitute the 2.1.139 profile / authority feature mapping.

title: mu-context-ulimit: soft/hard context caps with judge-mediated raise requests
description:
Exploration bead. Treat context size as a **bounded capability with soft/hard thresholds**, where the agent must request elevation past soft, and an independent **judge** (not the agent) evaluates whether elevation is warranted.

## Motivation

Context rot is empirical: model performance degrades well before nominal max context, and the degradation isn't graceful. Anthropic's auto-compaction target of ~150k for Opus reflects this; past that point attention quality drops measurably. Today mu compacts at a threshold (mu-kgu.4) but treats context size as a *budget* managed silently. Reframing it as a *capability with soft and hard caps* surfaces the cost and creates a decision point.

## Design sketch (ulimit-style)

- **soft cap** — per (provider, model) calibrated to the degradation knee (e.g. ~150k Opus, ~80k Sonnet, ~40k Haiku). Hitting soft triggers compaction policy (the mu-kgu integration point).
- **hard cap** — absolute ceiling (nominal max minus safety margin). mu refuses to grow context past hard regardless of agent request.
- **raise request** — between soft and hard, the agent may emit `session.request_context_raise { current_tokens, requested_cap, claimed_reason }`. The supervisor / policy decides.
- **judge-mediated decision** — the request is NOT decided by the agent's self-assessment. Instead, a separate judge call (reusing the mu-kgu.3 HashAndSummaryPolicy + mu-kgu.11 ProviderJudge plumbing) independently evaluates: "given the current rope, is the request justified, or is X tokens droppable instead?"

### Judge verdict shape

```
enum CapRaiseVerdict {
  Approve { recommended_cap: usize, reason: String },
  Deny { recommended_compact: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
  ApproveAndCompact { recommended_cap: usize, also_drop: CompactionDirective, reason: String },
}
```

The third option (compact-and-raise) is the most honest answer when context genuinely contains both load-bearing and droppable spans.

## Composition with existing substrates

- **mu-kgu (compaction substrate)** — soft cap triggers the existing CompactionPolicy. Same machinery. New input: the judge can be asked "is this compactable?" separately from "compact it now."
- **mu-fvy0 (accountable runtime)** — \"raise cap\" is a CapabilityKind axis. Operator can grant blanket raise authority to a session, or require per-request approval. Judge verdict is evidence; capability holder decides.
- **mu-035 (provider_status)** — a new `ProviderStatusKind::JudgeEvaluatingCapRequest` sub-kind so the operator sees the evaluation happening in real time.
- **mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i)** — every (request, judge verdict, operator decision, downstream outcome) is a TaskTelemetry datapoint. Over time the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measures whether judge-approved raises correlate with better task outcomes vs judge-denied + compacted, vs no-request baselines. Empirical loop for tuning the policy.

## Open questions (decide before implementing)

1. **Per-model soft caps**: yes (Opus 4.7 ≠ Sonnet 4.6 ≠ Haiku 4.5 ≠ GPT-5.5). Where do calibrated values live — config file, hardcoded const, telemetry-derived? Probably a config table seeded with vendor defaults, refined from observed telemetry.
2. **Hysteresis**: once raised, when do we downgrade? Auto-downgrade when usage drops under 0.8×soft for N turns? Sticky-for-session? Decide based on telemetry of how often raised sessions stay near the new ceiling vs settle back down.
3. **Judge model selection**: cheap-small (Haiku 4.5, ~$0.005/eval) is ideal — fast, focused, parallel to the main turn. But the judge's accuracy matters for the loop. Calibration question.
4. **Per-request capability vs blanket capability**: per-request is more accountable, blanket is less ceremony. Probably both should be possible, with the default being per-request and operators able to grant blanket per-session.
5. **Failure modes of the judge**: what if the judge itself hallucinates? Falls back to default-deny? Default-approve? The hollow-commit / lying-state classifier (mu-8alb) gives us a way to detect judge errors retrospectively but not prevent them in-flight.

## Acceptance (when this becomes implementable)

- Soft/hard cap fields on session config, per-(provider,model) defaults
- `session.request_context_raise` wire event + policy that intercepts it
- `CapRaiseJudge` consumer of existing JudgeAdapter, with a new prompt schema
- Three verdict types correctly produced and respected
- Telemetry envelope captures the full request→verdict→outcome chain
- Spec'd in `specs/mu-NNN-context-ulimit.md`

## Not implementing now

This is an exploration / vision bead. Implementing requires mu-kgu (done), mu-fvy0-telemetry (mu-5g7i — not yet started), and a config-system pass for per-(provider,model) tables. Worth filing as a coherent target so the substrate pieces are designed with this in mind.

## Provenance

Operator observation at 2026-05-16: "was looking at your context ctx:45%... /compact tries to get under 150k... context rot... should LRU keep at 150k? or let it go to max. Then I thought of ulimit and soft and hard caps." Plus follow-up: "we already have a judge function. That might be a good place for an independent assessment."

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Refinements 2026-05-16 (operator)

Two structural changes from the initial sketch:

### 1. The raise-request is fire-and-forget from the agent's side

The agent does NOT block waiting for a verdict. It emits `session.request_context_raise` as a signal and continues with whatever context it has. The judge + supervisor process asynchronously. This removes the "synchronous capability gate" complication and matches the mu-kgu.8 background-compaction-worker pattern — the model keeps working, mitigation happens in parallel.

Consequences:
- The agent loop never stalls on a cap request
- The verdict may come AFTER the current turn completes (still useful: informs the NEXT turn's cap)
- The judge can fire background compaction directly when it decides "drop these spans"; the cap-raise flow becomes a unified "context-pressure response" pipeline

### 2. Judge verdicts are recorded, always

Every verdict goes to the event log as a durable EventPayload variant (and into the mu-5g7i telemetry envelope). This is non-negotiable even when nothing acts on the verdict — the recording is what lets the analytics layer (mu-8ypx) measure judge accuracy over time. Without records there is no feedback loop.

### 3. The judge is pluggable

The JudgeAdapter trait from mu-kgu.11 is the extension point. Operators can plug in:
- A cheap-small judge (Haiku 4.5) — default, fast, low-cost
- A same-family judge (Opus 4.7 evaluating its own context) — more expensive, possibly more accurate, possibly more biased
- A domain-specific judge (custom prompt for specific workloads — e.g. preserve all type definitions, drop file contents older than 5 turns)
- A composite judge (multiple judges, vote)
- A null judge (always-approve or always-deny, for testing or specific policies)

Pluggability means experimentation is cheap — operators can A/B different judges and let the telemetry layer compare outcomes.

### Composite picture

```
agent: ...mid-turn, context approaching soft cap...
  -> emit session.request_context_raise (fire-and-forget)
agent: ...continues turn with current context...

(in parallel)
supervisor: receive raise request
  -> invoke pluggable Judge::evaluate(rope, request_envelope)
  -> Judge returns: Approve | Deny | ApproveAndCompact | ScheduleBackgroundCompact
  -> Record verdict in event log + telemetry sink
  -> Act:
     - Approve: raise soft cap for next turn
     - Deny: no action; verdict recorded for analytics
     - ApproveAndCompact: schedule mu-kgu.8 background worker AND raise cap
     - ScheduleBackgroundCompact: dispatch worker, don't raise

agent: ...completes turn, next turn sees new cap and/or compacted rope...
```

The agent never knows it asked. The agent just operates with whatever context shape it has now. The judge's accuracy is empirically measurable retrospectively, which is the only honest way to tune it.

title: aws_recon runner must enforce subprocess timeout
description: Follow-up to PR #29 review. The runner-backed aws_recon path must not rely solely on the sandbox script honoring --call-timeout; mu-coding needs an outer subprocess timeout that kills the child if the runner hangs. Preserve live-recon semantics where call_timeout_secs is per AWS call, so add/derive an overall runner timeout rather than wedging the daemon.
close_reason: completed

title: forensics: add SkippedTest outcome class — detect +#[ignore] in diff
description:
Sub-bead 2 of mu-uvsq (overnight 2026-05-19).

## Scope

Add a new `Outcome::SkippedTest` discriminator to forensics.rs. Detect commits that add `#[ignore]` annotations to existing tests as a means of bypassing test failures rather than fixing them. Per-repo rule #1: `No #[ignore] shortcuts unless explicitly authorized.`

## Discriminator order

Goes AFTER hollow_commit (more severe — hollow is dishonest output, SkippedTest is process violation) but BEFORE CI-based discriminators (a SkippedTest + CI-green commit still classifies as SkippedTest, because the green CI is masking the disabled tests).

## Detection

Heuristic: scan the commit diff for additions matching `+#[ignore]` or `+#[ignore(` (allowing the variant with a justification string). Heuristic — not a parser, but stable enough since `#[ignore]` is a fixed token.

Probable confidence (not Definite — there are legitimate cases like ignoring tests that depend on external services in CI; the discriminator should fire and let operator/analytics decide).

## Inputs

`CommitInfo` already carries `diff_files: Vec<String>`. Need a new field carrying the raw diff text (or a per-file list of added lines) so the discriminator can scan for `+#[ignore]`. Smallest viable: add `added_lines: Vec<String>` to `CommitInfo`. Caller (the analytics/backfill layer) is responsible for populating it from `git diff` output.

If wiring the caller is out of scope: stub the field, gate the discriminator on `added_lines.is_empty()` so it cleanly no-ops until plumbing lands. Sub-bead is still useful because the OUTCOME exists, fixture tests pass, future caller work is unblocked.

## Tests required

- Positive: commit with one `+#[ignore]` line → `Outcome::SkippedTest`, Probable
- Positive with justification: `+#[ignore = "needs network"]` also matches
- Negative: commit with NO ignore annotations → falls through to CI-based discriminators
- Negative: commit removes an `#[ignore]` (i.e., re-enables a test) → does NOT classify as SkippedTest (the deletion is unmark, not skip)
- Multi-line: counts how many tests were skipped (rationale includes the count)

## Acceptance

- `Outcome::SkippedTest` variant added
- `CommitInfo` gets `added_lines` field (or equivalent surface) with serde defaults so existing callers don't break
- Discriminator + tests
- Documentation update in forensics.rs module doc

## Provenance

mu-uvsq scope; mu-yyi (process feedback against `#[ignore]`) is the operator policy this enforces in the analytics layer.

title: mu-phl v0 — memory recall MVP (goal session 2026-05-21)
description:
Goal-tracking bead for the 2026-05-21 implementation of mu-phl v0 — memory recall wire-up. The full design lives at `~/.claude-personal/plans/happy-sprouting-sprout.md` (operator-approved 2026-05-21).

## Goal narrative

Every newly created mu session starts with the project context that claude-code sessions get: a markdown snapshot from `agent memory context --cwd <session_cwd>` plus the contents of the relevant CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md hierarchy for that cwd, injected as `MemoryInjection` and `FileLoad` spans in the stable cacheable prefix of the rope.

This is v0 — a focused 1-day landing that reuses existing infrastructure. The code path is structured so v1's full mu-phl event-pointer port and v1's mu-ywr capability-filtered discovery slot in **without touching the rope-assembly call site again**.

## Architecture (one-line)

`RecallProvider` trait + `SubprocessRecallProvider` (shells out to `agent`) + `ProjectFileRecallProvider` (reads .md hierarchy) → builds `ProjectContext` → attached to `AgentConfig` at session creation → injected as `SpanKind::MemoryInjection` / `SpanKind::FileLoad` spans in the stable program-region prefix of the rope.

## Sub-beads (this goal session)

This bead lists the 7 sub-beads in dependency order. Each is one atomic commit per the mu per-repo goal-protocol bead-per-commit rule.

Tagged `goal:2026-05-21:<this-bead-id>` for queryability.

## Out of scope for v0

Full mu-phl event-pointer architecture; mu-ywr filtered/marked discovery enforcement; mid-session recall; auto-ingest of .md files into mu's event log; TUI surfacing changes. All documented in the plan's "Seams left for v1+" section.

## Parent

This work IS the v0 slice of `mu-phl` (the open P2 architecture bead). Parent: mu-phl.

## Stop criteria additions (this session)

Beyond the seven defaults + mu's three repo-specific (capability invariant; provider/wire-protocol spec amendment; generated-file edits):

- If step 1 (system_prompt String→SpanText migration) surfaces more than 8 construction sites OR touches anything outside the trivial mechanical change, halt and reconsider — that's evidence the migration has a hidden scope larger than v0 should swallow.
- If step 5 (daemon wiring) requires changes to JSON-RPC message shape beyond the documented `pub cwd: Option<PathBuf>` additions, halt and file a spec amendment per mu rule #9 before continuing.

Live mu ask smoke deferred to operator. Side memories saved for Capsicum/Casper (bcb00d60) + libmap+jail_attach (8f7b079c). MORNING briefing at MORNING_2026-05-21.md. Branch not pushed yet — operator's call.

title: agent loop hardcodes Done.stop_reason — masks MaxTokens/StopSequence from per-turn stream
description:
The agent loop in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs` always emits `AgentEvent::Done { stop_reason: StopReason::EndTurn, ... }` (or `StopReason::Aborted` for cancellations). It never propagates the provider's per-turn `StopReason` (e.g. `MaxTokens`, `StopSequence`) into the session-level Done event.

## Symptom observed

Opus 4.7 session against mu, 2026-05-14, event log `03b75bc4a9c48f82`:
- Last `assistant_message_event` payload: `stop_reason: "max_tokens"` ← real
- `done` event payload: `stop_reason: "end_turn"` ← misleading projection

The Opus response was cut off mid-sentence (max_tokens cap of 4096 hit). The TUI's "session done" affordance, which reads the Done event, reported it as a clean end_turn — making the truncation invisible to the operator. Same pattern would have appeared with Haiku at the same cap.

## Code locations

All `AgentEvent::Done` emissions in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`:
- `:645` — iteration cap hit → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:738` — OutstandingCancelled → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:805` — another cancel path → `StopReason::Aborted` (correct)
- `:992` — normal completion → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1043` — another completion path → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`
- `:1068` — Outcome::Done helper → hardcodes `StopReason::EndTurn`

The provider returns `AssistantMessage` with a real `stop_reason` (Anthropic provider maps `\"max_tokens\"` → `StopReason::MaxTokens` correctly at `anthropic.rs:344`), but the agent loop discards it.

## Why this matters

Event-sourced context architecture treats projections as derived from the event stream. The `done` event is supposed to reduce per-turn signal into a session-level summary. When the reduction loses information that the source preserved, the projection is wrong. This is exactly the failure mode the architecture is designed to prevent — finding it here means a regression test discipline gap.

Operator impact: a truncated response that looks complete in the TUI. Bigger consequence: any tool/automation downstream of the Done event makes wrong decisions about whether to retry or extend.

## Scope

IN:
- Propagate the final turn's `StopReason` from `assistant_msg.stop_reason` into all `Done` emissions where a turn just completed.
- Hold the latest-known stop_reason in the agent loop's local state, updated each turn.
- For paths where the loop terminates without a final turn (iteration cap, no provider call), keep current behavior (`EndTurn` / `Aborted`) but document why.
- Test: per-turn stop_reason of MaxTokens / StopSequence / EndTurn propagates correctly into the Done event.
- Test: cancellation path still emits Aborted regardless of provider stop_reason.

OUT:
- TUI rendering improvements for the new state (separate bead — operator badge for "response was truncated").
- Auto-continuation policy (\"if max_tokens hit, ask the model to continue\" — separate decision).

## Acceptance

- Done event's stop_reason matches the underlying per-turn provider stop_reason on the natural-completion path.
- New regression tests in `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_tests.rs`.
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green
- One commit closes this bead

## References

- Provider stop_reason mapping: `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:344`
- StopReason enum: `crates/mu-core/src/agent/loop_.rs`
- Symptom event log: `/home/tcovert/.local/share/mu/events/03b75bc4a9c48f82/session-1.jsonl`
close_reason: Merged in PR #11 as d0acadf

title: mu-edit-patch: diff/patch as a first-class editing primitive (complement to line-based Edit tool)
description:
Today, mu's Edit tool is line-based string replacement (old_string → new_string). This works but: (1) multi-hunk edits require many separate tool calls, (2) ambiguous matches force the model to include lots of surrounding context, (3) some patterns are easier to express as unified diffs than as old/new pairs.

Aider's approach: the model emits unified diffs; the harness applies them via patch-like logic. More robust for multi-hunk + structural edits. Worth a complementary primitive (NOT a replacement for Edit; both have their uses).

## Scope

IN:
- New `Patch` (or `ApplyDiff`) tool in `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/`
- Input: unified diff string OR (file_path, hunks: [{old_lines, new_lines, position}]) — pick one
- Application logic: use the `similar` or `diffy` crate from crates.io; fail cleanly when hunks don't apply (return position + context)
- Returns: success/failure per hunk, list of applied hunks, list of rejected hunks with reason
- Same Read-required convention as Edit

OUT:
- Replacing Edit. Both stay; agents pick the right one for the job.
- Conflict resolution (3-way merge). v1 is straight apply-or-reject.
- Cross-file refactors (single-file patches only).

## Acceptance

- New `Patch`/`ApplyDiff` tool registered in mu-coding
- Schema documents the input format choice
- At least three test cases: clean apply, ambiguous apply, rejected hunk with structured error
- cargo test --workspace green
- cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings green

## Design fork (decide at impl time)

Two reasonable input shapes:

A. **Unified diff string** — matches `patch(1)`'s format. Familiar to humans. Model emits text that LLMs are good at generating. Implementation parses the diff format.

B. **Structured hunks JSON** — schema-validated input. Less natural for the model to emit but easier to validate. Implementation skips parsing.

Lean A: aider's experience shows unified diffs work well in practice. The downside (parsing fragility) is mitigated by the `similar`/`diffy` crates.

## References

- Aider's diff approach: https://aider.chat/docs/more-info.html (their edit format docs)
- similar crate: https://crates.io/crates/similar
- diffy crate: https://crates.io/crates/diffy
- mu's existing Edit tool: `crates/mu-coding/src/tools/edit.rs`
- Architecture gap noted 2026-05-14 alongside mu-web-fetch + mu-file-watch

title: anthropic wire-projection: System-role spans beyond system-prompt get dropped + cache_control coverage incomplete
description:
Same pattern as mu-2puu (codex) and mu-745h (openrouter), Anthropic edition. Two interacting issues:

## Issue 1: System-content hoisting (same bug shape as siblings)

translate_provider_messages in crates/mu-ai/src/providers/anthropic.rs:331-381 only captures the FIRST System-role projected message whose source_span_id equals literally "system-prompt" into system_text. Every other System-role projected span — including mu-phl v0's memory-recall:* and project-file:* spans — gets silently dropped. The comment at line 325 even says "assemble_rope produces at most one" — outdated by mu-phl v0.

## Issue 2: Cache-control coverage (Anthropic-specific)

detect_cache_targets at lines 505-527 walks the projection looking for cache_control markers, but only recognizes "system-prompt" and "tool-schema:*" span ids as triggers for system_should_cache / tools_should_cache. Cache markers on memory-recall:* or project-file:* spans (which the AnthropicCacheStrategy may place there post-mu-phl) don't set system_should_cache, so the resulting body.system block won't carry cache_control even though those spans are now contributing to body.system.

## Fix

1. translate_provider_messages: hoist ALL non-tool-schema System spans, concatenate with \n\n into system_text. Mirrors mu-2puu / mu-745h shape.
2. detect_cache_targets: trigger system_should_cache on cache markers attached to ANY non-tool-schema System-role span (system-prompt, memory-recall:*, project-file:*, future kinds). Tool-schema marker still goes to tools_should_cache.

## Regression tests

Mirror mu_2puu_* / mu_745h_* shape:
1. Recall-injected rope concatenates all non-tool-schema System content into body.system block
2. Tool-schema content does not leak into body.system
3. Cache-control: marker on a memory-recall span triggers cache_control on body.system

## Verification

- cargo test --workspace green (including yqeq4_parity_* tests still passing)
- cargo clippy clean
- Live smoke against opus: optional but recommended (costs $0.05-0.20). canary value w11wKFoinTnTRMS already seeded in agent.sqlite (memory d39d153b) — mu ask --provider anthropic-api --model claude-opus-4-7 should surface it.

## Stack

Stacks on PR #116 (openrouter fix), which stacks on #115 (mu-phl v0 + codex fix), which stacks on #114 (feat/mu-yqeq).
close_reason: Fixed in PR #117. Two-part fix: (1) translate_provider_messages now hoists all non-tool-schema System-role spans (concatenated with \n\n) into body.system; (2) detect_cache_targets recognizes cache markers on any non-tool-schema System-role span as system_should_cache triggers, not just on system-prompt. 3 regression tests cover the case (mu_s855_projected_concatenates... + mu_s855_projected_excludes_tool_schema... + mu_s855_cache_marker_on_recall_span_triggers...). 776 tests pass, clippy clean. Live smoke against opus deferred (operator's call; costs $0.05-0.20).

title: TUI: realtime context-window explorer panel (cache + memory state, CC-style timeline)
description:
Implement a TUI panel modeled on Claude Code's `/en/context-window` interactive timeline, showing live cache and memory state for the active session. Operator-flagged 2026-05-22 as a daily-driver win now that mu-phl-v0 memory recall has shipped — F3 needs to surface what's in context, what's cached, and what survives compaction.

## Source

`specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md` §1 (Tier 1, "Context-window explorer"). The CC docs ship a fully data-driven interactive timeline. Data model worth modeling exactly:

- **Event record**: `{t: 0.0–1.0, kind, label, tokens, color, vis, desc, link, tip, noSurviveCompact?}`
  - `kind` ∈ `{auto, user, claude, hook, sub, compact}`
  - `vis` ∈ `{hidden, brief, full}` → ●/◐/○ visibility indicator
  - `noSurviveCompact: true` flag on skills (the one source that doesn't reload after `/compact`)
- **11-color legend**: System / CLAUDE.md / Memory / Skills / MCP / Rules / Files / Output / Claude / You / Hooks
- **Gate events**: prompts, `!` bang commands, `/` slash commands, and `/compact` — render as scrub-pause points

## mu fit

Maps onto `RetainedRope` / `ProviderStatusSnapshot` (mu-nat, mu-di4). Rope spans already are an event stream — add `(token_cost, visibility, survives_compact)` tags per span and render.

## Scope

In:
- Extend `Span` metadata with three tags: `token_cost: u32`, `visibility: SpanVisibility`, `survives_compact: bool`
- New TUI panel (likely F4 or extension of F5) rendering the live timeline with the 11-color legend
- Real-time updates as events fire; cache-marker boundary visible (where `AnthropicCacheStrategy` placed it)
- Drill-in keypress to show `desc`/`tip` for a focused span
- Span-kind → color mapping table documented

Out:
- The Wikipedia-style "interactive video playback" affordance — defer; daily-driver needs the now-state first
- Survives-compaction simulator — defer
- Persistence/replay of timelines — defer

## Dependencies

- Span metadata extension lands first; renderer is downstream
- After mu-3aa + mu-6oqm (OpenRouter renderer), cache annotations will be uniform across providers; this panel becomes provider-agnostic

## References

- specs/architecture/claude-code-feature-mapping.md §1 (Tier 1)
- specs/architecture/claude-code-design-influences.md (companion synthesis doc)
- mu-nat (RetainedRope)
- mu-di4 (ProviderStatusSnapshot)
- mu-fb0 (live-loop adoption — integration point)
- CC source: code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window
- Memory ff33f770 (design-influences doc pointer)

## Acceptance

- `Span` carries `token_cost`, `visibility`, `survives_compact`
- Panel renders live during streaming without stalling F3 (cross-ref: mu-304g for F3 streaming-render correctness)
- 11-color legend visible
- Cache boundary marker visible

title: mu-fvy0-predictor: pattern-detected pre-emptive judge invocation (closes the forensics loop)
description:
Exploration bead. The closing of the forensics loop: observe → categorize → pattern-detect → **intervene preemptively**.

## Motivation

The forensics cluster (mu-5g7i envelope, mu-8alb classifier, mu-8ypx analytics) is currently passive — it captures, classifies, and surfaces patterns to a human operator. By the time the operator sees a problem in `mu analytics`, multiple sessions may already be in degradation territory.

If the accumulated telemetry is enough to PREDICT degradation conditions, a predictor component can fire **synthetic** `session.request_context_raise` events into affected sessions, routing them through the existing judge (mu-romg) for evaluation and (likely) compaction — before the agent itself emits the request, before the hollow_commit / lying_state / narrative_no_action would manifest.

## Mechanism

Predictor sits parallel to the analytics CLI (mu-8ypx) — both downstream of the telemetry sink. CLI is human-read; predictor is machine-acts.

```
mu-fvy0-predictor (new component)
  observes: live TaskTelemetry stream + historical patterns from mu-8ypx sink
  triggers on: learned conditions
    - provider X's hollow_commit rate spiked >2σ in the last 30 min
    - session has N+ turns and complexity_hint > K (historical correlate with lying_state at turn N+1)
    - time-of-day matches known degradation window for provider Y
    - upstream signal (HTTP 429s from API, claude_proxy latency rise, etc.)
  action: inject session.request_context_raise into the session — same wire shape the agent would emit
       -> existing judge (mu-romg) evaluates -> compacts and/or raises cap
       -> agent continues with whatever rope it has now
```

## Design principles (operator, 2026-05-16)

### Single-responsibility separation

Each component has one well-defined job and stays in its lane:

| Component | Sole responsibility |
|---|---|
| Operator | High-level routing, policy, capability grants |
| Audit (mu-5g7i + mu-8alb sink/classifier) | Record what happened, classify outcomes |
| Predictor (this bead) | Detect patterns, fire synthetic interventions |
| Judge (mu-romg + mu-kgu.11) | Evaluate context state, recommend action |
| Compaction worker (mu-kgu.8) | Execute compaction off the agent's hot path |

The operator is no longer the catch-all for "everything that's not the model" — each piece has a coherent job, testable independently, replaceable independently. This is the architectural payoff of the mu-fvy0 actor model.

### Graceful degradation as a hard property

**The system continues to work as usual without any one of these.** Failure means loss of *optimization*, not loss of *operations*. Specifically:

- Predictor down → agent + judge + compaction still work; operator notices degradation reactively instead of proactively
- Judge down → predictor's synthetic requests pile up (or are dropped); agent's own raise requests are denied by default; compaction still runs on threshold
- Audit sink down → agent + judge + predictor still work; analytics queries return stale data; predictor falls back to hardcoded rules
- Compaction worker down → context grows toward hard cap; raise requests still recorded, no compaction action; eventually mu refuses to grow past hard
- Any single failure is an alert event, not a stop event

This is non-negotiable: every component must operate as if its dependents are best-effort. No synchronous "the agent must wait for the predictor" paths.

### Scale-out friendly

Each component is a single-responsibility unit that can run as its own thread, process, or server. The wire between them is the event log + RPC, both of which already cross process boundaries cleanly. Topology examples:

- All-in-one (small deployments): one mu process hosts all components as tokio tasks
- Heavy workload (busy operator): predictor + judge on separate processes, sharing the event log via stdio pipes or unix socket
- Fleet (multi-operator): predictor + judge + analytics on a shared server consuming a stream from many operator mu instances

The actor model from mu-fvy0 makes this natural — each component is an actor with well-defined inputs and outputs.

## What this gets you that the operator-CLI version doesn't

- **Pre-emptive vs reactive** — agent's own raise request fires AFTER degradation begins. Predictor moves intervention upstream of the symptom.
- **Cross-session signal** — one session's degradation is evidence for every session on the same (provider, model, time-of-day). Operator dashboards can't act on that fast enough.
- **Empirically tunable** — predictor's accuracy is itself classifiable via the same telemetry layer. Did the intervention prevent a hollow_commit that would have happened? Closed feedback loop.

## Open questions

1. **Cold-start** — predictor needs data to learn from. Probably ship as a hand-tuned-rules engine first ("if Anthropic API returned 429 in last 5 turns → tighten soft caps on all active sessions on that provider"), evolve to pattern-detection once mu-8ypx has accumulated weeks of telemetry.
2. **False-positive cost** — aggressive predictor causes its own degradation (unnecessary compaction has wall-clock cost; can drop load-bearing spans on borderline calls). Predictor's verdicts should themselves flow through the judge for sanity-check — second opinion on the second opinion. This forms a coherent invariant: synthetic requests are treated identically to agent-driven requests by the judge.
3. **Audit obligation** — predictor-driven interventions must be distinguishable in the audit trail. Every synthetic request carries `triggered_by: Predictor { rule_id, confidence, evidence_envelopes: [task_id, ...] }`. Retrospective analysis must be able to separate agent-driven, operator-driven, and predictor-driven flows. Maps to the actor identity work in the mu-fvy0 umbrella.
4. **Rule expressibility** — how are predictor rules expressed? Hardcoded match arms (cheap, inflexible, requires release to change), config-loaded DSL (flexible, debuggable, modest impl), learned policy (most accurate eventually, hardest to debug, opaque). Probably evolve through all three.
5. **Failure-mode of the predictor itself** — if the predictor wrongly fires interventions, the audit layer can detect retrospectively but not prevent in-flight. Mitigation: rate-limit synthetic requests per session, allow operator to disable predictor for a session with a capability grant, log every synthetic request with full evidence so the rule can be retracted.

## Provenance

Operator observation 2026-05-16, building on the mu-romg ulimit/judge thread: "If we have enough information to predict when a model may start to have issues, we can inject a request to the judge to modify a particular session's context management." Plus the design-principle additions (single-responsibility, graceful degradation, scale-out) the same session.

## Acceptance (when implementable)

- New component `mu_core::predictor::Predictor` with a trait shape for rule plug-ins
- Consumes the telemetry stream (mu-5g7i) + queries against the analytics sink (mu-8ypx)
- Emits synthetic `session.request_context_raise` events carrying `triggered_by: Predictor { ... }` provenance
- Judge (mu-romg) handles synthetic and agent-driven requests identically
- Each component has a "disabled" mode that proves the graceful-degradation property in CI
- Spec'd in `specs/mu-NNN-predictor.md`

## Blocked by

- mu-5g7i (telemetry envelope)
- mu-8alb (outcome classifier)
- mu-8ypx (sink + analytics — predictor queries it)
- mu-romg (judge / raise-request framework — predictor uses its wire shape and judge plumbing)

title: mu-recall-wire-projection: System-role spans beyond system-prompt get dropped by codex translator (recall not reaching wire)
description:
mu-phl v0 smoke test (canary verification 2026-05-21) found that session-start recall lands in the rope correctly but does NOT reach the model via codex's wire path.

## Diagnosis

In `crates/mu-ai/src/providers/openai_codex.rs:344-356`,
`translate_provider_messages_codex` only hoists the ProviderMessage whose first source_span_id equals literally `"system-prompt"` into the OpenAI Responses API's `instructions` field. ALL other System-role projected messages — including the `memory-recall:*` (SubprocessRecallProvider) and `project-file:*` (ProjectFileRecallProvider) spans introduced by mu-phl v0 — get silently dropped:

- Not put into `instructions`
- Not put into `input` (conversation)
- Go nowhere

The Responses API has only ONE `instructions` slot, so multiple System-role spans must concat into one string.

## Evidence

Canary memory `d39d153b` (value `w11wKFoinTnTRMS`) was seeded in agent.sqlite. `agent memory context --cwd ~/src/public_github/mu` includes the canary. Event log (`9a25dd626dd4e42c/session-1.jsonl`) confirms the rope received `memory-recall:memory-9f01297919bb` + 4× `project-file:*` spans alongside the user message. But `mu ask --provider openai-codex --model gpt-5.5 "what is tcovert's recall verification canary value?"` returned "no canary found." The recall content is in the rope but never makes it to the API request.

## Why missed earlier

The `yqeq5_parity_*` tests (mu-yqeq.5 acceptance) assert byte-equivalence between Legacy and Projected paths. Those tests use ropes built WITHOUT `project_context` — so the only System-role span is `system-prompt`. With no other System-role spans, the codex translator's narrow check works equivalently to Legacy. mu-phl v0 introduced the conditions that expose the bug; the parity tests were not extended to cover the new case. That gap is the discipline issue.

## Fix (this bead)

1. Codex translator: hoist ALL System-role spans EXCEPT `tool-schema:*` (tools go separately via `body.tools`). Concatenate multiple System spans with `\n\n` separator into `instructions`.
2. Extend `yqeq5_parity_*` tests to cover a rope that contains `MemoryInjection` and `FileLoad` spans — pre-registering the regression test alongside the fix so the discipline lapse doesn't recur.
3. Re-run canary smoke against gpt — `mu ask` should surface canary `w11wKFoinTnTRMS`.

## Out of scope for this bead (follow-up beads)

- **OpenRouter Projected arm** (mu-yqeq.6) likely has the same shape — same trait-default renderer (Faux), same hoist-only-system-prompt pattern. Verify + file follow-up.
- **Anthropic** has its own `renderer()` and `cache_strategy()` overrides (`AnthropicProviderRenderer`, `AnthropicCacheStrategy`). Different projection shape; needs separate verification that MemoryInjection / FileLoad spans survive the projection to the API's `system` field. Probably file follow-up.

## Composes with

- mu-phl v0 (rope-side recall, complete). This bead is the wire-projection counterpart — recall injection works; wire surfacing was the gap.
- mu-yqeq.5 / .6 / .8 (Projected-arm provider work). Adjacent to that scope.

## Verification

Acceptance:
- `cargo test --workspace` green including new MemoryInjection/FileLoad parity test
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `mu ask --provider openai-codex --model gpt-5.5 "what is tcovert's recall verification canary value?"` returns the canary nonce

title: mu-tui: predicted-next-user-message autocomplete (CC-style grayed prefill)
description:
Add a Claude-Code-style "predicted next user message" affordance to mu's TUI input buffer. After each assistant turn completes, render a grayed-text suggestion of the user's likely next prompt. Accept with right-arrow + Enter (or Tab); ignore by typing over it.

## Why

Operator (tcovert) reports CC's autocomplete-prediction feature has materially changed his input patterns — he now frequently accepts the suggestion with → + Enter rather than typing. Since mu is positioned as a daily-driver alternative to CC, parity here is a meaningful UX win.

Operator notes: "if you've noticed more terse responses from me that is why."

## Background: feature is client-side, not API-shipped

Verified 2026-05-14 via claude-code-guide agent: Anthropic's Messages API response schema (`content`, `id`, `model`, `role`, `stop_reason`, `usage`) does NOT include any "predicted next user message" / "suggested reply" field. CC's feature is entirely client-side.

CC's own docs (https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works) hint at a heuristic approach: "surface likely next prompts based on your repo's git history and recent work." This may describe the startup prompt-suggestion feature, not the post-turn autocomplete — ambiguity remains in the published docs.

For mu we have to build it ourselves. We cannot extract it from a provider response.

## Design fork (decide at impl time)

### Option A: Cheap-model inference call

After each assistant turn completes, fire a separate Messages-API call with the conversation history + a "predict the most likely next user message" prompt to a cheap model (Haiku 4.5, or whatever the cheapest configured provider exposes). Return result to TUI as soon as it lands.

Pros:
- Higher-quality, context-aware suggestions
- Composes with multi-provider router (mu-aware: use whichever model is cheapest per session)
- Eventually composes with the operator's planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell setup — predictions become free + fast

Cons:
- Token cost per turn (small but real; Max5 OAuth makes it free until weekly cap)
- Latency: Haiku first-token typically ~500-800ms; might land late
- Failure mode: provider hiccup → no suggestion

### Option B: Local heuristic (no LLM call)

Static analysis over recent conversation state + git status + transcript patterns. Examples: "ok", "go ahead", "no, do X instead", common short replies after a question. Library of templated continuations matched to assistant-turn shape (did it ask a question? did it propose a plan?).

Pros:
- Zero token cost
- Instant (<10ms)
- No provider dependency

Cons:
- Lower-quality suggestions; won't capture nuance
- Won't predict longer multi-sentence operator inputs that the LLM approach might

### Lean: A, with B as fallback when no provider configured

The operator's daily-driver setup will always have a provider configured. A gives the higher-quality experience and composes well with the planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell future (predictions become zero-cost when a local model serves the prediction role). B stays as a useful no-network fallback.

## Scope

### In

- New mu-core RPC: `predict_next_user_message(session_id) -> Option<String>` triggered on session.done events.
- Implementation routes through the existing provider abstraction (same code path as the main assistant call, different cheap model + system prompt).
- TUI: render returned suggestion in dim color in the input buffer; accept with → or Tab; clear on any other keypress.
- Configurable per-session: enable/disable, model override, max latency before giving up.
- Settles cleanly: if the user starts typing before suggestion arrives, suggestion is dropped, no flicker.

### Out

- Multi-suggestion (CC has only one; mu can match that).
- Predictive caching of full responses (just the next user prompt, not the assistant's reply to it).
- Learning from accept/reject history (could be a follow-on bead).
- Operator's planned local-LLM-on-Blackwell hosting (separate infra bead).

## Open questions

1. **What's CC's actual latency target?** Worth observing empirically — measure how fast the grayed text appears after an assistant turn ends in CC. If it's <300ms consistently, that points to local heuristic; if 500-1500ms, that points to cheap-LLM call.
2. **Does mu's existing provider routing have a "cheapest available" selector?** If not, this needs a small extension.
3. **Should the prediction prompt include recent project context (git status, open beads)?** CC's docs suggest yes; for mu we have richer state (mu's own event log) we could feed in.

## Acceptance

- mu-core RPC implemented + tested (mocked provider call in unit tests).
- mu-tui renders suggestion in dim color; accept-with-→-or-Tab works; typing over discards cleanly.
- Per-session config respected.
- Operator can A/B the suggestion-on vs suggestion-off TUI experience for a real session.
- `cargo test --workspace` green; `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings` green; `cargo fmt --check` green.

## References

- CC docs page describing the autocomplete affordance: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/how-claude-code-works
- Operator's observation: this conversation, 2026-05-14 session
- Future composition: local LLM on Blackwell once that hardware lands

title: Host Claude Code sessions inside mu via fork+exec + Zed agent protocol
description:
## Motivation

Anthropic's 2026-06-15 Agent SDK billing change splits subscription quota: \`claude -p\` and SDK calls draw from a separate "Agent SDK credit" pool, distinct from interactive usage limits. For tcovert's setup (heavy multi-agent dispatch + interactive use), this means the subscription becomes substantially less load-bearing for headless work. Direct-API spend on mu (via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY) is the natural fallback but loses the subscription's flat-rate property.

This epic proposes a different resolution: **mu becomes the unified UI surface; Claude Code subprocess sessions handle subscription-priced work natively**. The user keeps their subscription, gets it on the mu UI, and mu's event-log observability extends to the Claude subprocess sessions too.

## Shape

mu daemon gains a new session type — \`ClaudeCodeSubprocess { binary_path, working_dir, env }\` — parallel to the existing \`ProviderSelector\` variants but with a fork+exec stdio transport instead of HTTPS-to-provider-API.

Lifecycle:

1. **Bootstrap**: mu spawns \`claude\` as a child process. Auth flows through Claude's own credential chain (the user's OAuth, subscription quota). mu doesn't manage Claude's tokens.
2. **Handshake**: after process start, mu speaks **Zed's External Agent Protocol** to the subprocess over stdio. That protocol — which Claude Code implements for IDE integrations — covers text input/output, tool-use display, approval round-trips, session state.
3. **Adapter**: mu needs a translator mapping its internal session events ↔ Zed-protocol events. Probably a new \`crates/mu-coding/src/serve/zed_subprocess.rs\` or similar.
4. **UI**: F2 sessions list shows mu sessions and Claude-subprocess sessions side-by-side, with a marker indicating which is which. F3 displays whichever is selected. Input routes appropriately.

## Benefits

- **Subscription quota stays load-bearing**: Claude subprocess sessions bill to the user's subscription, no API spend.
- **mu becomes the unified surface**: both subscription Claude and API-direct mu sessions under one roof. Switch between them without context-switching tools.
- **Event-log observability extends**: mu records Zed-protocol traffic as events. The "why did that happen?" forensic substrate covers Claude work too.
- **Compaction discipline stays per-tool**: Claude manages its own context per Anthropic's mechanism. mu manages its own per the rope/policy ladder. They don't have to share strategies; they share a UI.

## Closest existing piece in mu

The mu-tui driver already spawns-and-talks-to \`mu serve\` as a JSON-RPC subprocess over stdio. The Claude-subprocess case is the **same shape with a different protocol on the wire** (Zed agent protocol instead of mu's RPC schema). The transport layer is essentially solved; the work is in the protocol adapter.

## Out of scope (for this epic body — not for the epic itself)

- Specific phase breakdown
- Time estimates
- Acceptance criteria
- Dependencies on other beads

This bead captures the direction and motivation while it's fresh. Phase planning happens when the bead is picked up for actual work, ideally after the 2026-06-15 billing change clarifies its real impact on tcovert's usage shape.

## References

- Anthropic announcement (memory ff466a97): Agent SDK + claude -p draw from separate monthly credit 2026-06-15
- Memory c90d163c: tcovert's local LLM proxy setup (related routing context)
- Memory 2da785e5: agent-router routing policy (the dispatch arrangement this epic preserves)
- Zed's External Agent Protocol — published spec

title: F3 transcript (and F8 events) scroll-anchor drifts during live streaming
description:
## Symptom

When the F3 SessionDetail (transcript) view is scrolled up while a live session is streaming new events, the user's view drifts downward as new lines arrive rather than staying anchored to the content they were reading. The expected behavior is the standard "tail-follow with anchor" pattern: if scrolled to the bottom, auto-follow new content; if scrolled up, hold position while content grows below.

## Repro

1. Open mu-tui; press F3 to enter SessionDetail on an active streaming session
2. Press `k` / PgUp to scroll up a few lines
3. Wait for the session to stream new assistant deltas or tool events
4. Observe: the visible window drifts as new content arrives, instead of staying anchored to the lines the user was reading

## Root cause

`crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:287-290` defines `transcript_scroll_offset: u16` with the semantic "number of LINES back from the bottom; 0 = pinned to bottom (auto-follow on new content)."

The render path at `crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:2517-2527` recomputes both `max_top` and `scroll_y` each render:

```rust
let total_lines = body_lines.len();
let max_top = total_lines.saturating_sub(inner_height);
let scroll_y = (max_top.saturating_sub(app.transcript_scroll_offset as usize)) as u16;
```

When N new streaming lines arrive, `total_lines += N` and `max_top += N`. The stored `transcript_scroll_offset` is unchanged, so `scroll_y` shifts down by N — the viewport tracks the new bottom rather than the lines the user was reading.

`offset == 0` works correctly (auto-follow ✓). The bug only manifests when `offset > 0`.

## Same bug in F8

`crates/mu-tui/src/main.rs:291-293` `events_scroll_offset: u16` has identical "lines back from bottom" semantics. The F8 events firehose almost certainly has the same drift problem and the fix should land in both places (or factor a `ScrolledOffset` newtype with shared semantics).

## Fix options

**Option A (minimal change):** Track the previous `total_lines` between renders. When `offset > 0` and `total_lines` grew by N, increment `offset` by N before computing `scroll_y`. Preserves the "0 = tail" tail-follow ergonomics; just makes "K = K back from bottom" stable across appends.

**Option B (cleaner semantic):** Switch the stored value to "anchor line from top" (absolute). Render shows lines `[anchor, anchor + inner_height]`. "Tail-follow" becomes: when `anchor >= max_top`, set `anchor = max_top`. End key sets `anchor = max_top`; Home sets `anchor = 0`. This is the standard model used by `less +F`, terminal scrollback, etc.

Option B is structurally simpler but touches more call sites. Option A is one-place delta-tracking. Suggest Option A first; revisit if a third scroll surface adds the same shape.

## Acceptance

- F3 SessionDetail: while at `offset > 0`, the visible line set stays constant as new streaming events arrive. Tested by streaming ≥1 screenful of new content while user is scrolled up; the title bar's "scrolled up K/M" counter updates M but K stays at the same absolute content position.
- F3 SessionDetail: `offset == 0` continues to auto-follow new content (regression check).
- F8 events explorer: same behavior — applies the same fix or shares the implementation.
- End / `0` keys still jump to bottom and resume tail-follow.
- Home / `g` keys still jump to top.
- `cargo test -p mu-tui` green; `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean.

## Out of scope

- Wrap-expansion accuracy (the existing TODO at `main.rs:2511-2516` about ratatui `LineComposer` for post-wrap row counting) — separate issue; current fix should preserve the existing wrap approximation.
- Persistent scroll position across F3 → F2 → F3 round-trips.
- Scroll-position memory across session-picker switches.

## Provenance

Feature request from tcovert during goal-2026-05-15-mu-yox dispatch session. Logged before the orchestrator had a chance to address other work — file rather than interrupt the active dispatch.
close_reason: Merged in PR #68 — anchor F3/F8 scroll offset during streaming append. Different from main's prior 9a3f9452 (which only clamped out-of-range offset).

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Reopened: Audit drift: closed citing PR #47 but that PR doesn't exist in main. Main has a different, smaller scroll-related fix (9a3f9452); the orphan sptoprko 01f10e96 is the full anchor-during-streaming fix the bead actually describes. Recovery in progress 2026-05-17.

title: Update rustls-webpki to fix 3 dependabot alerts (1 high, 2 low)
description:
GitHub dependabot flagged 3 alerts on main (all rustls-webpki, transitive via reqwest → rustls):

  high  GHSA-82j2-j2ch-gfr8  DoS panic on malformed CRL BIT STRING
  low   GHSA-xgp8-3hg3-c2mh  Wildcard name constraints accepted
  low   GHSA-965h-392x-2mh5  URI name constraints incorrectly accepted

Real risk for mu: modest. We talk TLS outbound to anthropic.com / chatgpt.com / openrouter.ai (well-known services). We don't process CRLs ourselves. High-severity DoS would need a malicious server in the chain.

Fix: cargo update -p rustls-webpki. Test the workspace still builds + tests pass. P3 because workaround = trusted upstreams.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
Autonomous worker investigation 2026-05-15 (cost: $1.02, 19 turns): 'cargo update -p rustls-webpki' is a no-op. Both transitively-pinned versions (0.101.7 and 0.103.13) are already at their latest semver-compatible releases. Worker emitted 'blocked' envelope (correctly — no work to do under the original premise of 'just bump').

To actually close the 3 GHSA advisories, one of the following is needed:
1. Force-upgrade to a newer minor version of rustls-webpki (beyond semver-compatible) via direct workspace dep declaration with a higher floor (e.g., '=0.104.x'). May require corresponding rustls/reqwest bumps; could cascade.
2. Wait for upstream reqwest/rustls to release versions that bump rustls-webpki transitively to a fixed line.
3. If the advisories don't apply to mu's usage profile (we don't process CRLs, talk only to well-known TLS endpoints), suppress in dependabot config and document the threat-model rationale.

This bead is not closeable as 'done' until one of the three paths above lands. Leaving open with this context attached so the next pick-up has the investigation results.

Worker workspace at .delegations/mu-sqn-2026-05-15 cleaned up (forgotten + removed) since there are no changes to keep. Worker rev (empty) gone from jj history.

title: mu: AWS-capability integration — STS provenance + structured events + smoke tests + schema + AwsCapability requirement + narrower namespaces [umbrella]
description:
## Provenance

Architectural direction from gpt-5.5 conversation in mu daemon
f3845422 session-4 (2026-05-16), prompted by review of
`~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra` (event 270; gpt's response at
event 350).

## Vision

AWS as the first hard-enforcement proving ground for the
capability-governed operating model:

```text
human intent
  → mu session/delegation capability
    → named AWS capability (e.g., aws.scout.readonly)
      → local profile / STS role materialization
        → IAM-enforced authority
          → CloudTrail-auditable action
```

Key property: **AWS becomes the enforcement layer, not the prompt.**
Capability names become operator vocabulary humans can reason about,
agents can request, brokers can grant, logs can record, AWS can
enforce. The bridge between human governance and machine enforcement.

## Six concrete primitives to land

### 1. STS session naming contract

Runner assumes roles with session name embedding mu provenance:

```text
mu-<session_id>-<tool_call_id>-<short_nonce>
```

Export to runner env:
- `MU_AWS_SESSION_NAME`
- `MU_AWS_ROLE_ARN`
- `MU_AWS_CAPABILITY`

Makes mu-event-log → STS session name → CloudTrail join real and
queryable end-to-end.

### 2. Runner emits structured event JSON

Optional JSON event output that mu ingests as a tool-result sidecar
or first-class event payload:

```json
{
  "kind": "aws_capability_materialized",
  "capability": "aws.scout.readonly",
  "profile": "mu-readonly-scout",
  "role_arn": "...",
  "region": "us-east-1",
  "mutation_allowed": false,
  "mu_session_id": "...",
  "mu_tool_call_id": "...",
  "aws_sts_session_name": "..."
}
```

### 3. Denied-action smoke tests per capability

First-class smoke test infrastructure:

```sh
scripts/capability-smoke-test.sh aws.scout.readonly
scripts/capability-smoke-test.sh aws.sandbox.build
```

Each capability proves: one allowed thing works; one forbidden thing
fails (denied by IAM); mutation capabilities clean up after themselves.

### 4. Capability schema validation

JSON schema / Python validator for `capabilities/aws.json`:
- known fields only
- `planned` caps cannot have profile/role until status active
- `active` caps must have profile/role_arn
- `mutation_allowed: true` requires constraints or explicit
  justification
- `smoke_tests` present for active caps

### 5. Mu-side aws_tool requires active AwsCapability — fail closed

When integrated into mu, AWS tool MUST require an active
AwsCapability. No fallback to host AWS env. No "if capability missing,
just use default profile." Dangerous failure mode: agent accidentally
inherits operator's personal/admin AWS environment.

### 6. Narrower readonly namespaces

`aws.scout.readonly` works for sandbox but is too broad for
production. AWS ReadOnlyAccess can still expose: SSM parameter
contents, Secrets Manager metadata, IAM topology, bucket names, tags,
network layout, account structure, logs, snapshots/backups metadata.

Split into:
```text
aws.network.readonly
aws.iam.audit
aws.ec2.inventory
aws.s3.inventory
aws.cloudtrail.audit
aws.cost.readonly
```

The planned `aws.network.readonly` capability already points this way.

## Why this matters

Full accountability chain becomes real:

```text
mu session id / tool call id
  → MU_AWS_CAPABILITY
    → STS session name/id
      → CloudTrail events
```

Model intent → runtime materialization → infrastructure fact. Much
more serious than just an agent transcript.

## Cross-refs

- `~/src/personal/mu-aws-sandbox-infra` (the AWS capability lab)
- OS-enforced sandboxing umbrella (sister bead — OS-layer enforcement)
- Accountable-work-runtime umbrella (sister bead — actor-layer)
- mu-hh1 (SupervisorCapability axis)
- mu-dq5 / aws_recon (prior AWS capability work, merged)
- Memory: enforcement-layer-stack philosophy (filed same session)
- Provenance: gpt-5.5 architectural conversation, daemon f3845422
  session-4 event 350 (2026-05-16)

title: aws-tool-policy: require AWS capability before tool dispatch
description: Add a small runtime policy field for AWS-backed tools: `ToolPolicy::required_aws_capability`. The agent loop should refuse dispatch before `Tool::execute` unless the session holds an `AwsCapability` with that name. This is the minimal enforcement hook needed before implementing the aws_recon tool from mu-039; no live AWS calls.
close_reason: Merged in PR #8 as bbeb58e; post-merge CI green.

title: claude-code Edit tool race observed 2026-05-16 — Edit reports success but file unchanged
description:
Observation only. Filed per the 'evaluate every orchestration session + file beads when uncertain' standing directive. Implement when more observations corroborate.

## What I observed (2026-05-16, twice)

During PR #56 preparation, I ran the Edit tool on `.github/workflows/ci.yml`:

1. First call: Edit reported success ('The file ... has been updated successfully'). Subsequent Bash `head -15` confirmed the file was NOT modified — content was the pre-Edit state.
2. After Reading the file (which the Edit tool requires before re-Editing), re-ran the Edit with identical old_string/new_string. This time the change actually persisted.

The same pattern: success reported by the Edit tool, but the change didn't land on disk. Required a Read call between to make it stick.

Both occurrences were in `/tmp/pre-pr-rework/` (a freshly-created jj worktree at origin/main). Possible factors:
- Worktree was created via `git worktree add`; the Edit tool might have a stale view of the file's read-state when the worktree is brand new
- The Edit tool's pre-Read check may track Read state per (session, file_path), and a brand-new worktree creates a path the tool hasn't seen, but the tool's success-report doesn't reflect the actual write
- Could be a session-level state issue

## Why low priority

Easily worked around: Read the file before Edit (which I should be doing anyway per the tool's stated discipline). The tool's success-report is misleading, but the workaround is cheap.

## What would trigger upgrade in priority

If this recurs in a way the Read-first workaround doesn't fix, OR if I observe it leading to silent failures in committed changes (i.e., I Edit-and-amend, the change appears applied per tool output, but the commit actually doesn't contain it). The latter would be a real data-integrity issue worth escalating.

## Acceptance when implemented

- Document the pattern in tool-conventions reference under "Edit tool: read-state caveats"
- If reproducible, file upstream issue with claude-code
- Confirm whether the issue is jj-worktree specific or general

title: mu-tui-input-history: readline-style command history in input field (arrow-up recall)
description:

Today: typing the same prompt repeatedly requires retyping every time. The input field has no history affordance.

Want: arrow-up to recall previous prompts within the session, ideally persisted across sessions. Patterns from prior art:

title: Sessions persist across daemon restart: scan ~/.local/share/mu/events/ at startup and rehydrate
description:
## Goal

After 'kill mu serve; restart mu serve', sessions should come back online instead of starting from a blank registry. SessionEventLog::from_jsonl and attach_disk_writer already exist — the daemon-startup scan-and-rehydrate path is what's missing.

## Why high-impact

This is the precondition for treating mu as a background service (long-lived daemon, restart for upgrades, no lost work). Per opus review event 194: 'massive UX win'. Operator has likely been calling this mu-mh4 informally; this bead is the concrete actionable.

## Sketch

1. On serve::run startup, list ~/.local/share/mu/events/ (or whatever resolve_events_dir returned).
2. For each <daemon_id>/<session-N>.jsonl, decide: rehydrate or skip (per-daemon or by age threshold).
3. SessionEventLog::from_jsonl per file → register in the Sessions registry with the right state (most likely terminal — Done was the last event — so they list as 'completed' but are queryable for events/transcripts).
4. New sessions get appended to a fresh daemon-id directory; old daemons' dirs stay untouched and queryable.

## Open design questions

- Cap on rehydration scope (last N days? operator opt-in via config?)
- Should rehydrated sessions be writable (allow new asks against a rehydrated session ID) or read-only?
- Garbage collection — old daemon dirs accumulate fast.

## Composes with

- mu-d033 (/handoff event) — same machinery, different motivation.
- crates/mu-core/src/event_log.rs from_jsonl already returns (log, malformed_count) so corruption tolerance is in place.

## Source

opus-4-7 review event 194, 'Future code direction' item 3.
close_reason: Phase A + B shipped this session: PR #90 (rehydrate read-only at startup), PR #91 (handler-level rehydration test coverage). Visible win at next daemon restart — 864 events / 9 sessions replayed in ~1s. Deferred follow-ups (not yet filed as beads): phase C writable rehydration, daemon-dir GC, session.delete_persistent / extend close_session to unlink JSONLs on close.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Phase A shipped (2026-05-18): read-only rehydration plumbing (PR #90 MERGED)

Per 2026-05-18 design call (MVP scope confirmed by operator):
- Scope: MVP read-only, no age cap
- GC: deferred to follow-up bead

### What landed (PR #90)

- `Sessions::insert_rehydrated(id, event_log, parent)` — registers a read-only ghost session in a separate inner map. Live-state queries (input_sender, capability, mailbox, provider_status_snapshot, snapshot_outstanding_calls, take_pending_approval) unchanged — they only see the live map, which is the "session not found" contract for new asks against rehydrated IDs.
- `snapshot_for_listing` and `event_log(id)` merge both maps; live shadows rehydrated on ID collision.
- `remove(id)` evicts from both maps for symmetry.
- New `rehydrate_sessions(events_dir, sessions) -> usize` function in `serve/mod.rs` — walks `events_dir/<daemon_id>/*.jsonl`, loads via `SessionEventLog::from_jsonl`, calls `insert_rehydrated`. Best-effort: I/O errors return 0 rather than aborting startup.
- Wired into `serve_with_io_with_config` after `Sessions::new()`.

### Why scan all daemon-id subdirs

`daemon_info.rs:46` generates `daemon_id` randomly each process start. After restart the new daemon's own dir doesn't exist yet, so every visible subdir is a prior or concurrent daemon. All adopted as locally-rehydrated read-only entries. FileBackend continues to expose them as remote for `session.list --include-remote`.

### Test coverage (6 new tests, 690 total)

- Rehydrated entry appears in listing + event_log; absent from live-state queries
- remove() evicts ghost entries
- Live shadows rehydrated on ID collision (verified via Arc::ptr_eq)
- Scan registers two daemon-dir JSONLs with parent flow-through
- Missing events_dir is a best-effort no-op
- Non-JSONL files and loose files in events_root are skipped

### Follow-up beads (not yet filed)

- **Phase B**: verify `session.events`, `session.stats`, `session.transcript` queries work against rehydrated IDs (probably yes via the unified `event_log(id)`, but needs explicit test coverage)
- **Phase C**: writable rehydration — reattach disk writer, restore agent loop state, reattach provider
- **GC bead**: operator-configurable age threshold for prune-on-startup

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text:
## Phase B shipped (2026-05-18): handler-level contract tests (PR #91 MERGED)

No production code changes — phase A already made `Sessions::event_log(id)` consult both live and rehydrated maps, and `handle_session_stats` / `handle_session_events` route through that. Phase B pins the contract with explicit tests.

3 new tests in `handlers/session.rs`:
- `session_stats_works_for_rehydrated_session` — full happy path: insert_rehydrated with SessionCreated/UserMessage/Done payloads, call handler, assert response shape
- `session_events_works_for_rehydrated_session` — all events returned in the array with correct payload kinds
- `session_stats_returns_not_found_for_unknown_session` — sanity guard against accidental fall-through

## What remains in mu-u1ld scope (not in this PR)

- Phase C: writable rehydration — reattach disk writer, restore agent loop state, allow new asks against rehydrated IDs (probably its own bead given the design surface)
- GC bead: operator-configurable age threshold for prune-on-startup
- `session.delete_persistent` (or extend close_session): closing a rehydrated session currently evicts it from the registry but the .jsonl file stays — next restart, it rehydrates again

Read-only rehydration (the "massive UX win" the bead invoked) is now complete and pinned. The bead can be closed; the items above warrant separate beads.

title: Add verify-claims CI job (mirror local gate to GitHub Actions)
description:
Per 2026-05-17 handoff: verify-claims is local-only (scripts/verify-claims.sh runs via gh-wrapper, pre-pr-check, delegate.sh). Workers that bypass the local gate (raw 'gh pr create' without the wrapper) would slip past auto-merge.

Add a GitHub Actions job that runs scripts/verify-claims.sh over the PR's main..head commit range. Pattern mirrors scripts/pre-pr-check.sh:76-110 — iterate non-merge commits, skip-on-opt-out, fail-on-mismatch.

Branch protection already requires fmt/clippy/test green; once added, also require this job to keep auto-merge from landing claim-drift commits.

comments.author: tcovert
comments.text: Correction (post-close): actual landed merge commit is bee1a729 (auto-merge --rebase). Original close_reason cited a non-existent hash 5b3b6c10. Caught by bead-close-audit run later same session. Real PR head was 8946654c, real merge was bee1a72.

title: TUI color polish — moonfly/github_dark family
